programmeName: Master of Arts in Artificial Intelligence and the Future
summary: Hosted by the Hong Kong Catastrophic Risk Centre and the Department of Philosophy at Lingnan University, the Master of Arts in Artificial Intelligence and the Future (MAAIF) offers a humanities-based perspective on Artificial Intelligence (AI). Our students gain a fundamental understanding of AI and delve into the ethical and social issues arising from AI technologies through a rigorous interdisciplinary programme that combines philosophy, computer science, logic, psychology, and the arts. In our wide-ranging curriculum, students explore the ramifications of AI across a variety of domains, including science, law, economics, public policy, healthcare, and business and finance.
suitableBackgrounds: Our primary objective is to introduce students to major works in SF literature and visual arts, with a specific focus on themes related to AI and reality.

A student who has fulfilled all the requirements for graduation shall be awarded a Master of Arts in Artificial Intelligence and the Future degree with one of the following classifications: • Distinction for Minimum Cumulative G.P.A.

learningObjectives: * For 2025 intake: During the first two weeks (add/drop period), students can enrol in both AIF502 and AIF603. After this period, they will choose one as their fourth core course.
(any 4 courses, a total of 12 credits)
AIF601 Computability and Logic (3 credits)
Prerequisite(s): AIF502 Introduction to Automated Reasoning

What sorts of problems can be solved with a computer? This course provides students with a clear understanding of the possibilities and limitations of computable problems through an examination of fundamental concepts and results in proof theory, computability theory, and model theory. Specific topics of coverage may include: basic set theory including diagonalization and uncountable sets; meta-logic of first-order logic, including soundness and completeness; effective computability, undecidability, and incompleteness; the Löwenheim-Skolem and compactness theorems; philosophical issues concerning the nature of physical computers; machine learning and formal learning theory.
AIF602 AI and Risk (3 credits)
AIF604 Policy, Business, and Regulatory Issues in AI (3 credits)
Prerequisite(s): AIF501 Contemporary Issues in AI
In contrast, the Department of Philosophy and the Hong Kong Catastrophic Risk Centre are ideally equipped to meet this challenge.
In the written assignments, students will be free to address and improve their knowledge concerning their personal AI-related interests.

courseDescriptions.courseName: AIF501 Contemporary Issues in AI (3 credits)
AIF502 Introduction to Automated Reasoning (3 credits)
courseDescriptions.description: Artificial intelligence is possible because we can automate reasoning. While the use of mechanical devices to solve problems goes back to the abacus, the 20th and 21st centuries have seen the development of extremely powerful methods for automating reasoning. How can something like reasoning be done by a computer? And why do programmers use particular ways to automate it? This course provides an introduction to the automation of reasoning. It also gives students the tools from logic that they will need to understand other courses in the MAAIF. * For 2025 intake: During the first two weeks (add/drop period), students can enrol in both AIF502 and AIF603. After this period, they will choose one as their fourth core course.
courseDescriptions.descriptionZh: 人工智能之所以成为可能，是因为我们可以自动推理。虽然使用机械设备解决问题​​的历史可以追溯到算盘，但在 20 世纪和 21 世纪，我们已经看到了极其强大的自动化推理方法的发展。像推理这样的事情怎么能由计算机来完成呢？为什么程序员使用特定的方法来实现自动化？本课程介绍推理的自动化。它还为学生提供了理解 MAAIF 中其他课程所需的逻辑工具。 * 对于 2025 年入学：在前两周（加减期），学生可以报名参加 AIF502 和 AIF603。在此期间之后，他们将选择一门作为他们的第四核心课程。

courseDescriptions.courseName: AIF603 Sci-fi, AI, and Reality (3 credits)
AIF604 Policy, Business, and Regulatory Issues in AI (3 credits)
courseDescriptions.description: Prerequisite(s): AIF501 Contemporary Issues in AI In the coming decades, we will increasingly come to rely on AI in making public policy and business decisions. The same advantages that enable AI systems to beat humans in chess enable it to better predict the implications of governmental policies or beat human traders in the stock market. But while these AI technologies promise to improve our decision-making in policy and business, they also pose new and serious threats. In this course, we examine the potential risks and rewards of AI for policy and business. This will include studying current efforts to regulate AI systems, e.g. the recent EU proposed Artificial Intelligence Act, China’s 2023 Interim Measures for the Management of Generative Artificial Intelligence Services, and court cases covering the use of copyrighted material in AI training data sets.
courseDescriptions.descriptionZh: 先决条件：AIF501 人工智能的当代问题 未来几十年，我们将越来越依赖人工智能来制定公共政策和商业决策。人工智能系统在国际象棋中击败人类的优势使其能够更好地预测政府政策的影响或在股票市场上击败人类交易员。但是，尽管这些人工智能技术有望改善我们的政策和业务决策，但它们也带来了新的严重威胁。在本课程中，我们研究人工智能对政策和商业的潜在风险和回报。这将包括研究当前监管人工智能系统的努力，例如最近欧盟提出的《人工智能法案》、中国《2023 年人工智能生成服务管理暂行办法》，以及涉及在人工智能训练数据集中使用受版权保护的材料的法院案件。

courseDescriptions.courseName: AIF605 Applications of AI in Science and Medicine (3 credits)

courseDescriptions.description: This capstone project enables students to deploy conceptual frameworks and research methodologies learned throughout the MAAIF, to explore in-depth relevant topics of personal interest, and to develop skills of critical research, analysis, presentation, and academic writing. The capstone research is conducted through individual or group (max 2 persons) work and direct supervision, with consultation meetings between students and advisors arranged roughly once a month. Students will work with a supervisor whose expertise includes topics relevant to their project and who will provide guidance and academic support with developing, researching, and writing the MAAIF capstone paper. Students choosing to work in pairs will be required to write a final paper of 7000-8000 words. Those working individually will be required to write a final paper of 5000-6000 words. Footnotes and quotations from primary and secondary sources in the main text are included in the word count, but appendices, tables, image captions, and the bibliography are excluded from the final word tally. The capstone project may focus primarily on themes and concepts from within the fields of AI and its impact. For full-time students, capstone project planning and supervision normally occurs in Term 2. Part-time students may plan and consult with supervisors in the second half of Year 1 and proceed to research and writin
courseDescriptions.descriptionZh: 该顶点项目使学生能够运用在 MAAIF 中学到的概念框架和研究方法，深入探索个人感兴趣的相关主题，并培养批判性研究、分析、演示和学术写作的技能。顶点研究是通过个人或小组（最多 2 人）工作和直接监督进行的，学生和顾问之间大约每月安排一次协商会议。学生将与一位导师一起工作，该导师的专业知识包括与其项目相关的主题，并为开发、研究和撰写 MAAIF 顶点论文提供指导和学术支持。选择两人一组的学生将被要求写一篇 7000-8000 字的期末论文。那些单独工作的人将被要求写一篇5000-6000字的最终论文。正文中主要和次要来源的脚注和引文均包含在字数统计中，但附录、表格、图像标题和参考书目不包括在最终字数统计中。顶点项目可能主要关注人工智能领域的主题和概念及其影响。对于全日制学生，顶点项目规划和监督通常发生在第二学期。非全日制学生可以在第一年下半年与导师进行规划和咨询，然后继续研究和写作

Master of Arts in Artificial Intelligence and the Future

Introducing MAAIF

Hosted by the Hong Kong Catastrophic Risk Centre and the Department of Philosophy at Lingnan University, the Master of Arts in Artificial Intelligence and the Future (MAAIF) offers a humanities-based perspective on Artificial Intelligence (AI). Our students gain a fundamental understanding of AI and delve into the ethical and social issues arising from AI technologies through a rigorous interdisciplinary programme that combines philosophy, computer science, logic, psychology, and the arts. In our wide-ranging curriculum, students explore the ramifications of AI across a variety of domains, including science, law, economics, public policy, healthcare, and business and finance.



Existing AI programmes with a strong technical focus do not adequately prepare students to tackle the broader ethical and philosophical issues raised by this technology. In contrast, the Department of Philosophy and the Hong Kong Catastrophic Risk Centre are ideally equipped to meet this challenge. Classes will be held at Lingnan’s state-of-the-art M+ Campus in the heart of the West Kowloon Cultural District (subject to change owing to limited classroom availability).

Course Descriptions

Course Descriptions

Core Courses
(4 courses, a total of 12 credits)

AIF501 Contemporary Issues in AI (3 credits)

This course synthesises and elucidates material covered in the other core courses of the MA. It also introduces the areas covered in the electives. It proceeds by considering concrete contemporary scenarios involving AI. It focuses on how machine learning systems have been successfully deployed in many contexts, from gaming to triage, and on the issues – epistemic, practical, social, and ethical – that these raise in the present and near future. In the written assignments, students will be free to address and improve their knowledge concerning their personal AI-related interests. The students will also gain some ‘hands on’ experience using AI systems, such as ChatGPT and OpenArt.

AIF503 Ethics of AI (3 credits)

This course provides an introduction to the ethics of AI for students of the MAAIF. After introducing the main ethics theories, as well as ethically relevant features of AI systems, this course discusses a series of practical ethical issues that are or will be caused by the widespread use of artificial intelligence in human society. The topics discussed range from issues that are already relevant today to other that may be exacerbated by future technologies like superintelligent AI systems. The course also discusses issues of AI governance and the legal regulation of AI.

AIF504 Minds & Machines (3 credits)

We often think of artificial intelligence by analogy with human intelligence. Moreover, some of the most impressive machine learning systems, such as deep neural networks (DNNs), are modelled on the brain. But when, and to what extent, should we take such analogies seriously? Consider a chess-playing DNN, such as AlphaZero, for example. Does this generate representations of positions on chess boards, akin to mental representations? And does it apply concepts such as piece mobility and space advantage, as human chess masters do, in deciding which moves to make? This course draws on the philosophy of mind and psychology to tackle such questions.

AIF502 Introduction to Automated Reasoning (3 credits)*

Artificial intelligence is possible because we can automate reasoning. While the use of mechanical devices to solve problems goes back to the abacus, the 20th and 21st centuries have seen the development of extremely powerful methods for automating reasoning.

How can something like reasoning be done by a computer? And why do programmers use particular ways to automate it? This course provides an introduction to the automation of reasoning. It also gives students the tools from logic that they will need to understand other courses in the MAAIF.

* For 2025 intake: During the first two weeks (add/drop period), students can enrol in both AIF502 and AIF603. After this period, they will choose one as their fourth core course.

AIF603 Sci-fi, AI, and Reality (3 credits)*

This course aims to provide students with a comprehensive exploration of contemporary science-fiction stories and films, coupled with a historical and theoretical understanding of these works. Our primary objective is to introduce students to major works in SF literature and visual arts, with a specific focus on themes related to AI and reality. Throughout the course, we will examine how the effects of AI can be interpreted in both an optimistic (utopian) and a pessimistic (dystopian) way. For instance, many works of science fiction have addressed the idea that AI may eventually perform most of the work that is currently done by human beings. Some science fiction authors have written stories in which this is a positive development because it improves humans’ quality of life, while others have written stories in which this is a negative development because humans become too reliant on AI. In general, authors have explored various effects that AI might have on humans and human society through both positive and negative perspectives.

* For 2025 intake: During the first two weeks (add/drop period), students can enrol in both AIF502 and AIF603. After this period, they will choose one as their fourth core course.

Elective Courses
(any 4 courses, a total of 12 credits)

AIF601 Computability and Logic (3 credits)

Prerequisite(s): AIF502 Introduction to Automated Reasoning

What sorts of problems can be solved with a computer? This course provides students with a clear understanding of the possibilities and limitations of computable problems through an examination of fundamental concepts and results in proof theory, computability theory, and model theory. Specific topics of coverage may include: basic set theory including diagonalization and uncountable sets; meta-logic of first-order logic, including soundness and completeness; effective computability, undecidability, and incompleteness; the Löwenheim-Skolem and compactness theorems; philosophical issues concerning the nature of physical computers; machine learning and formal learning theory.

AIF602 AI and Risk (3 credits)

As artificial intelligence becomes increasingly powerful and less restricted in scope, researchers face an increasing number of increasingly weightier risks in developing it. These include risks involving the loss of control of AI systems, the intentional misuse of AI systems, conflicts between the goals of AI systems and the goals of researchers, and relying on AI systems whose inner functioning is opaque. After an introduction to risk and related concepts, this course explores the key risks arising from AI development as well as strategies for preventing and mitigating these risks. More specifically, the course will cover issues like the following. What is a risk? How can you mitigate a risk? How does risk arise with respect to developing AI systems? Could AI systems become too advanced or powerful for their programmers to control? How can researchers ensure that the goals of AI systems “align” with human values? Can we trust the outputs of AI systems whose internal functioning is opaque? Are there risks of an AI “arms race?” Should regulation or policy be introduced to help mitigate these risks? Are the risks of catastrophe great enough to warrant giving up on AI development altogether?

AIF604 Policy, Business, and Regulatory Issues in AI (3 credits)

Prerequisite(s): AIF501 Contemporary Issues in AI

In the coming decades, we will increasingly come to rely on AI in making public policy and business decisions. The same advantages that enable AI systems to beat humans in chess enable it to better predict the implications of governmental policies or beat human traders in the stock market. But while these AI technologies promise to improve our decision-making in policy and business, they also pose new and serious threats. In this course, we examine the potential risks and rewards of AI for policy and business. This will include studying current efforts to regulate AI systems, e.g. the recent EU proposed Artificial Intelligence Act, China’s 2023 Interim Measures for the Management of Generative Artificial Intelligence Services, and court cases covering the use of copyrighted material in AI training data sets.

AIF605 Applications of AI in Science and Medicine (3 credits)

Prerequisite(s): AIF501 Contemporary Issues in AI

From locating black holes in the night sky, to predicting the structure of proteins, to identifying potential causes of cancer, AI techniques are now widely applied in science and medicine. Organized around a set of detailed case studies, this course examines some of the most pressing questions raised by these applications, such as: How do AI techniques complement – or supplant – more traditional scientific or therapeutic methods? To what extent should we embrace automated scientific research? And what are the potential ethical implications of these applications for clinicians?

AIF606 Anticipating AI Futures (3 credits)

This course provides an introduction to topics in the field of speculative AI, that is, future developments of AI that are probable but not yet within the realm of the technologically possible. The course will first focus on the question of how we can try to estimate future technological and social development and then discuss a series of particular AI applications that will likely become relevant to humanity in the future; for example: superintelligence and the singularity, alternative futures, long-term AI risks, cyborgs, cyborg identity and human enhancement, post- and transhumanism, smart cities, AI and the environment, effects of AI on poverty, human freedom, dignity and social structures, and the future of work.

Capstone Project

AIF701 Capstone Project (6 credits)

This capstone project enables students to deploy conceptual frameworks and research methodologies learned throughout the MAAIF, to explore in-depth relevant topics of personal interest, and to develop skills of critical research, analysis, presentation, and academic writing. The capstone research is conducted through individual or group (max 2 persons) work and direct supervision, with consultation meetings between students and advisors arranged roughly once a month. Students will work with a supervisor whose expertise includes topics relevant to their project and who will provide guidance and academic support with developing, researching, and writing the MAAIF capstone paper. Students choosing to work in pairs will be required to write a final paper of 7000-8000 words. Those working individually will be required to write a final paper of 5000-6000 words. Footnotes and quotations from primary and secondary sources in the main text are included in the word count, but appendices, tables, image captions, and the bibliography are excluded from the final word tally. The capstone project may focus primarily on themes and concepts from within the fields of AI and its impact.
For full-time students, capstone project planning and supervision normally occurs in Term 2. Part-time students may plan and consult with supervisors in the second half of Year 1 and proceed to research and writing across Year 2.

Programme Information

The Master of Arts in Artificial Intelligence and the Future (MAAIF) programme is composed of 30 credits, including 4 required courses (12 credits) and 4 programme electives (12 credits) and a capstone project (6 credits).



Mode of Study: Full-time and Part-time (for Hong Kong Residents only).



Programme Duration: Full-time: 1 year (Maximum 4 years);

Part-time: 2 years (Maximum 5 years).



Medium of Instruction: English Only.






Course Arrangement

Core Courses (4 courses, a total of 12 credits):



1) AIF501 Contemporary Issues in AI (3 credits)



2) AIF503 Ethics of AI (3 credits)



3) AIF504 Minds & Machines (3 credits)



4) AIF502 Introduction to Automated Reasoning (3 credits) * Or

AIF603 Sci-fi, AI, and Reality (3 credits) *



* During the first two weeks (add/drop period), students can enrol in both AIF502 and AIF603. After this period, they will choose one as their fourth core course.







Elective courses (any 4 courses, a total of 12 credits):



1) AIF601 Computability and Logic (3 credits)



2) AIF602 AI and Risk (3 credits)



3) AIF604 Policy, Business and Regulatory Issues in AI (3 credits)



4) AIF605 Applications of AI in Science and Medicine (3 credits)



5) AIF606 Anticipating AI Futures (3 credits)







Capstone project (6 credits):



1) AIF701 Capstone Project (6 credits)




Graduation Requirements

To qualify for the award of the Master of Arts in Artificial Intelligence and the Future offered by Lingnan University (LU), students must, where applicable:



• Obtain 30 credits (8 courses and 1 capstone project);



• Achieve a minimum CGPA of 2.67 (equivalent to B-) or above; and



• Fulfil the University exit requirement on English language competency, if applicable; and fulfil all University/programme requirements.







A student who has fulfilled all the requirements for graduation shall be awarded a Master of Arts in Artificial Intelligence and the Future degree with one of the following classifications:



• Distinction for Minimum Cumulative G.P.A. of 3.50



• Pass for Minimum Cumulative G.P.A. of 2.67





Students will be awarded a Postgraduate Diploma in Artificial Intelligence and the Future if they complete no less than five courses with a minimum CGPA of 2.67, and do not continue in the Programme. Acceptance of an intermediate award implies an intention not to complete the final award.

Admission Requirements

Academic Qualification(s)
Applicants shall fulfill the minimum admission requirements for the Master of Arts in Artificial Intelligence and the Future programme as follows.
hold a relevant bachelor’s degree awarded by a tertiary education institution recognised for this purpose by the University; or
have obtained an equivalent qualification; or
have provided satisfactory evidence of academic and professional attainment.

English Language Requirements
In addition to the above, an applicant whose degree, or exceptionally, an alternative qualification deemed equivalent, was not awarded by a tertiary institution where English was used as the medium of instruction (MOI) for the programme shall obtain:

a minimum score of 79 in TOEFL Internet-Based Test 1 ; or
a band score of 6.0 or above in IELTS (Academic) 1 , or
a minimum score of 450 or above in College English Test Band 6 (CET-6), with proof of competency in spoken English 2 ; or
an equivalent score in a recognised test; or
an equivalent qualification to prove his/her English language proficiency, which will be considered on a case-by-case basis.

1 For IELTS and TOEFL qualifications for admission purposes, the University accepts only results from a single test sitting . The test must be conducted at a recognised test centre and fall within the two-year validity period , counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort.

2 For CET-6 results submitted for admission purposes, the University accepts only those obtained within the five-year validity period , counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort. Applicants must also demonstrate competency in spoken English through an oral interview conducted by the Programme and/or obtain grade B or above in the CET-6 Speaking English Test.

Application Documents
Applicants should scan and submit the following documents to the Online Application System for evaluation of their applications:
Identity documents (e.g. Identity card*, passport or other travel document)
Official Transcript (for academic qualifications of undergraduate and/or postgraduate studies): An official final transcript for a completed programme
The latest transcript if the programme is not yet completed
An official English-translated copy must be attached if the transcript is not in English. The interpretation of grades (in English) is also required.

Degree Certificate/Graduation Certificate/Proof of Study (as appropriate)
Verification proof by third-party authorities (for a completed programme): Online Verification Report of Higher Education Degree Certificate (中國高等教育學位在綫驗証報告), both Chinese and English versions, issued by the CHSI (中國高等教育學生信息網) for qualification(s) awarded by universities in Mainland China
Online Query of Overseas Credentials Evaluation Reports國(境)外學歷學位認証書 issued by the Chinese Service Center for Scholarly Exchange (CSCSE) (教育部留學服務中心) (applicable to Mainland applicants with qualifications awarded by overseas universities)

Proof of English language proficiency, e.g. IETLS, TOEFL Score Report (applicable to applicants whose degree or, exceptionally, an alternative qualification deemed equivalent, is not from a tertiary institution where English is used as the medium of instruction (MOI) for the degree programme)
Personal statement (Please write a personal statement of around 200-300 words in English outlining your academic achievements, choice of study and career goals.)
Reference letters (if any)
An English CV

*Do not upload copy of HKID. Local students will be required to present HKID for verification upon registration.

Programme Highlight

Quick access to The Future is Still Human | A Unique Approach at Lingnan | Structure of the Programme | A Unique Home for the MAAIF

The Future is
Still Human

Artificial Intelligence (AI) promises to revolutionise all aspects of modern life. From healthcare to education and more, AI is streamlining processes, enabling personalised solutions, and creating opportunities for growth and development. However, from its use to propagate misinformation on social media, to military applications in which targets are autonomously identified, to questions of authorship in AI-generated creative works, this technology raises complex philosophical issues which need to be addressed.



Lingnan University’s Master of Arts in Artificial Intelligence and the Future (MAAIF) will equip students with perspective necessary to shape a responsible, human-centred, and AI-enhanced future. Students will acquire a range of broadly applicable skills, allowing them to take a holistic approach to evaluating AI applications in domains as diverse as finance, law, public policy, and many more.

A Unique Approach
at Lingnan

Unlike traditional IT- and engineering-centric programmes, the MAAIF offers a unique, comprehensive approach to AI. The broad, interdisciplinary foundation provided by the programme makes it suitable for everyone, from new graduates to accomplished professionals. Students do not require a background in AI – nor a technical background or strong mathematical skills – but will be trained in the application of analytical tools that will allow them to think clearly and critically about AI systems. Students will learn the fundamentals of AI, although the main focus will be on philosophical issues that AI systems raise.



In MAAIF’s wide-ranging curriculum, students will consider the ramifications of AI in a variety of domains, including science, law, economics, public policy, healthcare, and business and finance.



Upon completion of the programme, graduates will be well-positioned for careers in different fields, including positions blending AI technology with artistic or humanistic pursuits. Examples of these positions include: AI Ethicist or Analyst, AI Developer or Art Creator, Educator, or AI Consultant or Strategist. With industries increasingly integrating ethical considerations into their AI strategies, our graduates will also find ample opportunities in tech, healthcare, finance, and government sectors, both in Hong Kong and abroad.



A number of scholarships are available for outstanding candidates.

Structure of the
MAAIF Programme

The programme, which welcomed its first cohort in September 2024, can be pursued on a one-year full-time or two-year part-time basis.



Its four core courses are: Contemporary Issues in AI; Ethics of AI; Minds & Machines, which will examine the possibility of building human or animal thought processes into machines; Introduction to Automated Reasoning*, which will look at the history of the field and the way in which algorithms are designed; or Sci-fi, AI, and Reality*.



The programme electives include: Computability and Logic; AI and Risk; Policy, Business and Regulatory Issues in AI; Applications of AI in Science and Medicine, and; Anticipating AI Futures. Students will also work on a capstone project on a topic of their choice. These projects can be used to show a prospective employer what they have to offer.



*During the first two weeks (add/drop period), students can enrol in both courses. After this period, they will choose one as their fourth core course.

A Unique Home
for the MAAIF

Existing AI programmes with a strong technical focus do not prepare students to tackle the wider ethical and philosophical issues introduced by this technology.



As one of the leading anglophone philosophy departments in Asia, the Department of Philosophy and the Hong Kong Catastrophic Risk Centre at Lingnan University are ideally equipped to meet this challenge. Our faculty members have expertise and research experience in areas as diverse as Applied Ethics, Artificial Intelligence, Normative Ethics, Value Theory, Metaphysics, Philosophical Logic, Philosophy of Mind, Philosophy of Science, and Political Philosophy.

Why Lingnan? | Learning Outcomes | Career Prospects

What will I study?

Unlike traditional IT- and engineering-oriented programmes, the MAAIF combines technical and philosophical topics, providing a humanities-based perspective on AI. Our students gain a fundamental understanding of AI and delve into the ethical and social issues arising from AI technologies through a rigorous interdisciplinary programme combining philosophy, computer science, logic, psychology, and the arts. Students acquire an applicable set of analytical tools and learn to think clearly and critically about AI systems. In our wide-ranging curriculum, students consider the ramifications of AI in a variety of domains, including science, law, economics, public policy, healthcare, and business and finance.


Who is it for?

The broad, interdisciplinary foundation provided by the MAAIF is suitable for anyone, whether you’re a new graduate looking for a future-proof career to an accomplished professional looking to maintain your competitive edge. No specific background in AI is necessary – we provide you with the training necessary to understand cutting-edge AI systems, reason critically about them, and make expert decisions in the AI-driven future.


What can I do
with MAAIF?

Designed to align with the Level-6 parameters of Hong Kong Qualification Framework, MAAIF graduates will be well-positioned for careers that blend AI technology with artistic and humanistic pursuits, such as AI Ethicist or Analyst, AI Developer or Art Creator, Educator, and AI Consultant or Strategist. With industries increasingly integrating ethical considerations into their AI strategies, our graduates will find ample opportunities in tech, healthcare, finance, and government sectors, both in Hong Kong and abroad.


Why Lingnan?

The Department of Philosophy at Lingnan University is one of the leading anglophone philosophy departments in East Asia and home to the Hong Kong Catastrophic Risk Centre. Students will enjoy unprecedented access to an international network of research and business leaders in AI. Classes will be held at Lingnan’s state-of-the-art M+ Campus in the heart of the West Kowloon Cultural District (subject to change owing to limited classroom availability).

Learning
Outcomes

Upon completion of the MAAIF, students will be able to:



• Explain basic theoretical and ethical principles underlying the growing field of AI.



• Communicate technical and ethical concepts related to AI to different audiences, including researchers, policymakers and non-experts.



• Generate strategies for creating more responsible, ethical, and human-centered AI systems and applications.



• Identify and critically assess the potential risks associated with AI development and implementation, including issues related to fairness.

Career
Prospects

Our graduates will be especially well-positioned to take up jobs that combine AI technology with artistic or humanistic pursuits. Examples of career paths include:



• Art Creator: Collaborating with teams designing AI systems for various purposes that meet ethical and societal standards.



• Educator: Teaching and training others on the principles, ethical standards, and challenges of AI and how to deploy AI technologies in responsible, sustainable, and effective ways.



• AI Consultant or Strategist: Providing advice and guidance to organizations on how to utilize AI technologies ethically for their business goals while minimizing security risks and maximizing benefits.



• Data Analyst: Interpreting complex digital data to assist businesses in decision-making, while ensuring ethical use of AI and data privacy.



• Compliance Officer: Ensuring corporate activities are in line with ethical guidelines and regulations related to AI use.



• Start-Up Founder: Launching innovative businesses that leverage AI technologies in an ethical and socially responsible manner.

---

Source: https://www.ln.edu.hk/philoso/hkcrc/maaif/about-us/programme-committees

Programme Committees

Explore More

About us

People
Programme Committees

Programme Committees

Programme and Curriculum Committee (PCC)

A PCC will be set up to monitor the Programme. It reports to the Department Board and to the PSC.

The terms of reference of the PCC are:
To take charge of the programme including programme design, and the implementation and review of the curriculum;
To liaise with teaching staff on Programme matters such as course offerings for each year;
To review admissions policy; and
To deal with all other matters related to the Programme.
To liaise with the Registry/School of Graduate Studies on all academic matters including review of academic regulations and examination matters

Membership of the Programme and Curriculum Committee：
Chairperson: Programme Director(s)

Members:
Head of the Department of Philosophy or representative
Director of the HKCRC or representative
Two staff members teaching courses in the Programme
Two student representatives
Secretary

Board of Examiners (BoE)

A BoE will be set up to oversee assessment and examination matters, and make recommendations of students for graduation.

The Terms of reference of the BoE are:
To maintain the academic standards of the Programme at a level appropriate to the type of award.
To approve examination question papers after considering the views and recommendations of External Academic Advisers*, where appropriate.
To maintain general supervision of the system of continuous assessment, where it applies.
To maintain the proper conduct of examinations.
To determine students' examination results.
To assess the progress of students and to report its decisions, including its recommendations for award classification, to the Postgraduate Studies Committee.
To consider appeals referred to it by the Registrar.

* Comments from External Academic Advisers should be used for future reference and forward planning purpose.

Membership of the Board of Examiners:
Chairperson: Programme Director(s) or his/her delegated representative

Members:
Dean concerned
Head of the Department of Philosophy or representative
Director of the HKCRC or representative
All staff involved with the setting and marking of any part of the examinations

Secretary: To be appointed by the Chairperson

Advisory Board

An Advisory Board will be set up to give advice on Programme matters.

Terms of Reference of the Advisory Board:
The Advisory Board will help plan and keep under review the following aspects of work, including future developments:

The level and length of the Programme and its courses in relation to local needs;

The relevance of the Programme and its courses in relation to local needs;

The prospects of local employment for graduates;

The adequacy of the equipment and other resources of the Programme for the fulfilment of local needs;

The development of teaching and other activities carried out jointly by the Programme and the appropriate sector of the community;

Investigation consultancy and other services given by the Programme;

The assistance which the community can give to further the objectives of the University in the subject concerned by way of practical training facilities, the provision of part-time teaching staff and equipment, the award of scholarships, student-fellowships, etc.

Composition of the Advisory Board:
Chairperson:
Simon David Goldstein,
Associate Professor, Department of Philosophy, HKU

Convener:
Andrea Sauchelli,
Professor and Head of Department (Philosophy), Associate Dean (Faculty of Arts), Director (Hong Kong Catastrophic Risk Centre), Programme Director (MA in Artificial Intelligence and the Future), LU

Members:
Min Kyoung Song,
Director, UBS

Edward Tsoi Ming Hin
Co-Founder, AI Safety Asia

Secretary: To be appointed by the Convener

Observers/advisers: The Chairperson may invite any persons to attend meetings as observers or advisers.

Staff-Student Consultation Committee (SSCC)

The terms of reference of the SSCC are:
To promote understanding between students and teaching staff
To consider feedback from students regarding teaching, learning, course evaluation, and issues of importance for enhancing teaching and learning quality
To review and monitor the learning and teaching quality assurance mechanisms and processes within the MAAIF Programme.
To formulate strategies in helping students to learn more effectively
To consider any other matters of concern to students

Master of Arts in Chinese

Global Chinese Studies Concentration
Admission
Student Portal
Contact

Overview

Aims
The programme aims to equip students with a deeper knowledge of Chinese literature and culture with an emphasis on creative writing. It also provides the students a solid training on Chinese literary research. The programme introduces students to a wide range of theoretical, literary, filmic and cultural texts of Chinese literature. It enhances professional development for those engaging in education, media and publishing industries.

Programme Intended Learning Outcomes
Appraise modern Chinese literature under different historical context;
Apply writing skills in creative writings;
Reflect critically on a broader view of Chinese literature under the global context and comparative perspective with other bodies of knowledge and forms of arts;
Apply requisite knowledge and research methodology to conduct independent research in Chinese literature; and
Pursue further postgraduate studies.

Features
Courses mainly taught by our professors , with renowned local and overseas scholars and writers as guest speakers
Focus on training in research and writing
Literary research from multiple perspectives, new insights and interdisciplinarity

Teaching Mode
Normally, courses would be delivered in Lecture-Tutorial mode. For creative writing courses, tutorials would be replaced by workshop.

Duration of Study
Full-time: Each academic year is divided into 3 terms, including first term (September - December), second term (January - May), and summer term (June - August). The duration of study would be 2-5 terms normally.

Time & Venue
Ful-time: Except for CHI502 Directed Thesis, all subjects would take 3 hours during weekdays and Saturdays on the Main Campus, mainly.

Programme Structure & Course Description
Required Courses (3 courses, a total of 12 credits)
Elective Courses (3 credits each, select 6 courses)

CHI501 Methodology of Literary Studies and Literary Theory (3 credits)

CHI502 Directed Thesis (6 credits) (Prerequisite: Completion of CHI501)

CHI517 Seminar on Chinese Literature and Culture (3 credits)

CHI503 Practical Criticism

CHI504 Special Topics in Classical Chinese Literature

CHI505 Special Topics in Modern Literature in Chinese

Students may take CHI504 & CHI505 twice to earn a maximum of 6 credits for each
course, provided that distinct topics are covered in the two course offers.

CHI506 Creative Writing

CHI507 Chinese Literature in Comparative Perspective

CHI508 Literature and Cinema

CHI509 Chinese Stylistics

CHI510 Modern Fiction in Chinese

CHI511 Cultural Criticism

CHI512 Studies in Chinese Classics

CHI513 Classical Chinese Dramatic Literature

CHI514 Special Topics in Narrative Writing

CHI515 Special Topics in Hong Kong Literature

CHI516 Special Topics in Chinese Linguistics

CHI518 Selected Readings in World Literature

CHI519 Special Topics in Chinese Writing

Students may take CHI519 twice to earn a maximum of 6 credits for each course,
provided that distinct topics are covered in the two course offers.

CHI520 Modern and Contemporary Taiwan Literature

CHI521 Literary Exhibition and Writing

Progression Requirement (For 2025-26 Entry and afterwards)
An academic review will be conducted for students who have completed 6 or more credits at the end of each semester. Students with cumulative GPA less than 2.50 will only be allowed to continue their studies on a probationary basis. Besides, they will receive a warning letter if their cumulative GPA is above 2.50 but below 2.67, and their cumulative GPA must be raised to 2.67 or above after receiving the warning letter in order to graduate successfully.

In the following semester, if a probationary student fails to raise their cumulative GPA to 2.50 or above under the review, they will be required to withdraw from the programme. Additionally, they will receive a warning letter if their cumulative GPA is raised to above 2.50 but below 2.67, and their cumulative GPA must be raised to 2.67 or above after receiving the warning letter in order to graduate successfully.

As each student is permitted to be on probation only once throughout their entire study, if a student has previously been on probation and their cumulative GPA falls below 2.50 again, they will be required to withdraw from the program.

Grading System
Most of the courses would be accessed by continuous assessment (40%, including tutorial performance and written reports) and final paper (60%).

Graduation Requirement
Students are required to take 30 or more credits within the duration of study and achieve a cumulative GPA of 2.67 or above in order to graduate. The maximum study period for full-time students is 2 years (including any periods of leave).

Degree Classification
Students who meet the graduation requirement would be awarded Master of Arts in Chinese Literature degree and classified into the following categories based on the cumulative GPA:

Home

Department of Chinese

Master of Arts in Chinese

Admission

Admission

News
Application for Full-time Taught Postgraduate MA in Chinese Programme and MA in Chinese (Global Chinese Studies) Programme in Academic Year 2026-27 start from 01/11/2025, till 15/05/2026.
The Programme is supported by Hong Kong Future Talents Scholarship Scheme for Advanced Studies (FTSS) in academic year 2025-26. Under the FTSS, awardees are required to pay HK$50,000 tuition fee and the differences will be subsidised by the scholarship subject to a cap of HK$100,000 for each student for the whole TPg programme, regardless of the actual study period of the programme and mode of study.
The Programme was supported by Targeted Taught Postgraduate Programmes Fellowships Scheme. Under the TPgFS, awardees are required to pay a minimum tuition fee of HK$42,100, which is the prevailing rate of the UGC-funded programme, and the remaining balance will be subsidised by the fellowship subject to a cap of HK$120,000 for each student for the whole programme, regardless of the mode of study and actual study period.
Application of Part-time Taught Postgraduate MA Programme has no longer been accepted since AY 2018-19.

Entrance Requirements
Applicants for admission to the programme should:
hold a relevant bachelor’s degree awarded by a tertiary education institution recognised for this purpose by the University; or
have obtained an equivalent qualification; and
meet the requirement of English language proficiency; and
be interviewed by a panel consisting, of the Programme Director, the Associate Programme Director and other teachers involved in the programme

English Language Proficiency Requirement
Applicants whose bachelor's degree or, exceptionally, an alternative qualification deemed equivalent from a tertiary institution is not using English as the medium of instruction (MOI) for the programme should obtain:
a minimum score of 60 (internet-based test) in the Test of English as a Foreign Language (TOEFL)¹; or
a band score of 6.0 or above in the International English Language Testing System (IELTS)¹; or
a minimum score of 450 or above in the College English Test Band 6 (CET-6), with proof of competency in spoken English 2 .

¹ For IELTS and TOEFL qualifications for admission purposes, the University accepts only results from a single test sitting . The test must be conducted at a recognised test centre and fall within the two-year validity period , counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort.
2 For CET-6 results submitted for admission purposes, the University accepts only those obtained within the five-year validity period , counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort. Applicants must also demonstrate competency in spoken English through an oral interview conducted by the Programme and/or obtain grade B or above in the CET-6 Speaking English Test.

Application Period
The programme will be open for application from November of this year to mid May of next year, with the academic year commence in next year's September.
Application period of Full-time MA in Chinese Programme 2026-27 intake: 1st November 2025 to 15th May 2026

Application
Online Application System
Guidelines for Online Application System (Please read before starting the application)
Confidential Recommendation Form

Fee
Application fee: HKD400 (Non-refundable)

Student Visa application fee (applicable to non-local students): HKD700

Full-time programme fee: HKD16,000 for every 3 credits, total HKD160,000 for taking 30 credits as programme requirement. Total tuition fee is to be paid in two installments, with the first due before commencement of studies and the second due before the end date of first term.

Graduation fee: Full-time student should pay HKD600 by the start of first term.

* Full-time Chinese or English teach can apply for Professional Development Incentive Grant Scheme for Language Teachers from Standing Committee on Language Education and Research.

Reminders for Non-Local Applicatants
Applicants from Mainland China, Macau, and Taiwan who are provisionally admitted to this program must apply for an entry permit or student visa through the department to the Hong Kong Immigration Department. Mainland Chinese applicants are also required to apply for a travel permit and the relevant endorsement to Hong Kong at the Exit-Entry Administration Office in their place of residence. Formal admission will only be granted upon completion of these procedures.
According to the current immigration policy, Mainland students who have completed a recognized full-time bachelor's degree or higher program at a UGC-funded institution in or after 1990 may apply to work in Hong Kong. However, this arrangement does not apply to Mainland students enrolled in part-time programs.

programmeName: Master of Arts in Creative and Media Industries
summary: The MACMI programme is composed of 30 credits, including 5 required courses (15 credits) and 5 programme electives (15 credits) or 3 programme electives and a final project (6 credits).
suitableBackgrounds: Students will explore key components and major milestones of the relevant sectors as they developed into a crucial part of Hong Kong’s cultural economy and signposts of the city’s cultural landscape.
Some examples of questions to be addressed in the more art-oriented component of the course: To what degree is taste/aesthetic judgment a perceptual (visual) phenomenon?
Through this course, students will also have the chance to participate in major industrial fairs in Hong Kong, gaining valuable insight into the latest trends and developments in the industry.

It will focus on key themes and debates within exhibition-making such as the role of curator, boundary-pushing curatorial concepts, curating in and outside the museum and working with communities and other mediums and disciplines.
Applicants must also demonstrate competency in spoken English through an oral interview conducted by the Programme and/or obtain grade B or above in the CET-6 Speaking English Test.

Employment Prospects Graduates of the Programme will be capable of taking up jobs in creative and media industries sector, including government organisations, organisations in film, media and arts, arts and cultural trust foundations, media and publishing companies.
learningObjectives: Source: https://www.ln.edu.hk/daci/macmi/admission-requirements
Department of Digital Arts and Creative Industries
Master of Arts in Creative and Media Industries Programme (MACMI)
Admission Requirements
For admission to the programme, an applicant shall

Department of Digital Arts and Creative Industries

Master of Arts in Creative and Media Industries Programme (MACMI)

Course Details

Course Details

Curriculum
The MACMI programme is composed of 30 credits, including 5 required courses (15 credits) and 5 programme electives (15 credits) or 3 programme electives and a final project (6 credits).

Required Courses
Term 1
Term 2

VSP501 Creative and Media Industries: Concepts and Histories (3 credits)
VSP504 Creative and Media Industries in Hong Kong (3 credits)

VSP502 Media Industries Research: Production, Practice, Policy (3 credits)
VSP514 Creative and Media Industries Proseminar: Voices from the Field (3 credits)

VSP503 Media Ethics (3 credits)

Programme Electives (Any 4 Courses)
VSP505 Art and Technology (3 credits)

VSP506 Architectural Heritage and Creative Industries (3 credits)

VSP508 Mind, Creativity, and Imagination (3 credits)

VSP509 Film Conservation and Digital Humanities (3 credits)

VSP510 Creative Industries and Cultural Policies in East Asia (3 credits)

VSP511 Special Topics in Media and Creative Industries (3 credits)

VSP512 Capstone Project (6 credits)

VSP513 A.I. in Media and Creative Industries (3 credits)

VIS503 Approaches to Curating (3 credits)

VIS507 Digital Technologies and Museum Experience (3 credits)

Medium of Instruction
English is the medium of instruction for most courses including all core courses. Some electives may be offered in Cantonese or Putonghua.

Study Modes
1-year Full-time
2-year Part-time

Graduation Requirements
To qualify for the award of the Master of Arts in Creative and Media Industries offered by Lingnan University (LU), students must, where applicable:

obtain 30 credits;
attain a cumulative G.P.A. of 2.67 (equivalent to B-) or above; and
fulfil the University exit requirement on English language competency, if applicable; and fulfil all University/programme requirements.

VSP501 Creative and Media Industries: Concepts and Histories (3 credits)

This course will ground students in the conceptual foundations and historical claims made by the field of creative and media industries. And it will compare the features of a series of influential global/regional creative and media industries. Students will learn to skillfully reflect on the concepts and methods scholars have developed to analyse these industries and will build a research project addressing a creative industries topic of their own choosing.

VSP502 Media Industries Research: Production, Practice, Policy (3 credits)

The course aims to discover what media industries are, and how to approach them analytically. Matching research questions with businesses, practices, products and difficulties often found in film, television, and other media industries. Looking for revealing camouflage; learning how to approach media industries in the field, reverse engineering provisional theses from traits visible in media products.

VSP503 Media Ethics (3 credits)

This course will introduce students to some of the basic issues they can expect to face if they pursue a career in media industries, and will also delve deeper into select issues in order to encourage ethical reflection. The course will guide students to (1) discuss the basic legal and ethical issues facing the media in Hong Kong; (2) to reflect on current ethical questions relevant to a career in the media; (3) to discuss their views on ethical issues; and (4) to think creatively about solving ethical problems.

VSP504 Creative and Media Industries in Hong Kong (3 credits)

The course aims to install a deeper knowledge of Hong Kong as a robust creative and media industries hub in relation to cultural economy, creative talents and new business models. Students will explore key components and major milestones of the relevant sectors as they developed into a crucial part of Hong Kong’s cultural economy and signposts of the city’s cultural landscape.

VSP505 Art and Technology (3 credits)

This course will explore the relationship of art and technology through the study of art history from modern art to mixed-media art, net art, digital art, computer animation, CGI, interactive installation, robotic art, biotechnology and so forth. Different artists and their applications of different technologies will be studied to explore their relationship to transforming culture and society. Students will gain broader understanding and critical awareness of different concepts and developments of art and technology from early experiments by futurists and constructivists to most recent practices like interactive games and expanded reality experiments with wearable and portable media. The course will acquaint students with the creative ideas of art solutions related to technology and the overall impact of technology on the arts. It encourages students to analyze and criticize different forms and practices of electronic/media arts from conceptual design to practical applications.

VSP506 Architectural Heritage and Creative Industries (3 credits)

The course aims to provide students with fundamental theories and analytical tools for investigating the role of architectural heritage in creative industries and its potential contribution to the quality of life and economy of the corresponding areas. It also aims to acquaint students with the visual/architectural skills for conducting on-site case studies and composing architectural proposal for creative reuse.

VSP507 Aesthetics and Philosophy of Contemporary Art (3 credits)

This course discusses some of the central issues that have been debated in contemporary mainstream aesthetics and philosophy of art. The topics covered in the course include: (a) the notion of aesthetic value and its connections with other values (for example, moral or artistic), (b) the nature and structure of aesthetic concepts and the way they are deployed in aesthetic understanding, (c) the mind-dependence (or mind-independence) of aesthetic properties such as beauty, ugliness, gaudiness, elegance and so on, (d) the definition of art, (e) specific philosophical issues related to contemporary forms of art, such as installations and digital art.

VSP508 Mind, Creativity, and Imagination (3 credits)

This course offers an introduction to the notions of imagination and creativity from the point of view of contemporary philosophy of mind. Among the questions investigated in this course: how is the imagination like and different from belief? How does imagination connect with action? How does imagination function in our engagement with fictions? In this course, we will also consider creativity as it relates to imagination: is imagination necessary or essential to creative thought? Finally, we consider some theories of creativity in the arts.

VSP509 Film Conservation and Digital Humanities (3 credits)

The course will introduce theories and histories of film conservation and digital humanities. It will show students how to utilise digital technology as a tool for visualisation of knowledge, and processing and analysing digital repositories. In addition, practical and ethical issues derived from digital conservation projects will be highlighted to help students in conducting their case studies.

VSP510 Creative Industries and Cultural Policies in East Asia (3 credits)

The course aims to equip the students with the essential ideas about the key issues and agents involving Creative Industries (CIs) and cultural policy. In helping the students become familiar with the particular histories and practices of CIs and cultural policies in East Asia, the course aims to sharpen their critical thinking about the functioning and effectiveness of such practices in these countries. It aims at helping the students better understand the operations and forms of decision-making involving the media landscape across East Asia and beyond.

VSP511 Special Topics in Media and Creative Industries (3 credits)

This is a course partly on visual attention – from the point of view of contemporary philosophy of mind – and partly on art. Some examples of questions to be addressed in the more mind-oriented component of the course: What is the content of visual experience? Can vision be top-down affected/penetrated by expectations/knowledge/ values? What is attention? Some examples of questions to be addressed in the more art-oriented component of the course: To what degree is taste/aesthetic judgment a perceptual (visual) phenomenon? What space is there for objectivity in aesthetic judgment? Do different perceivers (say an expert painter vs. a complete novice) *see* artworks differently?

VSP512 Capstone Project (6 credits)

This course empowers students to apply theories and methodologies learned across the MACMI curriculum to analyse – in depth – a creative and media industries topics of their own choosing. With the supervision of a faculty member with relevant expertise, students will engage in self-directed research and interrogate relevant scholarly perspectives on the object of their study. The capstone project will offer students a valuable opportunity to connect their MACMI scholarship with their future careers.

VSP513 A.I. in Media and Creative Industries (3 credits)

The course will offer students introduction to the philosophical, ethical, and technical notions required to understand the current debates surrounding AI and its impact in various areas of the creative and media industries. Influential theories and arguments will also be highlighted throughout the course.

VSP514 Creative and Media Industries Proseminar: Voices from the Field (3 credits)

This course aims to provide students with the opportunity to engage directly with prominent practitioners, artists, and professionals who work in the creative and media industries. Through this course, students will also have the chance to participate in major industrial fairs in Hong Kong, gaining valuable insight into the latest trends and developments in the industry. To achieve this, the course instructor invites experienced media producers and senior managers in media institutes as guest speakers to share their industry experiences and offer practical guidance for students’ career development. Following the guest lectures, students will participate in one‐hour tutorial sessions to discuss their takeaways and relate them to the theories they learn from the MACMI core courses. Additionally, field trips to events such as Art Basel and the Hong Kong International Film Festival will be arranged to enrich students’ first‐hand experiences of Hong Kong’s media and creative industries showcases.

VIS503 Approaches to Curating (3 credits)

Curating is a contested field of practice, mediating the production, circulation and consumption of art and culture, as well as offering a reflection on the social and cultural phenomenon. To enable students’ understanding of curatorial approaches, this course will introduce the histories of curating and curatorial discourses and practices in both Western and Asian contexts. It will focus on key themes and debates within exhibition-making such as the role of curator, boundary-pushing curatorial concepts, curating in and outside the museum and working with communities and other mediums and disciplines. Besides, it provides an experimental platform for students to interpret artworks and design.

VIS507 Digital Technologies and Museum Experience (3 credits)

This course explores the role of digital technologies in curatorial practice. It aims to provide competent knowledge of various forms of digital media employed by curators in exhibition design and museum management. Digital media provides specific potentials in interactive, participatory, physical or virtual presentations. The course will start with an overview of such development and application in a museum setting. With case studies to different digital tools, students will be familiar with their advantages and limitations, especially to their potentials in narrative, communicative and educational purposes. Students will be guided to identify the media specific properties and based on these, they will plan for an effective strategy to match curatorial objectives.

Home

Department of Digital Arts and Creative Industries

Master of Arts in Creative and Media Industries Programme (MACMI)

Admission Requirements

Admission Requirements

For admission to the programme, an applicant shall
hold a relevant bachelor’s degree awarded by a tertiary education institution recognised for this purpose by the University; or
have obtained an equivalent qualification; or
have provided satisfactory evidence of academic and professional attainment.

In addition to the above, an applicant whose degree, or exceptionally, an alternative qualification deemed equivalent, was not awarded by a tertiary institution where English was used as the medium of instruction (MOI) for the programme shall obtain:
a minimum score of 79 in TOEFL Internet-Based Test 1 ; or
a band score of 6.5 or above in IELTS 1 ; or
a minimum score of 475 or above in College English Test Band 6 (CET-6), with proof of competency in spoken English 2 , which will be considered on a case-by-case basis;
an equivalent score in a recognised test; or
an equivalent qualification to prove his/her English language proficiency, which will be considered on a case-by-case basis.

1 For IELTS and TOEFL qualifications for admission purposes, the University accepts only results from a single test sitting . The test must be conducted at a recognised test centre and fall within the two-year validity period , counted from the time of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort.

2 For CET-6 results submitted for admission purposes, the University accepts only those obtained within the five-year validity period , counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort. Applicants must also demonstrate competency in spoken English through an oral interview conducted by the Programme and/or obtain grade B or above in the CET-6 Speaking English Test.

Department of Digital Arts and Creative Industries

Master of Arts in Creative and Media Industries Programme (MACMI)

Overview

Overview

Hosted by the Department of Digital Arts and Creative Industries (DACI) and the Department of Philosophy with the support of the Centre for Film and Creative Industries (CFCI) of Lingnan University, the Master of Arts in Creative and Media Industries (MACMI) provides both basic and advanced courses on creative and media industries, i.e. connected fields in film, television, music, performing arts, architecture, visual arts, and interactive media, as well as archive, museum and amusement services – which collectively constitute the most dynamic sector of cultural economy in Hong Kong.

The programme is structured on three grounds:

The concept of creativity and the evolution of creative and media industries;
Recent developments enabled by globalisation and digital technology;
Hong Kong as a hub for creative and media industries in the region.

Employment Prospects
Graduates of the Programme will be capable of taking up jobs in creative and media industries sector, including government organisations, organisations in film, media and arts, arts and cultural trust foundations, media and publishing companies. They are expected to find employment in more than a dozen creative and media industries sectors locally and regionally, including a number of signature trade fairs and events (e.g. Filmart, Art Basel, Ani-Com and Games Hong Kong, Hong Kong International Film Festival (HKIFF), Hong Kong Book Fair, Macau International Film Festival, Pusan International Film Festival (PIFF), Tokyo International Film Festival (TIFF), performing arts venues (e.g. Xiqu Centre, Cultural Centre, City Hall, Guangzhou Opera House), archives (HKFA), NGOs, museums and art galleries in Hong Kong and the Greater Bay Area (e.g. M+, National Palace Museum, Shenzhen Art Museum).

Programme Objectives
To provide an interdisciplinary understanding for students to explore the dynamics and processes of creative and media industries;
To foster development of the emergent field of creative and media industries studies, new to Hong Kong and the region;
To respond to the needs of Hong Kong society and economy.

Learning Outcomes
At the end of this programme students will be able to
Synthesise a variety of theories, narratives and practices of creative and media industries as a new knowledge economy and a new field of research and study;
Explain the concepts of creativity and its operations in creative and media industries locally, and in media capitals further afield;
Develop fluency in various methodologies and apply them in case studies;
Construct and evaluate selected case studies in Hong Kong and regional creative enterprises;
Become a practitioner in creative and media industries, such as programmers, event organisers, producers, managers, publicists and researchers, or a scholar by enrolling into more advanced programmes.

Home

Department of Digital Arts and Creative Industries

Master of Arts in Creative and Media Industries Programme (MACMI)

Programme Committees

Programme Committees

Programme and Curriculum Committee (PCC)

The terms of reference of the PCC are:
To take charge of the programme including programme design, and the implementation and review of the curriculum;
To review and carry out the admissions policy for the MACMI Programme;
To liaise with the Registry/School of Graduate Studies on all academic matters, including review of academic regulations and confirmation of course assessment results;
To ensure the intended student learning outcomes will be achieved;
To report to the Board of the Department of Digital Arts and Creative Industries and the Board of the Department of Philosophy on the progress, review, and development of the MACMI Programme;
To handle all other matters related to the MACMI Programme;

Board of Examiners (BoE)

The terms of reference of the BoE are:
To maintain the academic standards of the programme;
To maintain general supervision of the system of continuous assessment;
To assess the performance of students in the programme; To maintain the proper conduct of examinations;
To approve the final examination results;
To make recommendations of students for graduation/special consideration for continuation/discontinuation;
To deal with grievances and appeals from students; and
To report to the Postgraduate Studies Committee of the Senate.

Advisory Board

The terms of reference of the Advisory Board are:
The level and length of the programme and its courses in relation to local needs;
The relevance of the programme and its courses in relation to local needs;
The adequacy of the equipment and other resources of the Department for the fulfilment of local needs;
The development of teaching and other activities carried out jointly by the Department and the appropriate sector of the community;
Investigation consultancy and other services given by the Department;
The assistance which the community can give to further the objectives of the University in the subject concerned by way of practical training facilities, the provision of part-time teaching staff and equipment, the award of scholarships, student-fellowships, etc.

Staff-Student Consultation Committee (SSCC)

The terms of reference of the SSCC are:
To promote understanding between students and teaching staff
To consider feedback from students regarding teaching, learning, course evaluation, and issues of importance for enhancing teaching and learning quality
To review and monitor the learning and teaching quality assurance mechanisms and processes within the MACMI Programme.
To formulate strategies in helping students to learn more effectively
To consider any other matters of concern to students

summary: Administrated by the Department of Digital Arts and Creative Industries (DACI) at Lingnan University, Master of Arts in Curating and Art History (MACAH) combines two disciplines into one: museum curating and art history, with options to engage in a practicum programme. Aside from required and elective courses, students can choose to curate a group exhibition or complete a research dissertation as capstone experience. An internship programme and field trips are arranged to enhance students’ international exposure and their interaction with art historians, collectors and museum professionals. Students can also benefit from the insights and experiences of eminent scholars and experienced curators who are involved in our events at DACI.

admissionNotes: Applicants must have fulfilled the following requirements for admission to the MACAH programme: Applicants should hold a relevant degree or equivalent standard awarded by a tertiary education institution recognised for this purpose by Lingnan University; or have obtained an equivalent qualification; For those applicants who do not hold a relevant degree, they might be admitted on an exceptional and case-by-case basis. They must provide satisfactory evidence of academic/professional qualification deemed equivalent to a bachelor’s degree or having outstanding achievements or high distinction in the respective profession. Applicants should demonstrate proficiency in English; and An applicant whose degree or exceptionally an alternative qualification deemed equivalent is not from a tertiary institution in Hong Kong or an English-speaking country should obtain a 79 in TOEFL Internet-Based Test* or a band score of 6.5 or above in in IELTS*, or a minimum score of 475 or above in College English Test Band 6 (CET-6), with proof of competency in spoken English** ,or an equivalent score in a recognised test, or an equivalent qualification to prove his/her language proficiency which will be considered on a case-by-case basis. * For IELTS and TOEFL qualifications for admission purposes, the University accepts only results from a single test sitting. The test must be conducted at a recognised test centre and fall within the two-year validity period, counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort. **For CET-6 results submitted for admission purposes, the University accepts only those obtained within the five-year validity period, counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort. Applicants must also demonstrate competency in spoken English through an oral interview conducted by the Programme and/or obtain grade B or above in the CET-6 Speaking English Test. Each candidate must submit a personal statement between 500 and 1200 words describing their career objectives and reason for applying for the programme, as well as any relevant experiences and skills they have in relation to curating and art history. It is advised to include additional supporting materials, such as samples of English writing, internship credentials, and portfolios (if any). APPLICATION AND TUITION FEE Application Period for 2026-27 intake: 1 NOVEMBER 2025 to 30 JUNE 2026.

About MACAH

Administrated by the Department of Digital Arts and Creative Industries (DACI) at Lingnan University, Master of Arts in Curating and Art History (MACAH) combines two disciplines into one: museum curating and art history, with options to engage in a practicum programme. Aside from required and elective courses, students can choose to curate a group exhibition or complete a research dissertation as capstone experience. An internship programme and field trips are arranged to enhance students’ international exposure and their interaction with art historians, collectors and museum professionals. Students can also benefit from the insights and experiences of eminent scholars and experienced curators who are involved in our events at DACI.

MACAH is suitable for those who wish to pursue interdisciplinary study in art history and professional careers in museums, curatorial and other art-related industries. Through studying art theories and collections, curatorial practice, art projects, entrepreneurial initiatives, and professional training, MACAH offers students multiple perspectives to explore and engage in the development of art and cultural landscapes in Hong Kong, Asia and beyond.

Graduates can seek employment in the cultural and arts industries, which include private and public museums, galleries, auction houses, arts media, educational institutions, arts and cultural trust foundations, and art fairs. Alumni from MACAH have worked in galleries, museums, auction houses, and a variety of other industries.

Programme Curriculum

The MACAH programme entails 30 credits of work, including 4 required courses, 4-6 programme electives. The teaching pattern is normally a 3-hour seminar per week (except Capstone Project and Internship) for each course. The structure of the curriculum is as follows:

Remarks: Students who have no proven undergraduate background in art history will be required to take one elective course on art history.

Graduation Requirements

To qualify for the award of the Master of Arts in Curating and Art History offered by Lingnan University (LU), students must, where applicable:

obtain 30 credits;
attain a cumulative G.P.A. of 2.67 (equivalent to B-) or above;
and fulfil the University exit requirement on English language competency, if applicable;
and fulfil all University/programme requirements.

Targeted Taught Postgraduate Programmes Fellowships Scheme

MACAH has been included by the University Grants Committee in its Targeted Taught Postgraduate Programme Fellowship Scheme during 2022-2024. Eligible Local students will be invited by the Programme Office to submit their applications. Each awardee will receive a scholarship to a cap of HK$120,000.

Recipients of the Fellowships Scheme

Chu Kai Fung

2024-2025 awardee



With a fervent passion for arts and culture, Chris has served in a wide range of local art institutions and museums following his graduation. His career has been composed of work experience in arts administration, exhibition and programme planning, and museum education. As a prospective student pursuing a Master’s degree in Curating and Art History, he endeavors to enhance his professional knowledge in art history and remain abreast of the latest developments in museums worldwide. Through curating exhibitions and activities, he aspires to contribute to the museum industry in Hong Kong by enabling audiences to appreciate art in refreshing ways.

Admission and Application

ADMISSION REQUIREMENT

Applicants must have fulfilled the following requirements for admission to the MACAH programme:

Applicants should hold a relevant degree or equivalent standard awarded by a tertiary education institution recognised for this purpose by Lingnan University; or have obtained an equivalent qualification;
For those applicants who do not hold a relevant degree, they might be admitted on an exceptional and case-by-case basis. They must provide satisfactory evidence of academic/professional qualification deemed equivalent to a bachelor’s degree or having outstanding achievements or high distinction in the respective profession.
Applicants should demonstrate proficiency in English; and
An applicant whose degree or exceptionally an alternative qualification deemed equivalent is not from a tertiary institution in Hong Kong or an English-speaking country should obtain a 79 in TOEFL Internet-Based Test* or a band score of 6.5 or above in in IELTS*, or a minimum score of 475 or above in College English Test Band 6 (CET-6), with proof of competency in spoken English** ,or an equivalent score in a recognised test, or an equivalent qualification to prove his/her language proficiency which will be considered on a case-by-case basis.
* For IELTS and TOEFL qualifications for admission purposes, the University accepts only results from a single test sitting. The test must be conducted at a recognised test centre and fall within the two-year validity period, counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort.
**For CET-6 results submitted for admission purposes, the University accepts only those obtained within the five-year validity period, counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort. Applicants must also demonstrate competency in spoken English through an oral interview conducted by the Programme and/or obtain grade B or above in the CET-6 Speaking English Test.
Each candidate must submit a personal statement between 500 and 1200 words describing their career objectives and reason for applying for the programme, as well as any relevant experiences and skills they have in relation to curating and art history. It is advised to include additional supporting materials, such as samples of English writing, internship credentials, and portfolios (if any).

APPLICATION AND TUITION FEE

Application Period for 2026-27 intake: 1 NOVEMBER 2025 to 30 JUNE 2026.

Tuition Fee

1-year Full-time:
For 2026-2027intake, the annual tuition fee for full-time study is HK$160,000 .

2-year Part-time:
For 2026-2027 intake, the annual tuition fee for part-time study is HK$80,000 .

Note:
Additional charges apply to optional co-curricular activities such as internships and the field trips taking place outside Hong Kong.

Scholarship

All applicants admitted to MACAH will automatically be qualified for the entrance and exit scholarship, which will be offered to outstanding admittees.

Application Method

Applications must be submitted online with settling an application fee of HK$400 via Lingnan University's Online Application System .

Note:
All documents submitted will not be returned. Information on all unsuccessful applicants and applicants who have declined the offer will be destroyed after the admission exercise. All application fees paid are non-refundable and no receipt for the application fee will be issued.
