Hong Kong Future Talents Scholarship Scheme for Advanced Studies

MACAH has been included by the University Grants Committee in its Hong Kong Future Talents Scholarship Scheme for Advanced Studies during 2025-2027. Eligible Local students will be invited by the Programme Office to submit their applications. Each awardee will receive a scholarship to a cap of HK$100,000.

Recipients of the Hong Kong Future Talents Scholarship Scheme

Cheung Yiu Hin Tommy

With a Bachelor’s degree in International Business and Global Management, Tommy initially pursued what he believed to be a “good life”, and progressed to working in an international bank. However, after a decade, he began questioning the deeper meaning of his work and sought to align his knowledge and skills with his personal aspirations.



In 2023, he co-founded an experimental platform to explore art’s role in addressing societal challenges and inspiring new perspectives. He believes art and curating have a vital role to play in navigating Hong Kong through the global turmoil- to act as a civic muscle in fostering dialogues, building community cohesion, and celebrating the city’s unique cultural identity.



By pursuing MACAH, he aims to equip himself with tools to contribute meaningfully to Hong Kong’s cultural landscape.


Leung Wing Tung Candice

I am excited to pursue my master’s degree in Curating and Art History this year, following my graduation from City University majoring in Asian and International Studies. My journey into the world of art began in museums, where I discovered the transformative power of visual storytelling. Passionate about exploring diverse cultures through art and preserving cultural heritage, I see this scholarship as a significant opportunity to create engaging exhibitions that foster community connections.

Tang Pui Ching

As a Marketing Executive at Eaton HK, Pui Ching develops and implements creative campaigns that reflect the hotel’s dedication to culture, community, and social impact.



Passionate about curating and promoting art with a purpose, she focuses on raising awareness of issues such as LGBTQ+ inclusion and climate change.



By integrating curatorial practice with innovative marketing, Pui Ching strives to connect communities and inspire positive change through the arts.

Scholarships

All applicants admitted to MACAH will automatically be qualified for the Entrance and Exit scholarship, which will be offered to outstanding admittees and students.

Entrance Scholarship (2026-27)

Outstanding admitted students will be awarded a scholarship of up to HK$35,000 .

Application Requirements:
English Proficiency: An IELTS score of 6.5 or above, or graduation from a higher education institution in Hong Kong or an English-speaking country where English is the medium of instruction;
Academic Performance: Demonstrated excellent academic records during undergraduate or postgraduate studies;
Educational Background: Hold a Bachelor's or Master's degree from a recognized/renowned university.

Exit Scholarship (2026-27)

Outstanding admitted students will be awarded a scholarship of up to HK$15,000 .

Application Requirements:
MACAH students who achieve a cumulative GPA of 3.50 or above and obtain a "Distinction" in their final grades will be awarded the Graduation Scholarship.

Programme objectives

The MACAH programme aims to:

nurture and inspire art and museum practitioners including curators, art educators and art administrators;
enhance students’ knowledge of curatorial studies and history of art in a global context;
broaden and refresh students’ approaches to art-historical research and curating;
expand students’ horizon and professional networks with scholars, art professionals and museum practitioners in the fields of art education, museums, cultural and creative industries in Hong Kong, Asia and beyond;
acquire skills in managing art collections, undertaking research, engaging in exhibition projects and spearheading public programmes in museums, galleries and other cultural venues.

On completion of the MACAH programme, students are expected to:

acquire an in-depth understanding of the making, meaning, theory and display of art in different contexts;
identify and evaluate important artworks of various art forms in art history using appropriately applicable theoretical concepts/perspectives and case studies of individual artists, art industries, and cultural policies
work individually and in groups to apply subject knowledge, methodologies and references in art-historical investigation and curating practices
develop professional knowledge and technical skills in handling objects, manuscripts, tools, materials, interior setups and physical conditions for art exhibition and collection storage
convey creative and critical ideas through writing, physical/digital exhibitions, programmes and other channels in response to societal needs, cultural issues and other aspects of life.

Prof. Elaine Yin Ning KWOK

Assistant Professor of Teaching in Department of DACI and Programme Director of MACAH

Dr. Caroline HA THUC

PhD. in Asian Contemporary Art, CityUHK

Dr HA THUC is a French Hong Kong-based curator, art critic and researcher. Ha Thuc’s field of research focuses on research-based art practices and the emergence of alternative modes of knowledge production.

Dr. Camellia Ni Na NG

PhD. in History of Chinese Arts, CUHK

Dr. Ng serves as Assistant Professor at the HKSYU. She also researched and taught at the CUHK and LU.

Ms. Joel Kai Ling KWONG

MA. in Cultural Management, CUHK

Ms. Kwong is an international media art curator and transmedia specialist. She is currently the Programme Dicector for Microwave International New Media Arts Festival and the founder of SIBYLS.

Ms. Lee Siu Yin Cora

MA in East Asian Art, Sotheby’s Institute of Art, London

Head of Education at the Hong Kong Maritime Museum, with expertise in museum education, curatorship, archives, and research focus on Chinese Export Art and the Maritime Silk Road.

Dr. Nga Wing LAW (Wen Yau)

PhD. Academy of Visual Arts, HKBU

As a cross-media artist, researcher, curator and writer, Dr. Law has been concentrating on performance/live art and social practices. She researched and taught at Asia Art Archive, Northwestern University and LU.

Dr. Charles Lai Chun Wai

PhD. Architectual History, HKU

Dr. Lai is an architect and researcher in the history and conservation of Hong Kong and Asian modern architecture.

Dr. ZHU Yi Felicia

PhD in Visual Studies, Lingnan University

Dr ZHU’s research focuses on museum studies, exhibiting culture, digital heritage, and Chinese art.

Mr. Gordon LO

Bachelor of Arts, CUHK

Gordon Lo is a seasoned arts leader, curator, producer, and educator,with over 15 years of experience in the arts and cultural sector.

programmeName: Master of Arts in Digital English Studies
summary: In an era where the rise of digital technologies and AI tools is fundamentally transforming English education and language studies—our Master’s programme in Digital English Studies stands as the very first to directly address this profound shift. Our innovative curriculum uniquely integrates rigorous training in traditional English studies—deep reading, contextual analysis, cultural sensitivity, creative imagination, intercultural competence, critical thinking, and narrative skills—with practical, hands-on expertise in digital tools for solving real-world challenges. By fostering digital competence, critical reflection, and intercultural understanding, we empower students to harness these technologies to enhance human creativity and productivity, contributing to societal flourishing, and navigate the ethical risks of a digitally mediated world.
suitableBackgrounds: MADES Career Forum 2026 MADES held a career forum featuring aviation and creative digital media professionals, where students gained practical career guidance and learned how their English major strengths—such as intercultural communication and cultural sensitivity—apply to real-world workplaces.

Career Prospects Career Pathways for Graduates Strategically positioned at the heart of the Greater Bay Area ( 大灣區 ) and Northern Metropolis ( 北部都會 ), the MADES programme is a nexus of research, innovation, and professional development.
Prepared to lead across Greater China, Asia, and the global stage, our graduates master the critical blend of linguistic, communicative, and cultural expertise with cutting-edge digital skills.
Scholarships All applicants will automatically be considered for scholarships upon application to the programme.

Admission Requirements Degree Applicants should hold a relevant bachelor’s degree, such as in English studies, or other disciplines related to literature, linguistics, communication, media studies or business , etc.

Language proficiency MOI is English: An applicant whose degree or, exceptionally, alternative qualification deemed equivalent from a tertiary institution is using English as the medium of instruction (MOI) will need no proof of English language proficiency.

MOI is not English: An applicant whose degree or, exceptionally, alternative qualification deemed equivalent from a tertiary institution is not using English as the medium of instruction (MOI) for the programme should obtain a minimum score of 79 (internet-based test) in the Test of English as a Foreign Language (TOEFL), or a band score of 6.5 or above in the International English Language Testing System (IELTS).

Applicants who have not yet fully met the language requirements may also be considered on a case-by-case basis, taking into account their academic background, work experience, interview performance, and other relevant factors.
learningObjectives: Cutting-Edge Research Methods Students will engage with state-of-the-art research methods such as language corpora and various data analysis tools, setting our programme apart from other applied English linguistics programmes.

Advanced Knowledge and Practical Digital Skills MADES is designed to equip students with advanced knowledge in applied linguistics and proficiency in digital research tools, providing a comprehensive and critical perspective on using digital technologies for effective English communication at future workplace.

Applicants from Chinese Mainland with qualifications awarded by overseas universities should provide Online Query of Overseas Credentials Evaluation Reports 國 ( 境 ) 外學歷學位認証書 issued by the Chinese Service Centre for Scholarly Exchange (CSCSE) ( 教育部留學服務中心 ) (applicable to applicants from the Chinese Mainland with qualifications awarded by overseas universities).

admissionNotes: Applicants should hold a relevant bachelor’s degree, such as in English studies, or other disciplines related to literature, linguistics, communication, media studies or business , etc. awarded by a tertiary education institution recognised for this purpose by Lingnan University; or have obtained an equivalent qualification; or have provided satisfactory evidence of academic and professional attainment. MOI is English: An applicant whose degree or, exceptionally, alternative qualification deemed equivalent from a tertiary institution is using English as the medium of instruction (MOI) will need no proof of English language proficiency. MOI is not English: An applicant whose degree or, exceptionally, alternative qualification deemed equivalent from a tertiary institution is not using English as the medium of instruction (MOI) for the programme should obtain a minimum score of 79 (internet-based test) in the Test of English as a Foreign Language (TOEFL), or a band score of 6.5 or above in the International English Language Testing System (IELTS). Applicants who have not yet fully met the language requirements may also be considered on a case-by-case basis, taking into account their academic background, work experience, interview performance, and other relevant factors. Language qualifications other than TOEFL or IELTS will be considered on a case-by-case basis, which may include an equivalent score in a recognised test, or an equivalent qualification (such as CET 6, CEFR, CEA, HKALE, GCE, HKDSE, etc.) as proof of an applicant's English language proficiency. Preference will be given to applicants with an IELTS score of 7.0 or higher, or a TOEFL score of 94 or higher. IELTS and TOEFL Validity: Lingnan University only accepts IELTS and TOEFL results that are within the validity period of 2 years from the time of submission of an admission application for the specific intake cohort.The validity period of CET-6 is 5 years from the time of submission of an admission application. Submission of Supporting Documents Please upload the scanned copies of the following supporting documents to Online Application System. Applications with incomplete/ missing document will not be processed. Copies of identity card, passport, or any other identity document(s) of home country; Original valid official English test report (Submit ONE of the followings): TOEFL: Internet-based: 79 or above

Master of Arts in Digital English Studies

10 Oct 2025 - 30 June 2026

Source: https://www.ln.edu.hk/eng/mades
Master of Arts in Digital English Studies (MADES)

In an era where the rise of digital technologies and AI tools is fundamentally transforming English education and language studies—our Master’s programme in Digital English Studies stands as the very first to directly address this profound shift. Our innovative curriculum uniquely integrates rigorous training in traditional English studies—deep reading, contextual analysis, cultural sensitivity, creative imagination, intercultural competence, critical thinking, and narrative skills—with practical, hands-on expertise in digital tools for solving real-world challenges. By fostering digital competence, critical reflection, and intercultural understanding, we empower students to harness these technologies to enhance human creativity and productivity, contributing to societal flourishing, and navigate the ethical risks of a digitally mediated world.

APPLY NOW

-->

Director’s message

I invite you to explore the brilliant work of our students, who are harnessing digital tools to reimagine writing, translation, and storytelling while rethinking the sociocultural significance of English for a digitalised multilingual world.

Highlights from Students' Works

MADES Students Digital Creation Portfolio

A Linguistic Journey from Xinjiang to Hong Kong - - from Mr. XIU

My English Learning Journey - - from Ms. Li

News & Events

Why choose MADES?

MADES programme offers an immersive one‑year experience with small‑class teaching, close teacher–student relationships, and dynamic interaction that foster personalised learning. Complemented by diverse extracurricular activities in art, culture, and the humanities within a vibrant English‑medium environment, the programme ensures students enjoy a rich and memorable academic and personal journey.

MADES Career Forum 2026

MADES held a career forum featuring aviation and creative digital media professionals, where students gained practical career guidance and learned how their English major strengths—such as intercultural communication and cultural sensitivity—apply to real-world workplaces.

MADES Field Trip

MADES students joined Crossroads Foundation for the simulation activity "Struggle for Survival", learning about world poverty and what we can do as individuals to help eradicate extreme poverty.

MADES Launching Collaboration with Artist and Entrepreneur Hilde Mertens

MADES joined hands with Artist and Entrepreneur Hilde Mertens on an exciting intercultural project, exploring contemporary calligraphy, language and cross-cultural experiences.

MADES Welcomes the Class of 2025

Our orientation for the class of 2025 was held on 29th August, where our fresh MADESers met with the Faculty and learned about all the exciting workshops, seminars, fieldtrips and internship opportunities that await them in the year ahead.

More coming soon...

Programme Highlights

Pioneering Integration

MADES is the first Master's programme in Hong Kong that uniquely joins the study of language and technology, offering a comprehensive educational experience that bridges academic study with real-world challenges.

Interdisciplinary Strength

MADES stands out by taking an interdisciplinary approach, strategically positioned at the intersection of linguistic theory, digital communication, and practical application.

Cutting-Edge Research Methods

Students will engage with state-of-the-art research methods such as language corpora and various data analysis tools, setting our programme apart from other applied English linguistics programmes.

Advanced Knowledge and Practical Digital Skills

MADES is designed to equip students with advanced knowledge in applied linguistics and proficiency in digital research tools, providing a comprehensive and critical perspective on using digital technologies for effective English communication at future workplace.

Career Prospects

Career Pathways for Graduates
Strategically positioned at the heart of the Greater Bay Area ( 大灣區 ) and Northern Metropolis ( 北部都會 ), the MADES programme is a nexus of research, innovation, and professional development. We leverage our faculty's groundbreaking research to cultivate a new generation of visionary, English-speaking professionals—educators, communicators , business leaders, and cultural experts. Prepared to lead across Greater China, Asia, and the global stage, our graduates master the critical blend of linguistic, communicative, and cultural expertise with cutting-edge digital skills.

1. English Education in Diverse Contexts
Innovative English Teacher : Integrates strong language proficiency with digital tools to deliver engaging, technology-enhanced, and contextually relevant English learning experiences.
Digital Educational Designer/Developer : Creates online courses, digital learning modules, and educational materials for schools, industries, professional organisations, and technology companies.

Note: The programme has been officially recognised by the Standing Committee on Language Education and Research (SCOLAR), as eligible for exemption from the Language Proficiency Requirement (English). For details, please refer to 20250731_Appendix1_EN.pdf .

2. Business, Trading, and Management
Intercultural Communication and Strategy Specialist : Applies advanced intercultural and professional communication skills, contextual analysis, and data analytics to excel in branding, strategic planning, and leadership roles, where cultural insights and digital competence drive success.

3. Creative Cultural Industries
Cultural Content Curator and Consultant : Deploy s critical thinking, creativity, and narrative skills from humanities training alongside technological tools to curate exhibitions, create content, and provide ethically informed consultations in museums, galleries, publishing houses, media companies, and other cultural industries.

4. Content and Communication Management
Digital Content Strategist/Manager : Develops content strategies, assesses performance, and aligns materials with brand narratives aiross digital platforms in marketing agencies and tech startups.
Technical Writer/Content Developer : Produces clear, user-centred documentation, guides, and knowledge-based resources for software, technological products, and complex services in software companies and engineering firms.

5. Cultural Data Analysis
UX Researcher : Applies qualitative and quantitative methods to investigate user behaviours, needs, and motivations across diverse cultural contexts, informing product design for e-commerce platforms and consumer apps.
Conversational AI Designer/Prompt Engineer : Designs dialogue flows, AI personalities, and training datasets for chatbots and virtual assistants, ensuring natural, contextually appropriate, and ethically sound interactions.

6. Research and Technical Specialisation
Digital Humanities Research Specialist : Employs advanced digital methods to explore humanistic questions in academic, cultural, or heritage projects.
Academic Researcher/Lecturer : Pursues advanced research and teaching in applied linguistics, education, and English studies, with a focus on digital language practices and their broader societal implications.

Programme Information
Curriculum Overview: The curriculum is composed of required courses, electives, and a capstone project, totaling 30 credits, covering recent developments in English linguistics and digital technology. Some of the highlighted courses include:

Required Courses
DES501: Digital English Linguistics: Innovation and Development
DES502: New Media, Discourse, and Society
DES503: Research Methods in Applied Linguistics
DES504: Corpus Linguistics: Fundamentals and Practice
Elective Courses

DES601: Introduction to Digital Research Tools and Resources
DES602: Advances in Digital English Linguistics
DES603: Data Visualisation for English Communication
DES604: Digital Literary Stylistics and the Practice of Creativity
DES605: Computer-Assisted English Language Learning
DES606: Technologies of Writing: Multimodality and Meaning
DES607: Digital Perspectives on Spoken English
DES608: Research Practicum
DES609: Special Topics in Digital Linguistics
DES612: Language, Creativity and Technology
Capstone Project
DES610: Capstone Project: Solving Real-world Issues

Recommended/ Supplementary Readings

Duration of Study: minimum 1 year and maximum 3 years (full-time mode) or minimum 2 years and maximum 5 years (part-time mode)

Staff : The programme will be taught by faculty members of the Department of English at Lingnan University, who are highly qualified teachers and researchers in their respective fields. Due to the highly interdisciplinary nature of the programme, the programme may invite experts from other units of Lingnan University, such as the School of Data Science, to lecture on relevant subjects.

Scholarships

All applicants will automatically be considered for scholarships upon application to the programme. No separate application is required.

Merit-Based Credential Scholarship
Applicants who meet one of the following criteria will automatically be awarded the Outstanding Credential Scholarship in the form of tuition waivers:

IELTS overall band score: 7 or above
Bachelor’s degree (4-year full-time mode) from a double-first-class or 985&211 university in Mainland China
Bachelor’s degree (4-year full-time mode) from a university ranked within the QS Top 300
Outstanding performance during interview

Entrance Scholarship
The programme offers 3 to 4 Entrance Scholarships in the form of tuition waivers to applicants with exceptional backgrounds or those who can make special contributions to Lingnan University. Selection criteria include academic excellence, interview performance, international experience, and diversity, among other factors.

Performance Scholarship
MADES students whose GPA ranks in the top ten at the end of the first semester will automatically be awarded Performance Scholarships.

All scholarships will be awarded at the end of the first semester.

Important note: Each student may receive only one scholarship throughout the programme. If a student qualifies for more than one scholarship, they will receive the single highest-value award (or as determined by the programme). No student will be granted multiple scholarships.

Admission Requirements

Degree
Applicants should hold a relevant bachelor’s degree, such as in English studies, or other disciplines related to literature, linguistics, communication, media studies or business , etc. awarded by a tertiary education institution recognised for this purpose by Lingnan University; or have obtained an equivalent qualification; or have provided satisfactory evidence of academic and professional attainment.

Language proficiency
MOI is English: An applicant whose degree or, exceptionally, alternative qualification deemed equivalent from a tertiary institution is using English as the medium of instruction (MOI) will need no proof of English language proficiency.

MOI is not English: An applicant whose degree or, exceptionally, alternative qualification deemed equivalent from a tertiary institution is not using English as the medium of instruction (MOI) for the programme should obtain a minimum score of 79 (internet-based test) in the Test of English as a Foreign Language (TOEFL), or a band score of 6.5 or above in the International English Language Testing System (IELTS).

Applicants who have not yet fully met the language requirements may also be considered on a case-by-case basis, taking into account their academic background, work experience, interview performance, and other relevant factors.

Language qualifications other than TOEFL or IELTS will be considered on a case-by-case basis, which may include an equivalent score in a recognised test, or an equivalent qualification (such as CET 6, CEFR, CEA, HKALE, GCE, HKDSE, etc.) as proof of an applicant's English language proficiency. Preference will be given to applicants with an IELTS score of 7.0 or higher, or a TOEFL score of 94 or higher.

IELTS and TOEFL Validity: Lingnan University only accepts IELTS and TOEFL results that are within the validity period of 2 years from the time of submission of an admission application for the specific intake cohort.The validity period of CET-6 is 5 years from the time of submission of an admission application.

Programme Operation, Management, Quality Assurance and Enhancement
To ensure the quality operation of the MADES programme, a Programme Director has been appointed to take full responsibility of the day-to-day operations of the programme.

A Programme and Curriculum Committee (PCC) has been set up to monitor and assure satisfactory operation of the programme. It reports to the Department Board and to the PSC

An Admissions Panel , consisted of the Programme Director as Chairman and two teaching staff, will be set up to conduct interviews and screen applications.

A Board of Examiners (BoE) will be set up to oversee assessment and examination matters, and make recommendations of students for graduation.

An Advisory Board will be set up to give advice on programme matters. The role of the Advisory Board is to act as an interface between the community and the programme.

A Staff-Student Consultation Committee will be set up to discuss matters concerning the programme and the students. It provides a platform for students to express their views on the programme and individual courses, and for the Department to solicit student feedback on specific aspects of the programme and courses for continuous improvement.

programmeName: Master of Arts in Digital History in Global Asia
summary: The DHGA is the first MA of its kind, both within Hong Kong and the wider region, and Lingnan’s liberal arts ethos combined with the Department of History’s teaching talent, make the university the ideal home for such a programme. Students will also be taught by a strong faculty in both the History of Asia and in the Digital Humanities.
suitableBackgrounds: The intersection of computational tools and digital methodologies with history has revolutionised the discipline in many ways: historians are now able to find and collect sources at their fingertips; analyse historical patterns and uncover once-marginalised voices through computational tools and methodologies; and visualise, present, and disseminate historical materials and findings to wider audiences through digital media and Virtual Reality (VR)/Augmented Reality (AR) simulations.

With a more specialised scope on global Asia's history, our MA programme will allow students to gain an in-depth understanding of digital technology in relation to the history discipline through specific case studies.
The programme is suitable for both recent bachelor degree graduates and for professionals looking to develop their careers.

No specific technical background is necessary – we provide you with the training necessary to understand digital humanities in historical research and different digital methods to interpret historical analysis.

Designed to align with the Level-6 parameters of Hong Kong Qualification Framework, DHGA graduates will be well-positioned for careers that will be in demand in a range of fields such as education, the creative media, cultural management, heritage preservation, information science, programming, and academia.
Career Prospects Examples of career paths include: Our graduates will be especially well-positioned to take up jobs in education, academia and data analyst.
Academia: Pursuing a further degree in History or Area studies.

courseDescriptions.description: This pillar bridges historical inquiry with computational methods. Students learn and apply quantitative approaches—from cliometrics to AI-assisted analysis—while critically examining how these methods shape historical understanding. Focus Areas Quantitative History & AI: Understanding the debates around quantification and AI, and their impact on historiography. Database Construction: The foundation of nearly all digital history work. Students learn to extract data (increasingly using AI-assisted methods), design ontologies, develop domain models, and manage datasets at varying scales—from small collections to large corpora. This systematic approach to organizing historical information underpins all subsequent analysis and presentation. Historical Simulation: Reconstructing and analyzing historical processes through computational models, emulators, GIS scenes, and 3D environments. These tools enable researchers to test hypotheses, explore counterfactual scenarios, and gain analytical insights before presenting findings to the public. Digital Curation & Public History: Presenting and communicating history to diverse audiences through digital storytelling, online exhibitions, museum education, and data visualization. Relevant Courses: DHG501 , DHG502 , DHG503 , DHG505 , DHG507 , DHG508
courseDescriptions.descriptionZh: 该支柱将历史探究与计算方法联系起来。学生学习并应用从气候计量学到人工智能辅助分析的定量方法，同时批判性地研究这些方法如何塑造历史理解。重点领域定量历史和人工智能：了解围绕量化和人工智能的争论及其对史学的影响。数据库构建：几乎所有数字历史工作的基础。学生学习提取数据（越来越多地使用人工智能辅助方法）、设计本体、开发领域模型以及管理不同规模的数据集（从小型集合到大型语料库）。这种组织历史信息的系统方法是所有后续分析和呈现的基础。历史模拟：通过计算模型、模拟器、GIS 场景和 3D 环境重建和分析历史过程。这些工具使研究人员能够在向公众展示研究结果之前测试假设、探索反事实场景并获得分析见解。数字策展和公共历史：通过数字故事讲述、在线展览、博物馆教育和数据可视化向不同的受众展示和传播历史。相关课程：DHG501、DHG502、DHG503、DHG505、DHG507、DHG508

courseDescriptions.description: This pillar focuses on the practical workflow of digital research. Students learn to manage the entire lifecycle of a project, integrating distinct tools into a seamless process. This is what deep research truly means—a rigorous, methodical workflow grounded in scholarly practice. Workflows and Tools Research Design Workflows: Research project management Tools: Markdown, Git Reference Management Workflows: Bibliographic organization, citation management Tools: Zotero, BibTeX Personal Knowledge Management (PKM) Workflows: Note-taking, linking ideas, building a "second brain" Tools: Obsidian, Local RAG Pipeline Data Lifecycle Workflows: Digitization, data structuring, persistence Tools: OpenRefine, Tropy, Omeka Development Environment Workflows: Version control, dependency management Tools: VS Code, GitHub, uv, npm AI-Assisted Research Workflow Workflows: Semantic search, AI-assisted literature synthesis Tools: Semantic Scholar, Consensus, Elicit Digital Native Writing Formats Workflows: Academic writing in digital and AI-friendly formats Tools: Markdown, LaTeX, Pandoc Relevant Courses: DHG501 , DHG503
courseDescriptions.descriptionZh: 该支柱侧重于数字研究的实际工作流程。学生学习管理项目的整个生命周期，将不同的工具集成到无缝流程中。这就是深度研究的真正含义——以学术实践为基础的严谨、有条理的工作流程。工作流程和工具 研究设计工作流程：研究项目管理 工具：Markdown、Git 参考管理 工作流程：书目组织、引文管理 工具：Zotero、BibTeX 个人知识管理 (PKM) 工作流程：做笔记、链接想法、构建“第二大脑” 工具：Obsidian、本地 RAG 管道 数据生命周期工作流程：数字化、数据结构化、持久化 工具：OpenRefine、Tropy、Omeka 开发环境工作流程：版本控制、依赖管理 工具：VS Code、GitHub、uv、npm 人工智能辅助研究工作流程 工作流程：语义搜索、人工智能辅助文献合成 工具：语义学者、共识、引出数字原生写作格式 工作流程：以数字和人工智能友好格式进行学术写作 工具：Markdown、LaTeX、Pandoc 相关课程：DHG501、DHG503

courseDescriptions.courseName: DHG502 Digital Approaches in Historical Research (3 credits)
courseDescriptions.description: This course familiarises students with the approaches, theories, and tools of digital historical scholarship. It is aimed at developing their skills as thinkers, writers, and creators of Asian historical narratives with the use of digital approaches. Throughout the course, students will be exposed to several digital tools, such as QGIS (Mapping) and Gephi (Networks and Data Visualisation) and will utilise these platforms to enhance their digital skills in historical research and presentation. Drawing on specific case studies, students will be acquainted with how digital technology has revolutionised research on global Asia from the perspective of urban studies, heritage, mobilities, networks, race, class and gender, etc.
courseDescriptions.descriptionZh: 本课程使学生熟悉数字历史学术的方法、理论和工具。它旨在利用数字方法培养他们作为亚洲历史叙事的思想家、作家和创造者的技能。在整个课程中，学生将接触到多种数字工具，例如 QGIS（制图）和 Gephi（网络和数据可视化），并将利用这些平台来增强他们在历史研究和演示中的数字技能。通过具体案例研究，学生将从城市研究、遗产、流动性、网络、种族、阶级和性别等角度了解数字技术如何彻底改变全球亚洲的研究。

courseDescriptions.description: This course offers students the chance to critically assess the digital methods used in historical studies and the digital ways through which history is presented to the public. Students will engage with scholarly criticisms of digital history practices. Questions that will be explored include the inclusivity/exclusivity of digital history, the limitations of relying on selected data, how historical narratives are controlled and communicated to the public, and whether AI can be used responsibly to reshape the field.
courseDescriptions.descriptionZh: 本课程为学生提供了批判性评估历史研究中使用的数字方法以及向公众呈现历史​​的数字方式的机会。学生将参与对数字历史实践的学术批评。将探讨的问题包括数字历史的包容性/排他性、依赖选定数据的局限性、如何控制历史叙述并向公众传达，以及是否可以负责任地使用人工智能来重塑该领域。

courseDescriptions.courseName: DHG504 Visualisation and Digital Mapping of Asian Cities (3 credits)
courseDescriptions.description: This course focuses on the 'spatial turn' in historical studies and introduces a range of geospatial technologies available to historians including spatial theory and thinking, geographical text analysis, and GIS. This unit will take place over two complementary halves, the first considering the driving motives and potential uses of geospatial research, and the second turning to how such research has been applied to Asian case studies. Students will not only have the opportunity to acquire transferable digital skills in visualising data in two and three dimensions, creating timelines, producing maps, and performing spatial statistical analyses, but also develop an all-rounded understanding of the limitations and practical implications of geospatial technologies.
courseDescriptions.descriptionZh: 本课程重点关注历史研究中的“空间转向”，并介绍历史学家可用的一系列地理空间技术，包括空间理论和思维、地理文本分析和地理信息系统。该单元将分为两个互补的部分，第一个部分考虑地理空间研究的驱动动机和潜在用途，第二个部分讨论如何将此类研究应用于亚洲案例研究。学生不仅有机会获得二维和三维数据可视化、创建时间线、制作地图和执行空间统计分析等可转移的数字技能，而且还能全面了解地理空间技术的局限性和实际影响。

courseDescriptions.description: This course exposes students to the theory, practices, and issues surrounding digital public history in adjacent fields at museums, libraries, governments, and historical and communal institutions. Students will explore methods of presenting, communicating, researching, and creating history with digital technology that is accessible to and engages the public, and will have the opportunity to create their own work of digital public history in relation to contemporary concerns in Asian societies.
courseDescriptions.descriptionZh: 本课程向学生展示博物馆、图书馆、政府以及历史和公共机构相关领域的数字公共历史的理论、实践和问题。学生将探索利用数字技术呈现、交流、研究和创造历史的方法，这些技术可供公众使用并吸引公众参与，并将有机会创作自己的与亚洲社会当代问题相关的数字公共历史作品。

courseDescriptions.courseName: DHG506 3D Remodelling of Asian Heritage (3 credits)
courseDescriptions.description: This course will introduce students to applications in 3D modelling and basic skills in 3D modelling and animation. Students will focus on the challenges of digitally reconstructing Asia's past, problematise digital exhibition, and museums through critical heritage management, and public archaeology, etc., and take part in the reconstruction of a significant Asian cultural heritage. The course will also cover the ethical considerations on digital preservations and how digital approaches can contribute to the preservation of Asian cultural heritage sites and artifacts.
courseDescriptions.descriptionZh: 本课程将向学生介绍 3D 建模的应用以及 3D 建模和动画的基本技能。学生将重点关注数字化重建亚洲过去的挑战，通过批判性遗产管理和公共考古学等解决数字展览和博物馆问题，并参与亚洲重要文化遗产的重建。该课程还将涵盖数字保存的伦理考虑以及数字方法如何为亚洲文化遗产和文物的保护做出贡献。

courseDescriptions.courseName: DHG507 Rethinking the Historical Archive (3 credits)
courseDescriptions.description: This course introduces students to the shifting landscape of historical archiving and preservation that has accompanied the 'digital turn.' The course will focus on how digital access to resources is reframing academic research, the potential benefits and innovations increased access has for the discipline, and also the pitfalls that commonly accompany the digital curation of source materials. Students will further encounter the ways programming, Optical Character Recognition, and the free version of Natural Language Processing can provide new access to - and new ways of working with - digitised archives. This course looks at both scholarly debates and a variety of archival digitisation and databasing projects to explore the research questions, methods, and logic behind their creation, and to critically frame the potential and limitations of digital archival research.
courseDescriptions.descriptionZh: 本课程向学生介绍伴随“数字化转型”而来的历史归档和保存的不断变化的景观。该课程将重点关注资源的数字化访问如何重塑学术研究、增加访问对学科的潜在好处和创新，以及源材料的数字化管理通常伴随的陷阱。学生将进一步了解编程、光学字符识别和自然语言处理的免费版本可以提供对数字化档案的新访问和新的处理方式。本课程着眼于学术辩论和各种档案数字化和数据库项目，探索其创作背后的研究问题、方法和逻辑，并批判性地界定数字档案研究的潜力和局限性。

courseDescriptions.courseName: DHG509 Global History of Modern East Asia (3 credits)
courseDescriptions.description: This course introduces major themes in the history of modern East Asia, including China, Japan, and Korea, as well as Taiwan, Macau, and Hong Kong. With an emphasis on relevant digital sources, databases, and tools, it introduces students to themes ranging from the shaping of East Asia as a political and cultural entity to the evolution of East Asia's relationship with the Western world, to overlapping narratives and episodes of cooperation and tension linked to colonialism, nationalism, and independence. The themes will be studied from varying perspectives, in particular the dynamics of continuity and change within individual East Asian societies and across East Asia and the transformative roles of East Asia in the modern world.
courseDescriptions.descriptionZh: 本课程介绍近代东亚历史的主要主题，包括中国、日本、韩国以及台湾、澳门和香港。它强调相关的数字资源、数据库和工具，向学生介绍从东亚作为一个政治和文化实体的塑造到东亚与西方世界关系的演变，再到与殖民主义、民族主义和独立相关的重叠叙事以及合作和紧张关系的事件等主题。这些主题将从不同的角度进行研究，特别是东亚各个社会内部和整个东亚的连续性和变化的动态，以及东亚在现代世界中的变革性作用。

courseDescriptions.courseName: DHG511 Southeast Asia in the World (3 credits)
courseDescriptions.description: This course introduces the history of Southeast Asia, covering a broad temporal framework that runs from antiquity to the post-war period. It permits students to engage with the historical making of Southeast Asia through significant themes such as ancient trading systems, colonialism, the rise of print culture, modernisation, anti-colonialism, and independence. Students will be introduced to relevant digital sources, tools, and platforms to enrich their study and research of Southeast Asian history.
courseDescriptions.descriptionZh: 本课程介绍东南亚的历史，涵盖从古代到战后的广泛时间框架。它允许学生通过古代贸易体系、殖民主义、印刷文化的兴起、现代化、反殖民主义和独立等重要主题来参与东南亚的历史创造。将向学生介绍相关的数字资源、工具和平台，以丰富他们对东南亚历史的学习和研究。

importantCourses.courseName: DHG502 Digital Approaches in Historical Research (3 credits)
importantCourses.whyImportant: The official page provides a course description for this course; the recommendation assistant may use that description conservatively when matching student preparation and goals.
relatedStudentBackgrounds: The intersection of computational tools and digital methodologies with history has revolutionised the discipline in many ways: historians are now able to find and collect sources at their fingertips; analyse historical patterns and uncover once-marginalised voices through computational tools and methodologies; and visualise, present, and disseminate historical materials and findings to wider audiences through digital media and Virtual Reality (VR)/Augmented Reality (AR) simulations.

With a more specialised scope on global Asia's history, our MA programme will allow students to gain an in-depth understanding of digital technology in relation to the history discipline through specific case studies.
The programme is suitable for both recent bachelor degree graduates and for professionals looking to develop their careers.
relatedCareerGoals: The programme is suitable for both recent bachelor degree graduates and for professionals looking to develop their careers.

Designed to align with the Level-6 parameters of Hong Kong Qualification Framework, DHGA graduates will be well-positioned for careers that will be in demand in a range of fields such as education, the creative media, cultural management, heritage preservation, information science, programming, and academia.
Career Prospects Examples of career paths include: Our graduates will be especially well-positioned to take up jobs in education, academia and data analyst.

skillsDeveloped: What’s more, many of the early developments in the digital humanities, digital history and geographical information systems, were made by scholars in Asian studies.

As students put their acquired knowledge and skillsets to practise in individual/group digital projects, they will gain a stronger understanding of how digital tools help advance the field of history and the dissemination of historical findings.

It will be attractive to those seeking to advance their research in history, strengthen their knowledge and skills of teaching, writing, and presenting Asia's global past, and/or develop fundamental knowledge and skills in the use of digital technologies in the historical field and the information and cultural sectors.
The programme is suitable for both recent bachelor degree graduates and for professionals looking to develop their careers.

No specific technical background is necessary – we provide you with the training necessary to understand digital humanities in historical research and different digital methods to interpret historical analysis.
Explain the origins, developments, rationale, basic methods, and uses of digital history in relation to Asian studies; 2.
Education: Teaching and training others on global and Asian history with different digital approaches and methods.
Our programme offers comprehensive training across four key areas that equip students with both theoretical knowledge and practical skills.
Historical Training Students receive rigorous training in historical inquiry, focusing on how to locate, critique, and utilize primary sources to construct compelling narratives.
Fact Verification: In an era where AI can generate plausible but inaccurate content, training students to rigorously verify authenticity and ground all claims in solid historical evidence.
careerDirections: This has, in turn, created unprecedented opportunities for researchers, teachers, students, and the general public to engage and appreciate history as users, observers, creators, and/or contributors.
The programme is suitable for both recent bachelor degree graduates and for professionals looking to develop their careers.

Designed to align with the Level-6 parameters of Hong Kong Qualification Framework, DHGA graduates will be well-positioned for careers that will be in demand in a range of fields such as education, the creative media, cultural management, heritage preservation, information science, programming, and academia.
Career Prospects Examples of career paths include: Our graduates will be especially well-positioned to take up jobs in education, academia and data analyst.
Consultant or Strategist: Providing advice and guidance to organizations on how to utilize digital methods on conducting network and data analysis.
Data Analyst: Interpreting complex digital data to assist businesses in decision-making, while ensuring ethical use of AI and data privacy.
These tools enable researchers to test hypotheses, explore counterfactual scenarios, and gain analytical insights before presenting findings to the public.

Departmental Guidelines for Using GAI Tools

Master of Arts in Digital History in Global Asia

Understanding Asian and Global History with Cutting-Edge Digital Tools.

The DHGA is the first MA of its kind, both within Hong Kong and the wider region, and Lingnan’s liberal arts ethos combined with the Department of History’s teaching talent, make the university the ideal home for such a programme. Students will also be taught by a strong faculty in both the History of Asia and in the Digital Humanities.



“We hope to train our students to, first, use the tools really well and then to understand the meaning of the new data that is generated,” Prof Leung Vincent, the Programme Director says.



The focus on Asia’s global past in the programme is intended to provide students with concrete material to apply the digital tools to. What’s more, many of the early developments in the digital humanities, digital history and geographical information systems, were made by scholars in Asian studies.



“One of the first corpuses of text fully digitised was the Buddhist corpus,” Prof Leung notes. This track record provides the MA with deep foundations to build on, and Asia continues to be at the forefront of the evolution of digital history.

Programme Poster

Lingnan University’s new MA
in Digital History in Global Asia

What is Digital history (DH)?

Digital history (DH) has enabled a deeper understanding of the past through the use of digital technologies in the research, analysis, and preservation of texts, visuals, and other cultural artifacts. The intersection of computational tools and digital methodologies with history has revolutionised the discipline in many ways: historians are now able to find and collect sources at their fingertips; analyse historical patterns and uncover once-marginalised voices through computational tools and methodologies; and visualise, present, and disseminate historical materials and findings to wider audiences through digital media and Virtual Reality (VR)/Augmented Reality (AR) simulations. This has, in turn, created unprecedented opportunities for researchers, teachers, students, and the general public to engage and appreciate history as users, observers, creators, and/or contributors.

What Will I Study?

With a more specialised scope on global Asia's history, our MA programme will allow students to gain an in-depth understanding of digital technology in relation to the history discipline through specific case studies. As students put their acquired knowledge and skillsets to practise in individual/group digital projects, they will gain a stronger understanding of how digital tools help advance the field of history and the dissemination of historical findings. It will be attractive to those seeking to advance their research in history, strengthen their knowledge and skills of teaching, writing, and presenting Asia's global past, and/or develop fundamental knowledge and skills in the use of digital technologies in the historical field and the information and cultural sectors.

Who is this programme for?

The programme is suitable for both recent bachelor degree graduates and for professionals looking to develop their careers. No specific technical background is necessary – we provide you with the training necessary to understand digital humanities in historical research and different digital methods to interpret historical analysis.

What can I do with the DHGA programme?

Designed to align with the Level-6 parameters of Hong Kong Qualification Framework, DHGA graduates will be well-positioned for careers that will be in demand in a range of fields such as education, the creative media, cultural management, heritage preservation, information science, programming, and academia.

Learning Outcomes
Upon completion, students will be able to:

1. Explain the origins, developments, rationale, basic methods, and uses of digital history in relation to Asian studies;



2. Classify how digital technologies are advancing and reshaping historical teaching, research, and presentation.;



3. Implement digital application to history content that led to new perspectives to and/or interpretations and/or presentation of Asia’s global past to a broader public audience;



4. Critique the merits and challenges digital approaches pose to the learning, teaching, research, and presentation of Asian history.

Career Prospects
Examples of career paths include:

Our graduates will be especially well-positioned to take up jobs in education, academia and data analyst.



Museums and archives:

Preserving their collections, education and exhibition of objects and ideas.



Education:

Teaching and training others on global and Asian history with different digital approaches and methods.



Consultant or Strategist:

Providing advice and guidance to organizations on how to utilize digital methods on conducting network and data analysis.



Data Analyst:

Interpreting complex digital data to assist businesses in decision-making, while ensuring ethical use of AI and data privacy.



Academia:

Pursuing a further degree in History or Area studies.

Programme Curriculum

What You Will Learn

With a more specialised scope on global Asia's history, our MA programme will allow students to gain an in-depth understanding of digital technology in relation to the history discipline through specific case studies. As students put their acquired knowledge and skillsets to practise in individual/group digital projects, they will gain a stronger understanding of how digital tools help advance the field of history and the dissemination of historical findings. It will be attractive to those seeking to advance their research in history, strengthen their knowledge and skills of teaching, writing, and presenting Asia's global past, and/or develop fundamental knowledge and skills in the use of digital technologies in the historical field and the information and cultural sectors.
Our programme offers comprehensive training across four key areas that equip students with both theoretical knowledge and practical skills.
1. Historical Training Students receive rigorous training in historical inquiry, focusing on how to locate, critique, and utilize primary sources to construct compelling narratives. The curriculum is deeply rooted in the context of Global Asia, offering students a unique vantage point to understand regional dynamics and Hong Kong's specific historical trajectory through a digital lens.
Focus Areas
Regional & Global History: Specialized study of Global Asia and Hong Kong's role in world history.

Historiography: Tracing the evolution of historical thought from Sima Qian and Herodotus to modern historiography.

Primary Source Analysis: Advanced techniques for discovering and critically evaluating historical archives.

Fact Verification: In an era where AI can generate plausible but inaccurate content, training students to rigorously verify authenticity and ground all claims in solid historical evidence.

Relevant Courses: DHG501 , DHG505 , DHG508 , DHG509 , DHG510 , DHG511 , DHG512

2. Digital Approach to History This pillar bridges historical inquiry with computational methods. Students learn and apply quantitative approaches—from cliometrics to AI-assisted analysis—while critically examining how these methods shape historical understanding.
Focus Areas
Quantitative History & AI: Understanding the debates around quantification and AI, and their impact on historiography.

Database Construction: The foundation of nearly all digital history work. Students learn to extract data (increasingly using AI-assisted methods), design ontologies, develop domain models, and manage datasets at varying scales—from small collections to large corpora. This systematic approach to organizing historical information underpins all subsequent analysis and presentation.

Historical Simulation: Reconstructing and analyzing historical processes through computational models, emulators, GIS scenes, and 3D environments. These tools enable researchers to test hypotheses, explore counterfactual scenarios, and gain analytical insights before presenting findings to the public.

Digital Curation & Public History: Presenting and communicating history to diverse audiences through digital storytelling, online exhibitions, museum education, and data visualization.

Relevant Courses: DHG501 , DHG502 , DHG503 , DHG505 , DHG507 , DHG508

3. Hands-on Technical Skills Building on the foundations of Historical Training and Digital Approach, this pillar focuses on teaching techniques rather than tools alone. While tools evolve rapidly, the underlying methods and research questions remain fundamental. Students gain solid, practical skills by learning tools through the lens of enduring techniques.

4. Digital Research Workflow This pillar focuses on the practical workflow of digital research. Students learn to manage the entire lifecycle of a project, integrating distinct tools into a seamless process. This is what deep research truly means—a rigorous, methodical workflow grounded in scholarly practice.

Digital Native Writing Formats

Programme Courses

Required Courses (3 Courses, 9 credits) DHG501 Introduction to Digital History in Global Asia (3 credits) This course introduces students to digital history, providing them with foundational knowledge on its emergence, developments, benefits, and challenges, and its methods, theories, and practices. It will begin with an exploration of digital history's relationship to history, digital humanities, and Asian studies, and an overview of how historians of global Asian history have used and created digital history. Throughout the course, students will study and explore existing digital history projects and probe into the different kinds of digital expressions that historians have deployed. By the end of this course, students will be introduced to Digital History and the basics to create a digital history project.
DHG502 Digital Approaches in Historical Research (3 credits) This course familiarises students with the approaches, theories, and tools of digital historical scholarship. It is aimed at developing their skills as thinkers, writers, and creators of Asian historical narratives with the use of digital approaches. Throughout the course, students will be exposed to several digital tools, such as QGIS (Mapping) and Gephi (Networks and Data Visualisation) and will utilise these platforms to enhance their digital skills in historical research and presentation. Drawing on specific case studies, students will be acquainted with how digital technology has revolutionised research on global Asia from the perspective of urban studies, heritage, mobilities, networks, race, class and gender, etc.
DHG508 Critical Digital History (3 credits) This course offers students the chance to critically assess the digital methods used in historical studies and the digital ways through which history is presented to the public. Students will engage with scholarly criticisms of digital history practices. Questions that will be explored include the inclusivity/exclusivity of digital history, the limitations of relying on selected data, how historical narratives are controlled and communicated to the public, and whether AI can be used responsibly to reshape the field.

Elective Courses (any 5 courses, 15 credits) DHG503 Digital Data Management in Asian Studies (3 credits) Digital Data Management refers to the organisation, cleaning, analysis, and interpretation of data to support historical research. This course will walk students through the process of digital data management for historians, librarians, archivists, and museums curators. It will draw on the experiences of Asian libraries, archives, and museums to give students an in-depth understanding of the essential components of data management (e.g., collection selection, organisation, documentation, storage, analysis, preservation, presentation, and dissemination) with a focus on Asian history, culture, and society. Students will be expected to work with Excel and SQL to build a database and Gephi for data visualisation.
DHG504 Visualisation and Digital Mapping of Asian Cities (3 credits) This course focuses on the 'spatial turn' in historical studies and introduces a range of geospatial technologies available to historians including spatial theory and thinking, geographical text analysis, and GIS. This unit will take place over two complementary halves, the first considering the driving motives and potential uses of geospatial research, and the second turning to how such research has been applied to Asian case studies. Students will not only have the opportunity to acquire transferable digital skills in visualising data in two and three dimensions, creating timelines, producing maps, and performing spatial statistical analyses, but also develop an all-rounded understanding of the limitations and practical implications of geospatial technologies.
DHG505 Digital Public History (3 credits) This course exposes students to the theory, practices, and issues surrounding digital public history in adjacent fields at museums, libraries, governments, and historical and communal institutions. Students will explore methods of presenting, communicating, researching, and creating history with digital technology that is accessible to and engages the public, and will have the opportunity to create their own work of digital public history in relation to contemporary concerns in Asian societies.
DHG506 3D Remodelling of Asian Heritage (3 credits) This course will introduce students to applications in 3D modelling and basic skills in 3D modelling and animation. Students will focus on the challenges of digitally reconstructing Asia's past, problematise digital exhibition, and museums through critical heritage management, and public archaeology, etc., and take part in the reconstruction of a significant Asian cultural heritage. The course will also cover the ethical considerations on digital preservations and how digital approaches can contribute to the preservation of Asian cultural heritage sites and artifacts.
DHG507 Rethinking the Historical Archive (3 credits) This course introduces students to the shifting landscape of historical archiving and preservation that has accompanied the 'digital turn.' The course will focus on how digital access to resources is reframing academic research, the potential benefits and innovations increased access has for the discipline, and also the pitfalls that commonly accompany the digital curation of source materials. Students will further encounter the ways programming, Optical Character Recognition, and the free version of Natural Language Processing can provide new access to - and new ways of working with - digitised archives. This course looks at both scholarly debates and a variety of archival digitisation and databasing projects to explore the research questions, methods, and logic behind their creation, and to critically frame the potential and limitations of digital archival research.
DHG509 Global History of Modern East Asia (3 credits) This course introduces major themes in the history of modern East Asia, including China, Japan, and Korea, as well as Taiwan, Macau, and Hong Kong. With an emphasis on relevant digital sources, databases, and tools, it introduces students to themes ranging from the shaping of East Asia as a political and cultural entity to the evolution of East Asia's relationship with the Western world, to overlapping narratives and episodes of cooperation and tension linked to colonialism, nationalism, and independence. The themes will be studied from varying perspectives, in particular the dynamics of continuity and change within individual East Asian societies and across East Asia and the transformative roles of East Asia in the modern world.
DHG510 Hong Kong and Global Asia (3 credits) This course examines the history from British Hong Kong to the present by engaging with emerging themes that range from governance issues, social stratification, cultural developments, urbanization, etc. Students will study the economic, political, and social developments of Hong Kong, focusing in particular on the complex interplay between the British and Chinese cultures at policy and grassroots levels. The course also encourages students to analyse a century of Hong Kong history through the deployment of digitised primary sources and visualise Hong Kong society from the lenses of relevant digital history platforms. Special attention will be paid to local digitisation projects, museum narratives, and the use of digital platforms in tracing and examining Hong Kong's formation from a war-torn city to a world-renowned metropolis of international standing. Some of the themes featured will touch on the city's postwar recuperation, the development of civil society, the rise of a nascent middle class, Hong Kong's relations to the Cold War, the path to the handover, and Hong Kong's post-1997 national integration and subsequent shaping as a part of the Greater Bay Area.
DHG511 Southeast Asia in the World (3 credits) This course introduces the history of Southeast Asia, covering a broad temporal framework that runs from antiquity to the post-war period. It permits students to engage with the historical making of Southeast Asia through significant themes such as ancient trading systems, colonialism, the rise of print culture, modernisation, anti-colonialism, and independence. Students will be introduced to relevant digital sources, tools, and platforms to enrich their study and research of Southeast Asian history.
DHG512 Special Topics in History (3 credits) This course provides an opportunity for students to study and discuss selected topics in history.

Capstone Project (6 credits) DHG601 Capstone Project (6 credits) This capstone project enables students to deploy digital approaches and research methodologies learned throughout the master's programme, to explore in-depth relevant topics of personal interest, and to develop skills of critical research, analysis, the usage of digital applications, data organization and visualisation, presentation, and academic writing.
The capstone research is conducted through individual or group (max 2 persons) work under direct supervision, with consultation meetings between students and advisors arranged roughly once a month. Students will work with a supervisor whose expertise includes topics relevant to their project and who will provide guidance and academic support with developing, researching, and writing the MA capstone paper.
Word Count Requirements:
Students working in pairs: 8,000-10,000 words
Students working individually: 5,000-6,000 words

Footnotes and quotations from primary and secondary sources in the main text are included in the word count, but appendices, tables, charts, maps, image captions, and bibliographies are excluded from the final word count.
The capstone project may focus primarily on themes and concepts from within the fields of Digital History and its impact in Global Asia.

Graduation Requirements

To qualify for the award of the Master of Arts in Digital History in Global Asia offered by Lingnan University, students must, where applicable:
Complete 9 courses (8 courses and 1 capstone project) and obtain a minimum of 30 credits;

Achieve a minimum CGPA of 2.67; and

Fulfil the University exit requirement on English language competency, if applicable; and fulfil all University/programme requirements.

A student who has fulfilled all the requirements for graduation shall be awarded a Master of Arts in Digital History in Global Asia degree with one of the following classifications:
Degree Classification
Minimum Cumulative G.P.A.

Distinction
3.50

Pass
2.67

Degree Recognition

As a recognized taught postgraduate programme, our graduates are eligible to apply for official CSCSE certification (留服认证) upon graduation. While this is an independent administrative step that you will submit directly to the CSCSE, our programme team is fully committed to supporting you and will assist in preparing the necessary academic documentation to ensure a smooth application process. You can review the standard procedures on the official CSCSE website ( https://zwfw.cscse.edu.cn/ ).

Admission & Application Requirements

Admission Requirements

Applicants shall fulfil the minimum admission requirements for taught postgraduate’s degree programmes as follows.

Academic Qualification(s) :
hold a relevant bachelor’s degree awarded by a tertiary education institution recognised for this purpose by the University; or
have obtained an equivalent qualification; or
have provided satisfactory evidence of academic and professional attainment.

In addition to the above, applicants whose degree or, exceptionally, an alternative qualification deemed equivalent from a tertiary institution not using English as the medium of instruction (MOI) for the programme should obtain:

a minimum score 79 (internet-based test) in the Test of English as a Foreign Language (TOEFL) 1 ; or
a band score of 6.0 or above in the International English Language Testing System (IELTS) 1 , or
a minimum score of 450 or above in the College English Test Band 6 (CET-6), with proof of competency in spoken English 2
an equivalent score in a recognised test, or
an equivalent qualification

1 For IELTS and TOEFL qualifications for admission purpose, the University only accepts results of tests that are conducted at a test centre and within the validity period, viz. 2 years, from the time of submission of an admission application for the specific intake cohort.

2 For CET-6 results submitted for admission purposes, the University accepts only those obtained within the five-year validity period, counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort. Applicants must also demonstrate competency in spoken English through an oral interview conducted by the Programme and/or obtain grade B or above in the CET-6 Speaking English Test.

Additional Admission Requirements:
Applicants may be invited to attend a face-to-face or video interview when it is deemed necessary.

Scholarship
We offer annual awards in recognition of outstanding academic and creative achievement among its students.

MADHGA Capstone Project Scholarship Awards

The MADHGA Capstone Project Scholarship Awards honour graduating students who have demonstrated exceptional performance in their final-year Capstone Project (DHG601). Supervisors may nominate one or more of their students for consideration. No separate application is necessary. Two awards will be given each year:

Best Historical Argument : for the Capstone Project that exhibits outstanding originality, analytical depth, and clarity of historical interpretation.
Best Digital Showcase : for the Capstone Project that demonstrates exemplary innovation and technical excellence in digital presentation or methodology.

The final decision will be made by a judging panel consisting of scholars from the History Department of Lingnan University, as well as local and/or international scholars of digital humanities from outside the university.

MADHGA Academic Excellence Scholarship Award

The MADHGA Academic Excellence Scholarship Award recognizes the student who has attained the highest cumulative GPA in the programme. No application or nomination is necessary; all graduating students are automatically considered.

How to Apply
Applications must be submitted online via Lingnan University's Online Application System *. A non-refundable HK$400 application fee is required upon submission.

* Note: All documents submitted will not be returned. Information on all unsuccessful applicants and applicants who have declined the offer will be destroyed after the admission exercise. All application fees paid are non-refundable and no receipt for application fee will be issued.

Application Period
We will be accepting applications from 1 Oct 2025 - June 30 2026 .
Applicants are strongly advised to submit their applications as soon as possible.
*We continue to accept late applications if spaces are still available, and applicants are still considered for the entrance scholarship.

FAQ

When will the interview result be released?

The interview result will usually reach applicants within one to two weeks by email.

If I have received a conditional offer but not met the minimum English requirement, what should I do?

We advise you to take IELTS or TOEFL to meet the minimum English requirement. If you fail to meet the minimum English requirement by the specified deadline, the conditional offer will be forfeited.

Where will the classes take place?

Classes will normally take place on our West Kowloon campus, which is conveniently accessible by high-speed rail. The travel time between Shenzhen and West Kowloon is about 14–18 minutes from Futian or Shenzhen North.

Does MADHGA provide internship opportunities?

MADHGA provides internship opportunities based on individual performance and the availability of internship places each year.

Will I be able to seek qualification for Chinese Service Centre for Scholarly Exchange (CSCSE) certification after graduating from MADHGA?

Graduates will be eligible to apply for official CSCSE certification upon graduation. Our team is fully committed to supporting and assisting each application. The standard procedures can be found on the official CSCSE website: https://zwfw.cscse.edu.cn/ .

Does MADHGA offer scholarships?

MADHGA offers at least four scholarships under two categories: Best Student Award and Best Capstone Project Award . Both are open to all students.

Will late applications be considered?

Applications submitted after the deadline will be considered on a space-available basis. Applicants are advised to apply early.

Am I expected to have knowledge in history and/or digital humanities as a prerequisite?

No. MADHGA welcomes passionate students with different levels of knowledge and from diverse academic backgrounds. The programme is designed to help students acquire new knowledge while further sharpening their existing skills.

Apart from digital approaches to history, will I also learn about AI?

While AI is seamlessly integrated into all digital modules of our courses, we believe that without a solid foundation in historiography and critical digital approaches, the use of AI could be deleterious. We also have a dedicated course to critical AI which covers in-depth understanding and cutting-edge hands-on training.

I need more information. Who should I reach out to?

You are welcome to contact our Programme Director, Prof Vincent Leung , at [email protected] , and/or our programme administration team at [email protected] .

programmeName: Master of Arts in Translation Studies
summary: The MATS Programme is a Master’s programme in Hong Kong with a special focus on translation from Chinese to English. It is tailored for those who are proficient in Chinese and English and interested in the study of translation practice and translation research. The MATS Programme is distinctive in its global outlook and international team, with faculty members coming from Canada and across the Greater China region - including the Chinese mainland and Hong Kong SAR, giving students’ learning a significant edge. The Programme features small-class teaching to ensure plenty of interaction between students and lecturers. We also offer one-to-one supervision for translation projects and dissertation writing, which helps to train students in independent translation and research, and allows for more in-depth discussion with the supervisors.

Applicants must also demonstrate competency in spoken English through an oral interview conducted by the programme and/or obtain a grade B or above in the CET-6 Speaking English Test.
Additional Admission Requirements Applicants may be invited to attend a face-to-face or video interview when it is deemed necessary.
Upon meeting its entry requirements, MATS students would have the opportunity to complete two master’s degrees in two years.
learningObjectives: The broad aim of the programme is to provide, for students who are fairly competent in Chinese and English, a challenging bicultural education and vigorous academic training in translation and in research.
The learning outcomes expected at the completion of the programme are that students should be able to:
1. Communicate competently in Chinese and English for academic and professional purposes;
2. Perform translation independently or in a supervisory position in different milieus in Greater China: Hong Kong, mainland China, and Taiwan;
3. Reflect critically on the socio-cultural factors that affect the production and reception of texts in general and translated texts in particular;
4. Demonstrate an adequate understanding of the historical and current issues in translation practice and translation theory; and
5. Conduct independent research in translation studies and related fields.
