Admission

Entry Requirements

To be eligible for entry into this programme, candidates should have:
hold a relevant bachelor’s degree awarded by a tertiary education institution recognised for this purpose by the University (applicants with a non-psychology first degree have to complete a pre-entry course); or have obtained an equivalent qualification; or have provided satisfactory evidence of academic and professional attainment., which will be considered on a case-by-case basis; and

An applicant whose degree or, exceptionally, an alternative qualification deemed equivalent f rom a tertiary institution is not using English as the medium of instruction (MOI) for the programme in Hong Kong or an English-speaking country should fulfill the following minimum English proficiency requirement:
a minimum score of 79 in TOEFL Internet-Based Test 1 ; or

a band score of 6.0 or above in IELTS 1 ; or

a minimum score of 450 or above in College English Test Band 6 (CET-6), with proof of competency in spoken English 2 ; or

an equivalent score in a recognised test; or

an equivalent qualification to prove his/her English language proficiency, which will be considered on a case-by-case basis.

1 For IELTS and TOEFL qualifications for admission purposes, the University accepts only results from a single test sitting . The test must be conducted at a recognised test centre and fall within the two-year validity period, counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort.

2 For CET-6 results submitted for admission purposes, the University accepts only those obtained within the five-year validity period , counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort. Applicants must also demonstrate competency in spoken English through an oral interview conducted by the Programme and/or obtain grade B or above in the CET-6 Speaking English Test.
* An Interview will be conducted if applicant is shortlisted. And applicants are required to fulfill additional requirements, if any, set by the programme.
Details please find at: https://www.ln.edu.hk/sgs/for-prospective-students/taught-postgraduate-programmes/admission-information
Important Notes:

For academic qualification obtained in Mainland China. You are required to provide the online verification report of your bachelor degree certificate from the following website: https://xwrz.chsi.com.cn/gateway . You are required to submit the verification report in your online application.
Any false, unclear or misleading information and/or document(s) provided by applicants in their application will disqualify their applications or revoke their admission offers, and any fees paid, including down payment or deposit, will be forfeited. According to the laws of Hong Kong, forgery is a serious criminal offence that a convicted offender may be liable to imprisonment for fourteen years.
Supporting Documents:
Applicants should scan and submit the following documents to the Online Application System for evaluation of their applications:
Identity documents (e.g. Identity card*, passport or other travel document)

Official Transcript (for academic qualifications of undergraduate and/or postgraduate studies):

An official final transcript for a completed programme

The latest transcript if the programme is not yet completed

An official English-translated copy must be attached if the transcript is not in English. The interpretation of grades (in English) is also required.

Degree Certificate/Graduation Certificate/Proof of Study (as appropriate)

Verification proof by third-party authorities (for a completed programme):
Online Verification Report of Higher Education Degree Certificate (中國高等教育學位在綫驗証報告), both Chinese and English versions, issued by the CHSI (中國高等教育學生信息網) for qualification(s) awarded by universities in the Chinese Mainland

Online Query of Overseas Credentials Evaluation Reports國(境)外學歷學位認証書 issued by the Chinese Service Center for Scholarly Exchange (CSCSE) (教育部留學服務中心) (applicable to applicants from the Chinese Mainland with qualifications awarded by overseas universities)

Proof of English language proficiency, e.g. IETLS, TOEFL, CET-6 Score Report (applicable to applicants whose degree, or exceptionally, an alternative qualification deemed equivalent, was not awarded by a tertiary institution where English was used as the MOI for the degree programme)

Personal statement

Reference letters (if any)

*Do not upload copy of HKID. Local students will be required to present HKID for verification upon registration.

Applicants may be required to submit a Report for Qualifications Assessment issued by the Hong Kong Council for Accreditation of Academic & Vocational Qualifications (HKCAAVQ) to support their application.

No Dishonest and Bribery Practices
Any false or misleading information and/or document(s) provided in the application, or any dishonest conduct observed during the application process, e.g. plagiarism, employing surrogate examination-taker, etc., will result in disqualification of application for admission, revocation of admission offer or termination of studies at the University if admitted, and any fees paid will be forfeited.

Falsification of documents or the use of falsified documents is a very serious offence and is absolutely not tolerated by the University. According to the laws of Hong Kong, forgery is a serious criminal offence that a convicted offender may be liable to imprisonment for a maximum of 14 years.

Applicants or their family members and agents must not offer advantages to any staff or members of the University in relation to their admission applications or while having dealings of any kind with the University. These offers may constitute an offence under the Prevention of Bribery Ordinance (Cap.201) in Hong Kong. The University also prohibits its staff and members from soliciting or accepting such advantages.

Application Method

Application Fee
HK$ 400 (Non-refundable)

Online Application
The system is now opening for 2026/27 September intake. The application deadline is 15 June 2026 or fully occupied, whichever is earlier.
Apply Now
(You are advised to hand in the application as soon as possible, especially for those applicants who need a VISA and/or need to attend Pre-entry Course)
If there is any tuition fee changed and/or admission requirements changed, you have to comply with the university and the programme regulations.
If you miss the application deadline, your application will be considered as next intake. We usually offer two intakes per year, September and January.
The applications would be considered periodically before application deadline.

Home

Department of Psychology

Master of Social Sciences in Applied Psychology

Home

Home

Master of Social Sciences in Applied Psychology (MAP)


Introduction


In response to these developments, Lingnan University's Department of Psychology will launch its new Master of Social Sciences in Applied Psychology (MAP) programme in September 2022.


The MAP will not only provide students with a broad foundation in the practical applications of psychology, but also enable them to lay the foundations for a more specific career, as they will have the option to pursue a Concentration in Counselling Psychology.


As Professor Padmore Adusei Amoah of Lingnan's Department of Psychology explains, this is a conversion programme. "We can accept students from diverse backgrounds, with or without experience in psychology."


MAP graduates can go on to enrol in professional programmes, such as those in clinical, educational, and industrial and organisational, psychology, as well as in counselling. Alternatively, they can apply to doctoral research programmes. They will also be eligible to join the Hong Kong Psychological Society (HKPS).





A chance to pursue personal interests


In this one year full-time or two years part-time, programme, a range of elective courses enable students to pursue their own specific interests. A number of these courses will cover topics not usually available on postgraduate psychology programmes in Hong Kong, such as sleep and health, positive gerontology, and consumer psychology. Furthermore, MAP students will get the chance to conduct their own research with the support of a supervisor.


As on many other Lingnan programmes, MAP faculty will draw on an international perspective to highlight with is happening globally in this field.


For students wishing to begin training for a specific career, the options open to them include a Concentration in Counselling Psychology. Despite the huge and growing demand for counsellors and clinical psychologists on the Mainland, very few universities in China offer relevant degrees, either at undergraduate or postgraduate levels. This type of expertise is certain to be increasingly sought after in the wider region, as well.





Learn more about MAP programme


MAP Poster


MAP Pamphlet

programmeName: Master of Social Sciences in Comparative Social Policy (International)
summary: The IMCSP programme r esponds to the growing demand for policy professionals who can analyse, compare, and design social policies across diverse societies. It is the only taught Master’s programme in Hong Kong that places systematic international comparison at the heart of social policy, development, and governance.
suitableBackgrounds: >Learn More >Learn More >Learn More Mr TSANG King Hin (IMCSP Graduate 2024/25, Awardee of Dr.

James Tak Wu Awards for Outstanding Service) "Throughout my IMCSP journey, I have gained invaluable experiences through the training on research projects, all of which have significantly enhanced my scientific research literacy." Ms MAI TAM AUNG SEINN (IMCSP Graduate 2024/25) "Choosing the IMCSP was one of my best decisions.

This program is truly 'more than a degree'; it's a toolkit for future policymakers." Ms Wang Geyu (IMCSP Graduate 2023/24) "The IMCSP programme provided me with invaluable academic grounding and inspiration." Mr.

It is the only taught Master programme in Hong Kong, making international comparisons the focal point of studying social policy, development and governance.

Three reasons to choose the IMCSP Programme As a student at one of Asia’s rising research-focused liberal arts universities for the digital age, you will develop comparative knowledge, analytical expertise, and professional networks to build or enhance your career in government, NGOs, social enterprises, think tanks, or the private sector in Hong Kong, the Greater Bay Area, Mainland China, and internationally.

You will explore social policy across core domains: Ageing and social security Children and family support Health and well-being Housing and urban governance Employment and lifelong learning Environmental change and climate adaptation Through structured comparison, you will learn not only what works, but why policies succeed or fail across different political and cultural systems.

IMCSP offers two concentrations — Social Innovation and Social Research — allowing students to tailor their studies to their career goals: lead social change and design innovative solutions, or develop advanced research expertise for policy analysis and doctoral study.

Career Development Personalised and Practical IMCSP graduates pursue careers in government, NGOs, social enterprises, think tanks, financial services, insurance, and education across Hong Kong, Mainland China, and internationally.

You will benefit from: Personalised career coaching CV and application workshops Alumni networking events Targeted guidance for civil service or NGO recruitment Structured support for MPhil/PhD applications Work placements in ongoing research projects Whether you aim for public service, social sector leadership, or doctoral study, we provide tailored guidance to help you achieve your goals.
There are 20+ professions in the public, private and related third sectors open to you in Hong Kong and elsewhere.

admissionNotes: To be eligible for entry into this program, candidates should have: A bachelor's degree from a recognised tertiary institution; or equivalent qualifications and experience, which will be considered on a case-by-case basis; and An applicant whose degree or, exceptionally, an alternative qualification deemed equivalent from a tertiary institution is not using English as the medium of instruction (MOI) for the programme should obtain: a minimum score of 79 in TOEFL Internet-Based Test 1 ; or a band score of 6.0 or above in IELTS 1 ; or a minimum score of 450 or above in College English Test Band 6 (CET-6), with proof of competency in spoken English 2 ; or an equivalent score in a recognized test; or an equivalent qualification to prove his/her English language proficiency, which will be considered on a case-by-case basis. 1 For IELTS and TOEFL qualifications for admission purpose, the University only accepts results of tests that are conducted at a test centre and within the validity period, viz. 2 years, from the time of submission of an admission application for the specific intake cohort. 2 For CET-6 results submitted for admission purposes, the University accepts only those obtained within the five-year validity period, counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort. Applicants must also demonstrate competency in spoken English through an oral interview conducted by the Program and/or obtain grade B or above in the CET-6 Speaking English Test.

The IMCSP programme r esponds to the growing demand for policy professionals who can analyse, compare, and design social policies across diverse societies. It is the only taught Master’s programme in Hong Kong that places systematic international comparison at the heart of social policy, development, and governance.

The programme is offered by Lingnan University’s Department of Sociology and Social Policy, which is home to one of Asia’s leading Social Policy & Administration programmes (QS World University Rankings by Subject 2025: 14th in Asia; 3rd in Hong Kong). Lingnan also ranks among the top 100 universities in Asia (THE Asia University Rankings 2026), reflecting its growing international standing.

Programme Overview

Course Descriptions

Explore More

Programme Overview

Programme Overview
Programme Structure
Course Descriptions

Course Descriptions

SOC501

Social Policy Analysis: Comparative Perspectives
This course explores the field of policy analysis with a particular focus on how policy is formulated and delivered in different institutional contexts.
Download syllabus

SOC 504

Comparative Social Policy in Greater China and East Asia
In this course, you will critically examine how the government, NGOs, and private enterprises respond to significant social challenges.
Download syllabus

SOC 505

Globalisation, Policy and Society
This course provides students with an introduction to debates on globalisation and its consequences for social policy, well-being and contemporary social divisions.
Download syllabus

SOC 506

Policy Research and Evaluation Design
This course introduces you to the role of research in evidence-based policy and practice.
Download syllabus

SOC 601

Governance, Management and Delivery
This course is designed to explore the nature of governance, management, and delivery in different comparative contexts.
Download syllabus

SOC 602

Dialogue with Policy Practitioners: Theory and Practice
In this course, you will engage in field visits to government offices, NGOs, and policy institutes in Hong Kong.
Download syllabus

SOC 604

Comparative Social Policy Symposium
This course will enable you to interact with leading international researchers in an academic conference setting.
Download syllabus

SOC 605

Comparative Social Policy Research Project
In this course, you will demonstrate your analytical ability through the completion of an individually supervised research topic of your own choice.
Download syllabus

SOC 606

Comparative Social Indicators and Research Methods
In this course, you will be introduced to major social indicators and gain the opportunity to explore them through ‘hands-on' data exercises.
Download syllabus

SOC 607

Applied Survey Methodology
This course introduces you to principles in survey methodology applied to social policies.
Download syllabus

SOC 608

Qualitative Methods for Policy Analysis
This course offers you an in-depth exploration of the principles and applications of qualitative research methods to social policy.
Download syllabus

SOC 609

Social Entrepreneurship & Social Impact
In this course, you will be given a comprehensive understanding of social entrepreneurship as an innovative solution for addressing pressing social issues.
Download syllabus

SOC 610

Non-Profit Organisations & the Voluntary Sector
In this course, you will be equipped with knowledge of the role and management of non-profit organisations and the voluntary sector from a comparative perspective.
Download syllabus

Comparative Social Policy Research Project
Once you completed all coursework, you will work on your capstone project with the help of an expert faculty supervisor. This project will enable you to produce a substantial research paper of typically around 8,000-10,000 words in length that reflects a deep understanding of a topic entirely of your own choice. Due to the individual nature of students' research interests, the subjects studied vary considerably, but projects may ask, for example:

How is people's experience of poverty and material deprivation affected by welfare policies in the Southern Chinese Greater Bay Area?
Is the public support for older adults in Hong Kong adequate and can policymakers learn any lessons from European societies?
What makes a sound housing policy in contemporary Asian societies?
Are public policies to increase health care coverage in low-income countries effective?
How do higher education policies in different societies influence young people's school-to-work transition?
What are the factors impacting the work-life balance of families with children in different societies?
Do conditional cash transfers in South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa work?

A series containing the student works produced by individually supervised and executed research projects from the course SOC 605 Comparative Social Policy Research Project can be accessed here .

Programme Structure

The IMCSP programme is a one-year full-time or two-year part-time taught Masters programme.

As an IMCSP student you will graduate upon the completion of 30 credits .

Programme Curriculum (2025-26 Intake and onwards)

Starting from the 2025-26 intake, the IMCSP students (who do not specialise in any concentrations) are required to complete:
5 core courses (15 credits), 1 required course (3 credits), 2 elective courses (6 credits) and 1 capstone project (6 credits)

For students specialising in the Social Research (SoR) or Social Innovation (SoI) concentration, they are required to complete:
5 core courses (15 credits), 3 required courses (9 credits) and 1 capstone project (6 credits)

In accordance with the University's academic regulations, students must complete a total of nine courses, including one capstone project, amounting to 30 credits, and obtain a Cumulative G.P.A. of 2.67 or above for graduation.

Programme Overview

In today’s globalised world, studying the experiences of different societies is critical to understand the social issues that many of us face. The MSocSci in Comparative Social Policy (International) (IMCSP) programme meets the demand for individuals with relevant comparative knowledge in government, think tanks, NGOs, social enterprises, and the broader public at large. It is the only taught Master programme in Hong Kong, making international comparisons the focal point of studying social policy, development and governance.

Three reasons to choose the IMCSP Programme As a student at one of Asia’s rising research-focused liberal arts universities for the digital age, you will develop comparative knowledge, analytical expertise, and professional networks to build or enhance your career in government, NGOs, social enterprises, think tanks, or the private sector in Hong Kong, the Greater Bay Area, Mainland China, and internationally.
1. Comparative Focus
With a Choice of Concentration
Comparative Focus – Understanding Social Policy Across Societies
Learn to analyse how different societies address shared challenges from ageing populations and housing affordability to healthcare reform and labour market transformation.
You will explore social policy across core domains:
Ageing and social security
Children and family support
Health and well-being
Housing and urban governance
Employment and lifelong learning
Environmental change and climate adaptation

Through structured comparison, you will learn not only what works, but why policies succeed or fail across different political and cultural systems. IMCSP offers two concentrations — Social Innovation and Social Research — allowing students to tailor their studies to their career goals: lead social change and design innovative solutions, or develop advanced research expertise for policy analysis and doctoral study.

2. International Outlook
Applied, Not Just Theoretical
Learn where policy happens.
IMCSP takes you beyond the classroom through structured engagement with:
Government bureaux and policy units
Legislative Council sessions
NGOs and social enterprises
Policy institutes in Hong Kong and the Greater Bay Area

You will participate in:
International Social Policy Symposium (SOC604) – dialogue with leading global scholars
Dialogue With GBA Practitioners (SOC603) – comparative governance in the Greater Bay Area

3. Career Development
Personalised and Practical
IMCSP graduates pursue careers in government, NGOs, social enterprises, think tanks, financial services, insurance, and education across Hong Kong, Mainland China, and internationally.
You will benefit from:
Personalised career coaching
CV and application workshops
Alumni networking events
Targeted guidance for civil service or NGO recruitment
Structured support for MPhil/PhD applications
Work placements in ongoing research projects

Whether you aim for public service, social sector leadership, or doctoral study, we provide tailored guidance to help you achieve your goals.

Career Prospect

Default career destinations
As an IMCSP graduate, you have a wide choice of career options. There are 20+ professions in the public, private and related third sectors open to you in Hong Kong and elsewhere.

The IMCSP programme will train you to become a social policy researcher possibly employed by the government as a civil servant, a think tank, a local or international NGO, a political party, or a university research centre.

After completing the IMCSP programme, your acquired expertise will enable you to become a journalist, a film producer or a school teacher. Careers in social work or further study in an MPhil or PhD programme are also high-quality options.

Meeting some of our IMCSP alumni:

“As an IMCSP graduate, I have quickly gained graduate employment in a diversified financial and immigration group and currently share my working time between Hong Kong and Shenzhen. My job duties cover MPF pensions and cross-border assets, together with financial identity planning to support individual customers. The IMCSP programme endowed me with a global perspective, which opened up many career opportunities in the financial and education industries for me.”

Ms LI Yuanjia, IMCSP Graduate 2021

“I am passionate about everything related to “education”. At Lingnan University, the IMCSP Programme painstakingly helped me define this passion via all the courses offered. I truly enjoyed them all as they exposed me to the nitty-gritty of education policy in East Asia and worldwide. As a result, I quickly grasped the tools necessary to become an outstanding policy researcher and maker in the field of education. I graduated as one of the best students in my cohort, and my qualification, coupled with the writing and presentation skills I acquired, landed me my current job in Hong Kong. I have now become a registered educator with strong values. Thanks to IMCSP, I can now prepare to pursue both my short and long-term goals as I move on in life.”

Mr OWOYEMI Dare Seun, IMCSP Graduate 2020

"Currently, I work in the insurance industry, and what I learned in the IMCSP programme is very helpful for my work indeed. To some extent, it was the study of my IMCSP capstone project that made me determined to join this profession. Many people think that insurance is mainly a sales job. Yet, my lessons on elderly care policy and the health policy in the IMCSP Programme made me understand the advantages and disadvantages of social policies in different regions. Now, I care about my clients and always think about how I can use commercial insurance to make up for the deficiencies of social policy in their daily lives. I believe my background in social policy makes me a different professional than my peers in this competitive industry."

Ms LIN Xin, IMSCP Graduate 2019

"Thinking back to my time on the IMCSP Programme, there are so many good memories for me. The programme provided all kinds of different learning opportunities. For me personally, the guest lectures and field visits were particularly valuable because they introduced me to the work of NGOs. This knowledge prepared me to provide professional advice on social issues and policy problems to my employer. I am still very thankful to all the professors on the IMCSP Programme."

Ms CHEN Huimin, IMSCP Graduate 2018

Professions in sectors related to your focused Social Policy
The knowledge and insights you develop on the IMCSP programme will give you an advantage in industries directly related to your concentrated policy area. The five critical areas of interest offered by the IMCSP programme and the corresponding employment sectors are:

1. EMPLOYMENT
HR consulting firms, outsourcing firms, etc.

2. EDUCATION
Universities, community colleges, secondary and primary schools, special needs schools, etc.

3. HOUSING
Property developers, contractors, property management agencies, etc.

4. SOCIAL SECURITY
Nursing homes, medical services providers, etc.

5. HEALTH
Public and private hospitals, clinics, speech clinics, Chinese medicine practitioners, etc.

Employment opportunities in professions among these sectors also include public relations (PR), insurance, market research, marketing communications, sales support, human resources (HR), employee communication, etc.

Professions open to all Social Sciences majors including IMCSP
Besides occupations directly related to social policy, you will also be qualified to apply for those professions open to all Social Science majors: management consultant, MTs of a property developer, hotel management, retailer, merchandisers, etc.

Exclusive career support for IMCSP students
The IMCSP programme provides personalised career support to help you win the job you desire. This career support includes:
Background information on the job nature and outlook of all the professions as mentioned above
Details of the corresponding employers for these professions in Hong Kong
Support in compiling a compelling CV and cover letter
Arrangements for you to proactively approach and network with your potential future employer

---

Source: https://www.ln.edu.hk/socsp/imcsp/admission
Home

Department of Sociology & Social Policy

Master of Social Sciences in Comparative Social Policy (International)

Admission Information

Admission

The IMCSP programme is primarily designed for:
Graduates with degrees in social policy, international development, sociology or other social sciences

Professionals whose work is directly related to social policy analysis, design, advocacy and delivery

Graduates and professionals from other backgrounds and who seek to join a career in social policy analysis, design, advocacy, and delivery

Admission Requirements

To be eligible for entry into this program, candidates should have:

A bachelor's degree from a recognised tertiary institution; or equivalent qualifications and experience, which will be considered on a case-by-case basis; and

An applicant whose degree or, exceptionally, an alternative qualification deemed equivalent from a tertiary institution is not using English as the medium of instruction (MOI) for the programme should obtain:

a minimum score of 79 in TOEFL Internet-Based Test 1 ; or

a band score of 6.0 or above in IELTS 1 ; or

a minimum score of 450 or above in College English Test Band 6 (CET-6), with proof of competency in spoken English 2 ; or
an equivalent score in a recognized test; or
an equivalent qualification to prove his/her English language proficiency, which will be considered on a case-by-case basis.

1 For IELTS and TOEFL qualifications for admission purpose, the University only accepts results of tests that are conducted at a test centre and within the validity period, viz. 2 years, from the time of submission of an admission application for the specific intake cohort.

2 For CET-6 results submitted for admission purposes, the University accepts only those obtained within the five-year validity period, counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort. Applicants must also demonstrate competency in spoken English through an oral interview conducted by the Program and/or obtain grade B or above in the CET-6 Speaking English Test.

How to apply

To apply to the IMCSP programme, you are required to submit the following application materials through the online application system:

A completed application form
A personal statement of no more than two pages A4
Two recommendation letters (in a sealed envelope addressed to the IMCSP programme office)
Copy of academic certificate(s)
Copy of academic transcript(s)
Copy of English language proficiency result(s)

The IMCSP Programme Office will conduct interviews with all applicants before making a formal offer for admission. Applicants will enjoy admission preference if they previously studied relevant Social Sciences courses or possess work experience in social policy formulation, execution or analysis.

Apply Now

Programme Fees and Expenses

The IMCSP programme tuition fee from AY2026-27 is HK$160,000 .

An application fee of HK$400 will be charged upon completion of an online application. The application fee is non-refundable and non-referable.

You will also be expected to take care of additional expenses including accommodation, travel to and from Hong Kong, a graduation deposit (HK$600) and Hong Kong Immigration visa charges ( HK$430 , if applicable). For a useful summary of things to consider when coming to Hong Kong for postgraduate study, you may consult: http://www.studyinhongkong.edu.hk/en/ .

Application Period

For 2026-27 Intake: 1 Oct 2025 – 15 June 2026 (Non-local), 1 Oct 2025 – 31 July 2026 (Local)

Prestigious Fellowship Scheme

Following the positive experience with the UGC’s Targeted Taught Postgraduate Programmes Fellowships Scheme, the MSocSci in Comparative Social Policy (International) is delighted to announce the continuation of its ‘ IMCSP Prestigious Fellowship Scheme ’ for another three cohorts of students, from the AY2025/26 to AY2027/28 intake.

Building on the success of the initial three-year period (AY2022-23 to AY2024-25), the Scheme has been renewed and expanded to include both local and international students . This initiative reflects our commitment to supporting outstanding students who aspire to make meaningful contributions to the field of comparative and international social policy.

The IMCSP Prestigious Fellowship Scheme is designed to financially support the most deserving students who wish to pursue advanced studies in the critical areas of social policy, including old-age pensions, welfare policy, public support for children and families, work and employment, and life-long learning. Students interested in deepening their understanding in the related areas of health, well-being, social care, housing, and urban planning are also welcome to apply.

Number of Fellowships in Each Cohort

This scheme is expected to provide a maximum of three fellowships in each cohort. The next deadline for applications is 30 June 2025 for August/September 2025 entry .

Amount of Fellowship

Fellowship students will be granted a 100% tuition fee waiver . However, an application fee of HK$400 will be payable upon completing an online application. The application fee is non-refundable and non-referable.

Criteria for Selection

The IMCSP Prestigious Fellowship Scheme will adopt a merit-based approach in selecting the most deserving students to enter the IMCSP programme. In awarding individual fellowships, particular attention will be given to applicants’ previous academic achievements and explanation in their personal statementon how they plan to use the IMCSP programme to build their career in government, think tanks, NGOs, social enterprises, or to benefit the broader Hong Kong public at large. Applicants’ relevant working or volunteering experience will be a distinct advantage.

Applications for the IMCSP Prestigious Fellowship Scheme are open to local Hong Kong students as defined by the Education Bureau and international students from countries eligible for receiving Official Development Assistance (ODA) . However, both full-time and part-time students are invited to apply.

programmeName: Master of Social Sciences in Organisational Psychology and Education Management
summary: Master of Social Sciences in Organisational Psychology and Education Management (OPEM)
suitableBackgrounds: OPEM graduates could follow a range of careers in education policy and administration in schools, tertiary institutes, government, NGOs, think tanks, as well as in the field of human resource management.

Building upon the strong research in organisational psychology and education management in Lingnan University, students' learning will be enhanced through active participation in the research activities organised by School of Graduate Studies, Department of Psychology, and different research centres.
It covers major clinical issues (e.g., mood disorder and anxiety disorder) and daily challenges in school (e.g., academic stress and bullying) and digital world (e.g., online gaming disorder).

Education Management Cluster (At least any 9 credits) MOP601 Regional Study on Education Management in Greater China and East Asia (3 credits) In collaboration with major universities in Taiwan, Mainland China, Macau, and East Asia, special lectures and class discussions, professional visits, and cultural tours will be organised for students.

This course is designed for postgraduate (PG) students in the university with recent or current international experience (study abroad, exchange program, immersion program, internships, or educational visit).
Intermediate Award Students will be awarded a Postgraduate Diploma in Organisational Psychology and Education Management if they complete at least five courses with a minimum cumulative GPA of 2.33.
Degree Classification Minimum Cumulative G.P.A Distinction 3.50 Pass 2.67 These classifications are given according to the student's Cumulative G.P.A.

is below 2.50 at the academic review will be put on academic probation in the following term (unless his/her studies shall be discontinued as prescribed by Regulation 17- Regulation Governing Taught Doctoral/Master's Degree Programmes (effective from 2026-27 intake) .
learningObjectives: MOP609 Innovative Pedagogy: Frameworks & Application (3 credits)

Innovative pedagogy represents a shift from traditional teacher-centered approaches to student-centered, dynamic, and context-aware teaching practices. This course explores the theoretical foundations, design principles, and practical applications of innovative pedagogical models such as project-based learning, flipped classroom, inquiry-based learning, game-based learning, and technology-enhanced teaching. These innovative learning methods enable students to apply different innovative pedagogical models to solve real-world educational challenges.
MIH601 Internationalising Education: Institutional Strategy and Development (3 credits)

In this module you will explore the origins and development of international higher education with particular focus on current drivers and practices. Drivers include economic, political, cultural, and academic pressures, while the practices covered will include transnational campuses, international collaborations in teaching and research, the recruitment of overseas students and staff, and the use of new distance learning technologies. These will be illustrated through specific examples in different contexts, making use of both the literature and invited speakers.
MIH611 Generative AI in Education: Applications and Challenges (3 credits)

This course explores the application, integration, and challenges of Generative AI (GAI) in both school education and higher education. It aims to equip students with the knowledge and skills to effectively integrate GAI into teaching, learning, and assessment processes. The course covers contemporary strategies, techniques, ethical considerations, and practical challenges associated with GAI in education.
CRS512 Evolution of Early Childhood Education in China and the World (3 credits)
Source: https://www.ln.edu.hk/psy/opem/programme-overview/programme-structure

Students will have access to a wide range of sector-specific expertise across the University, including Education Policy and Management, Organisational Psychology, Globalisation and International Development, as well as Comparative and International Social Policy.
Students will learn about the dynamics between individuals and groups, and how these dynamics affect individuals’ behaviors in organizational contexts.

MOP608 Leadership in Educational Management (3 credits) This course is designed to equip students with the theoretical foundations, applied skills, and practical techniques necessary to assume leadership roles in educational administration.

Psychology Cluster (At least any 6 credits) MOP607 Youth Mental Health: Developmental Risk and Resilience (3 credits) This course aims to enhance students' scientific understanding of youth mental health issues, integrating theories and studies from developmental psychopathology, clinical disorders, and contemporary societal challenges for youth.
The course will equip students with knowledge and tools to promote resilience (minimize risks) for youth development and learning.

This course will require that students have some knowledge of statistics and quantitative methods prior to taking this course.
careerDirections: OPEM graduates could follow a range of careers in education policy and administration in schools, tertiary institutes, government, NGOs, think tanks, as well as in the field of human resource management.
Students will also be introduced to professional practices of educational psychologists.
This course is suitable for those who are interested in a career in education or understanding how to become more efficient learners.
Various issues faced by employees that hinder their professional and personal growth are also examined to promote performance, well-being, teamwork, and career development in work settings.

The course is organized around three domains relevant for psychological testing and assessment: (1) principles of psychological testing; (2) application of psychological assessments in various contexts, particularly in clinical settings; and (3) professional issues related to psychological assessment.

Some of the topics to be covered include reviewing one’s childhood and adolescence, body and wellness, career development, stress, coping and mindfulness, love and relationships, meaning and values, death and loss, and finding happiness.

Education Management Cluster (At least any 9 credits) MOP601 Regional Study on Education Management in Greater China and East Asia (3 credits) In collaboration with major universities in Taiwan, Mainland China, Macau, and East Asia, special lectures and class discussions, professional visits, and cultural tours will be organised for students.
With academic guidance of the Program Director, students may identify an area of professional/academic interest for taking the guided study.

Source: https://www.ln.edu.hk/psy/opem
Master of Social Sciences in Organisational Psychology and Education Management (OPEM)

Master of Social Sciences in
Organisational Psychology and Education Management

The Master of Social Sciences in Organisational Psychology and Education Management (組織心理學與教育管理社會科學碩士, OPEM, hereafter) is a one-year full-time or two-year part-time programme designed for students who want to advance their careers by acquiring in-depth knowledge in organisational psychology and education management. The OPEM Programme would appeal to local students and those from the region and beyond, who currently or aspire to assume leadership and policy-making roles in the education sector. OPEM graduates could follow a range of careers in education policy and administration in schools, tertiary institutes, government, NGOs, think tanks, as well as in the field of human resource management.

The OPEM programme offers students a rich experiential learning environment through interacting with leading scholars in the education management and applied psychology fields. Students will have access to a wide range of sector-specific expertise across the University, including Education Policy and Management, Organisational Psychology, Globalisation and International Development, as well as Comparative and International Social Policy. The Programme enables students to analyse policy and management issues from organisational, psychological, and international perspectives.

The OPEM Programme is an inter-disciplinary programme. It combines advanced training in education management and organisational psychology with a capstone project based upon students selected organizations. The Programme provides opportunities for students to interact with practitioners from education and psychology sectors to reflect upon management issues. Building upon the strong research in organisational psychology and education management in Lingnan University, students' learning will be enhanced through active participation in the research activities organised by School of Graduate Studies, Department of Psychology, and different research centres. The OPEM Programme consists of 30 credits which normally takes 1 year of full-time or 2 years of part-time studies to complete. The Programme provides core courses, capstone project, and conferences at our esteemed regional and international partner organisations.

Course Description

Course Description

A. Required Courses (4 compulsory courses, a total of 12 credits)

PSY513 Organisational Psychology (3 credits)
This course introduces students to the field of organizational psychology, which is the study of human behavior in groups and organizations. Students will learn about the dynamics between individuals and groups, and how these dynamics affect individuals’ behaviors in organizational contexts. Students will build their knowledge by learning the principles underlying the analysis and understanding of human behaviors in organizations.

PSY515 Research Methods in Work Psychology (3 credits)
This course provides an overview of advanced research methods used in industrial and organisational psychology, including research ethics. Students will learn to determine whether the data investigation approaches are the best ones for the research purpose. Tips on writing research articles are also discussed. Students will have hands-on practice in actual data analyses using statistical software and in research report writing. This course may also include extensive fieldwork experience at a selected site in the local community.

PSY619 Educational Psychology: Theory and Practice (3 credits)
This course is designed to introduce students to psychological theories and principles as applied to learning and teaching. Topics include student development, learning theories from both behavioural and cognitive traditions, effective teaching methods and practices, learners’ individual differences, achievement motivation, and classroom assessment. Students will also be introduced to professional practices of educational psychologists. Students are encouraged to reflect on their own learning and teaching by integrating what they learn in this course with their real-life experience. This course is suitable for those who are interested in a career in education or understanding how to become more efficient learners.

MOP608 Leadership in Educational Management (3 credits)

This course is designed to equip students with the theoretical foundations, applied skills, and practical techniques necessary to assume leadership roles in educational administration. Students will examine the pivotal role of leadership within educational institutions and explore how both classical and contemporary leadership theories can be applied to address the complex challenges faced by schools, colleges, and universities today.

B. Psychology Cluster (At least any 6 credits)

MOP607 Youth Mental Health: Developmental Risk and Resilience (3 credits)

This course aims to enhance students' scientific understanding of youth mental health issues, integrating theories and studies from developmental psychopathology, clinical disorders, and contemporary societal challenges for youth. It covers major clinical issues (e.g., mood disorder and anxiety disorder) and daily challenges in school (e.g., academic stress and bullying) and digital world (e.g., online gaming disorder). Students will critically analyze the biological, psychological, and social factors that influence mental health in children and adolescents, with emphasis on both risk and resilience factors from individuals, family, school, and broader sociocultural contexts. The course will equip students with knowledge and tools to promote resilience (minimize risks) for youth development and learning.

PSY605 Coaching and Counselling in the Workplace (3 credits ）
This course is intended as an introduction to the basic concepts and issues of workplace coaching and counselling. Various issues faced by employees that hinder their professional and personal growth are also examined to promote performance, well-being, teamwork, and career development in work settings.
Course highlights:
1. Evidence-based practice of organisational psychology
2. Development of critical thinking and reflexivity
3. Collaborative learning

PSY613 Principles of Psychological Assessment (3 credits ）
This course will provide an overview of basic issues involved in the field of psychological testing, as well as examine the application of various psychological assessments. The course is organized around three domains relevant for psychological testing and assessment: (1) principles of psychological testing; (2) application of psychological assessments in various contexts, particularly in clinical settings; and (3) professional issues related to psychological assessment. This course will require that students have some knowledge of statistics and quantitative methods prior to taking this course.

PSY618 Personal Growth (3 credits ）
This course aims to promote among students an interest and readiness for taking charge of their personal growth. It draws attention to the significance of mental and physical health, and the pathway to living a more meaningful and fulfilling life. Some of the topics to be covered include reviewing one’s childhood and adolescence, body and wellness, career development, stress, coping and mindfulness, love and relationships, meaning and values, death and loss, and finding happiness. Special emphasis will be placed on helping students identify some of the significant influences of personal growth and recognizing options to optimize one’s development. Current trends and issues in personal growth, such as sleep hygiene, wellness through exercise and healthy diets, and setting digital boundaries, will also be discussed.

PSY514 Occupational Health Psychology (3 credits ）
This course will provide students with an introduction to theories and practices in occupational health psychology. The important dynamic relationship between work and the workers is highlighted. This course offers a platform for an impressive blend of research, discussion, and training in promoting workers’ health and well-being, thereby enhancing the experience and consequences of work itself.

C. Education Management Cluster (At least any 9 credits)

MOP601 Regional Study on Education Management in Greater China and East Asia (3 credits)
In collaboration with major universities in Taiwan, Mainland China, Macau, and East Asia, special lectures and class discussions, professional visits, and cultural tours will be organised for students. In selected Asian societies, students will be engaged in field observations and field visits to develop a more critical understanding of education management and governance through their active participation in and personal experience and critical reflection. More specifically, this course is an intensive and experiential unit, which provides a platform for students to study beyond the classroom. Through observing different higher education institutions, student learning experience will be enhanced through theory-field experience integration and reflection.

MOP602 Applied Education Management Project (Term 1 & Term 2，6 credits)
This course aims to provide students with hands-on research experience. In particular, a research project related to education management and applied psychology will be conducted. In their projects, students will implement research ideas by extending theories and research skills that they have learned and acquired. They will produce a research report under the supervision of the instructor.

MOP605 Guided Study in Education Management (3 credits)
This course provides an opportunity for students to engage in an independent study on a topic of their own choice relating to education management. With academic guidance of the Program Director, students may identify an area of professional/academic interest for taking the guided study. The matching between the student and the tutor will be based on individual student's learning needs and expertise of the faculty member. Through one-to-one personal tutorials and guidance, students are expected to complete their studies within a semester.

MOP606 Fostering Interculturality through Intercultural Education (3 credits)
With the globalization and internationalization of higher education, there is a growing number of opportunities for students to cross borders, countries and cultures to start a new language and cultural learning journey in a different educational context. It is crucial to prepare our students with relevant theory and research together with some practical examples, facilitate their understanding of their intercultural education, and help them achieve positive learning outcomes by relating their international/intercultural learning experiences to the topics in this course. This course is designed for postgraduate (PG) students in the university with recent or current international experience (study abroad, exchange program, immersion program, internships, or educational visit). It will guide students in understanding theories and topics such as language/cultural shock, acculturation challenges and strategies, intercultural adjustment, intercultural relations, identity expansion, global citizenship and intercultural competence through using English as a second language from perspectives of international education (intercultural contact and diversity) and cross-cultural psychology (the stress and coping theory, cultural learning theory and the social identity theory).

MOP609 Innovative Pedagogy: Frameworks & Application (3 credits)
Innovative pedagogy represents a shift from traditional teacher-centered approaches to student-centered, dynamic, and context-aware teaching practices. This course explores the theoretical foundations, design principles, and practical applications of innovative pedagogical models such as project-based learning, flipped classroom, inquiry-based learning, game-based learning, and technology-enhanced teaching. These innovative learning methods enable students to apply different innovative pedagogical models to solve real-world educational challenges.

MIH601 Internationalising Education: Institutional Strategy and Development (3 credits)
In this module you will explore the origins and development of international higher education with particular focus on current drivers and practices. Drivers include economic, political, cultural, and academic pressures, while the practices covered will include transnational campuses, international collaborations in teaching and research, the recruitment of overseas students and staff, and the use of new distance learning technologies. These will be illustrated through specific examples in different contexts, making use of both the literature and invited speakers.

MIH611 Generative AI in Education: Applications and Challenges (3 credits)
This course explores the application, integration, and challenges of Generative AI (GAI) in both school education and higher education. It aims to equip students with the knowledge and skills to effectively integrate GAI into teaching, learning, and assessment processes. The course covers contemporary strategies, techniques, ethical considerations, and practical challenges associated with GAI in education.

CRS512 Evolution of Early Childhood Education in China and the World (3 credits)
Early childhood education (ECE) is one of the indicators in the education domain to reflect urbanization. This course is designed to introduce and review the development of ECE in regions including Mainland China and the Western world. Over the past few decades, following the policy of marketizing and privatizing non-compulsory preschool education and the announcement of the “Outline of China’s National Plan for Medium and Long-term Education Reform and Development (2010-2020)” (State Council of China, 2010), a dramatic change has been seen in ECE in Mainland China. Students will be able to learn more about the development of China and the world from the perspective of education and how the influences of social, political and economic development drive mainland China’s ECE evolution: the early development of ECE before the 1950s; the one-child policy of the 1980s; the decline of public kindergartens in the 1990s; and post-2010 developments. And by comparing studies from Mainland China and the Western world, the course will also discuss the quality standards, assurance policy development, kindergarten participation, and disparities in the quality of provision so as to let students have a more comprehensive understanding towards the ECE ecosystem establishment and related discussions. Current trends and challenges will also be explored.

---

Programme Overview

Aims of the Programme
The OPEM programme is a one-year full-time or two-year part-time inter-disciplinary programme, combines combining advanced training in education management and organisational psychology with a capstone project based upon students selected organizations. The OPEM programme offers students a rich experiential learning environment through interacting with leading scholars in the education management and applied psychology fields. The combination of three different types of learning, namely core courses, capstone and summer school, can benefit students academically in class and practically at work. In particular, core courses will equip students with academic knowledge on various organisational psychology and education management theories and concepts.

The OPEM programme aims to:

- Equip students with a broad knowledge, understanding of organisational psychology in education management (e.g. The role of leadership in school settings)
- Nurture students to become critical thinkers and problem solvers who could tackle issues in education management with a holistic approach
- Offer a wide range of experiential learning opportunities for critical analysis and perform reflective practice review of educational management in a wider policy context
- Conduct contemporary policy analysis and organisational research in education setting through engagements with practitioners and the wider policy community.

Progression Requirements

Effective from 2025-26 intake
A student should be subject to an academic review at the end of each term generally. In the case where a student enrolled in less than 6 credits in a term, the academic review shall be done when he/she has cumulatively enrolled in 6 credits or more.

A student whose Cumulative G.P.A. is below 2.50 at the academic review will be put on academic probation in the following term (unless his/her studies shall be discontinued as prescribed by Regulation 17- Regulation Governing Taught Doctoral/Master's Degree Programmes (effective from 2026-27 intake) .

At the end of the term in which the student on academic probation has cumulatively enrolled in 6 or more credits, if he/she obtains a Cumulative G.P.A. of 2.50 or above, probation will be lifted. Otherwise, his/her studies will be discontinued.

A student may be put on academic probation for only one time during his/her whole period of study on the programme. After the academic probation is lifted and the student continued on the programme, the student will be discontinued from his/her studies if his/her Cumulative G.P.A. is below 2.50 at an academic review.

A warning concerning the need to improve his/her academic performance should be issued to a student whose Cumulative G.P.A. is at or above the level required for progression (at or above 2.50) but below the level for graduation (at or above 2.67).

For 2024-25 intake and before

Graduates Sharing

ZHOU Ziwei
2019-20 OPEM Graduate with Distinction Award Recipient
full-time weekend mode

"As a proud graduate of OPEM, I can wholeheartedly attest to the remarkable academic voyage it provides. What truly distinguishes the OPEM program is its unwavering commitment to not only nurturing knowledge and skills but also cultivating a culture of exploration and personal growth. My time at OPEM served as an inspiring catalyst, propelling me to aspire to greater heights. Upon graduation from OPEM, I embarked on a transformative educational journey by pursuing the Doctor of Policy Studies program at Lingnan University. This decision led to a dual doctoral degree offered by National Chengchi University in Taiwan. This unique opportunity has allowed me to immerse myself in diverse academic landscapes and engage with individuals from a wide array of backgrounds, thus fostering a profoundly enriching and holistic learning experience."

CHEN Zixin
2022-23 OPEM Graduate
full-time weekday mode

"OPEM programme truly reshaped my thinking. It masterfully connects the dots between psychology and education, offering a powerful lens to understand human learning. I entered with no experience in the field yet found myself thriving thanks to the faculty who genuinely champion your growth. The most rewarding part was learning alongside a diverse cohort—each collaboration expanded my own perspective. This programme doesn't just teach me; it prepares me for a remarkable range of future paths."

JIANG Yiping
2022-2023 OPEM Graduate with Distinction Award Recipient
full-time weekend mode

"After 16 years of international education, returning to the campus of Lingnan University in Hong Kong, I have revisited an unforgettable campus life, felt the dedication and humanistic care of Lingnan University professors, and experienced the rigour and meticulousness of their academic research. Taking into account both educational management and psychology, OPEM has helped me to conceptualise and summarise the teaching experience of more than ten years from the perspective of pedagogy and management so that cases in practice and theories have been well matched, which has promoted my understanding of concepts and optimised my teaching and management methods. At the same time, the relevant courses in organizational psychology have also effectively helped me to clarify the new direction of personnel deployment and talent training. The OPEM learning journey answered my workplace puzzles and effectively solved the problems I encountered in the management process."

Admission

Application Procedure
Online Application

Admission

Entry Requirements

To be eligible for entry into this programme, an applicant shall normally

hold a relevant bachelor’s degree awarded by a tertiary education institution recognised for this purpose by the University; or
have obtained an equivalent qualification, or
has provided satisfactory evidence of academic and professional attainment

English Language Requirements

In addition to the above, an applicant whose degree, or exceptionally, an alternative qualification deemed equivalent, was not awarded by a tertiary institution where English was used as the medium of instruction (MOI) for the programme shall obtain:
a minimum score of 79 in the TOEFL Internet-Based Test 1 ; or
a band score of 6.0 or above in IELTS 1 ; or
a minimum score of 450 or above in College English Test Band 6 (CET-6), with proof of competency in spoken English 2 ; or
an equivalent score in a recognised test; or
an equivalent qualification to prove his/her English language proficiency, which will be considered on a case-by-case basis.

For IELTS and TOEFL qualifications for admission purposes, the University accepts only results from a single test sitting . The test must be conducted at a recognised test centre and fall within the two-year validity period, counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort.

For CET-6 results submitted for admission purposes, the University accepts only those obtained within the five-year validity period , counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort. Applicants must also demonstrate competency in spoken English through an oral interview conducted by the Programme and/or obtain a grade B or above in the CET-6 Speaking English Test.

No Dishonest and Bribery Practices
Any false or misleading information and/or document(s) provided in the application, or any dishonest conduct observed during the application process, e.g. plagiarism, employing surrogate examination-taker, etc., will result in disqualification of application for admission, revocation of admission offer or termination of studies at the University if admitted, and any fees paid will be forfeited.

Falsification of documents or the use of falsified documents is a very serious offence and is absolutely not tolerated by the University. According to the laws of Hong Kong, forgery is a serious criminal offence that a convicted offender may be liable to imprisonment for a maximum of 14 years.

Applicants or their family members and agents must not offer advantages to any staff or members of the University in relation to their admission applications or while having dealings of any kind with the University. These offers may constitute an offence under the Prevention of Bribery Ordinance (Cap.201) in Hong Kong. The University also prohibits its staff and members from soliciting or accepting such advantages.

Application Period
For 2026-27 Intake:

1 November 2025 to 15 June 2026*

*The Programme Office reserves the right to change the application closing date without prior notice based on the actual admission situation.

Application Procedure

To apply for the Master of Social Sciences in Organisational Psychology and Education Management programme, please follow the steps below:

Create an account in the Online Application System

Complete the online application form

Upload scanned copies of the following documents to the system:
Academic Certificates and Transcripts;

Proof of English Language Proficiency (IELTS, TOEFL) if needed;

ID Card (HKID is not required to upload), Passport, or any othe identity document(s) of your home country;

Personal Statement;

Recommendation Letter(s);

Other relevant materials.

Settle the application fee by a credit card. Application fee (HKDS400) is non-refundable and non-transferable

Invite two referees to write two recommendation letters and send the originals of completed recommendation letters in a sealed envelope to the Programme Team OR upload to Online Application System directly.

Rooted in the University’s liberal arts mission and its strategic vision to integrate interdisciplinary training with real-world relevance, the MSoDA responds to a growing demand for graduates who can combine sociological insight with advanced data analytical skills.
Through its innovative structure and interdisciplinary orientation, the MSoDA aims to prepare graduates for leadership in a data-rich world where sociological thinking is increasingly vital.

Source: https://www.ln.edu.hk/socsp/msoda/programme-overview/course-description
Home

Department of Sociology & Social Policy

Master of Social Sciences in Sociology and Data Analytics (MSODA)

Programme Overview

Course Description

Explore More

Programme Overview

Programme Structure
Course Description

Course Descriptions

Compulsory Courses

SOC611 Sociology in Action: Theories and Applications
This course introduces sociology as an applied analytical framework for understanding and addressing complex social problems. It emphasizes the practical use of sociological theories to analyse issues such as inequality, technological change, demographic shifts, and environmental challenges, linking theory with research, policy, and public discourse.

SOC612 Data Analytics for Social Research
This course introduces the principles and practices of social data analytics, combining conceptual foundations with hands-on experience in analysing large and complex social datasets. Students learn to use R and Python to clean, visualise, and analyse data, while critically reflecting on the ethical implications of data analytics.

SOC613 Applied Analytical Statistics for Social Scientists
This course provides an application-oriented introduction to statistical reasoning in the social sciences, covering multivariate analysis, regression techniques, and basic causal inference. Students develop skills to formulate research questions, apply appropriate methods, and interpret quantitative findings using real-world social data.

SOC615 Topical Seminars
This seminar-based course introduces students to current research in sociology and data analytics through faculty-led and invited talks. Students engage with data-driven studies across diverse topics and reflect on how sociological theory and empirical research are integrated in practice.

SOC616 Social Theory in the Digital Era
This course examines how classical and contemporary sociological theories can be used to analyse social transformations driven by digital technologies. Key topics include platform society, algorithmic governance, digital labour, surveillance, and online identity, linking theory with empirical case studies.

SOC617 Social Network Analysis
This course offers a hands-on introduction to the analysis and visualisation of social networks. Students learn to work with relational data and interpret network patterns such as centrality, clustering, and diffusion using real-world social applications.

SOC618 Machine Learning with Social Data
This course introduces machine learning methods for analysing social data, focusing on how algorithms can reveal, predict, and sometimes reproduce social patterns. Students gain practical experience with supervised and unsupervised models while engaging with issues of fairness and interpretability.

SOC619 Sociology & Data Analytics Research Project
This capstone course enables students to design and conduct an applied sociological research project that integrates theoretical reasoning with data analytics. Working in pairs, students analyse primary or secondary data and communicate their findings in a professional research report.

Elective Courses

SOC607 Applied Survey Methodology
This course introduces the principles of survey design and implementation for social research and policy analysis. Students learn questionnaire construction, data processing, and basic statistical analysis, with an emphasis on scientific integrity and practical application.

SOC608 Qualitative Methods for Policy Analysis
This course explores qualitative research methods commonly used in social policy, including interviews, ethnography, content analysis, and focus groups. It emphasizes research design, ethical practice, and analytical skills for policy-relevant qualitative inquiry.

CDS539 Natural Language Processing
This course provides an introduction to Natural Language Processing, covering key text analysis tasks such as classification, summarisation, topic modelling, and question answering, alongside an overview of contemporary computational approaches.

CDS542 Data Visualization
This course introduces fundamental principles and tools for transforming complex data into effective visual representations. Through hands-on projects, students learn data preparation, visual design, and storytelling using widely used visualisation platforms.

Master of Social Sciences in Sociology and Data Analytics (MSODA)

Programme Overview

Explore More

Programme Overview

Programme Structure
Course Description

Programme Overview

The Master of Social Sciences in Sociology and Data Analytics (MSoDA) , is hosted by the Department of Sociology and Social Policy (SOCSP) at Lingnan University . Rooted in the University’s liberal arts mission and its strategic vision to integrate interdisciplinary training with real-world relevance, the MSoDA responds to a growing demand for graduates who can combine sociological insight with advanced data analytical skills.

The programme is designed to equip students with the theoretical grounding and methodological expertise necessary to analyse complex social phenomena using both traditional sociological tools and cutting-edge computational techniques. In doing so, it bridges a significant gap between academic training in the social sciences and the data literacy increasingly required in government, policy institutions, NGOs, and the private sector across Hong Kong, Mainland China, and the Asia-Pacific region. The MSoDA builds on the Department’s established strengths in social policy, cultural sociology, and inequality studies, as well as its growing engagement with computational social science. It also strategically complements existing programmes such as the Master of Social Sciences in Comparative Social Policy (International) (IMCSP) , with which it shares several courses and resources, while offering a distinct curriculum focused on applied sociology and data-driven research.

Structured as a one-year, full-time programme, the MSoDA combines a core curriculum in sociology and statistics with cutting-edge computational methods, such as machine learning and social network analysis. Students will gain hands-on experience working with real-world data and producing research outputs tailored to academic, policy, or professional audiences. Through its innovative structure and interdisciplinary orientation, the MSoDA aims to prepare graduates for leadership in a data-rich world where sociological thinking is increasingly vital.

Programme Structure

Source: https://www.ln.edu.hk/socsp/msoda/admission-information
Home

Department of Sociology & Social Policy

Master of Social Sciences in Sociology and Data Analytics (MSODA)

Admission Information

Admission Information

Mode of Study and Duration

The MSoDA programme requires students to to complete a total of 10 courses amounting to 30 credits as specified in the Programme curriculum.

Mode of Study

The normal duration of the programme is one year .

Medium of Instruction

English

Admission Requirements

To be eligible for entry into this programme, candidates should have:

A relevant bachelor’s degree (e.g., sociology, psychology, political science, economics, public policy, data science) awarded by a tertiary education institution recognised for this purpose by the University; or have obtained an equivalent qualification; or have provided satisfactory evidence of academic and professional attainment;
Prior training in statistics is not required, though basic familiarity with research methods or descriptive statistics is welcomed and will be advantageous for study in the programme ; and
An applicant whose degree or, exceptionally, an alternative qualification deemed equivalent from a tertiary institution is not using English as the medium of instruction (MOI) for the programme should obtain:

A minimum score of 79 (internet-based test) in the Test of English as a Foreign Language (TOEFL) 1 ;
A band score of 6.0 or above in the International English Language Testing System (IELTS) 1 ;
A minimum score of 450 or above in College English Test Band 6 (CET-6), with proof of competency in spoken English 2 ;
We will consider equivalent qualifications on a case-by-case basis.

For academic qualification obtained in Mainland China, the applicants are required to provide the online verification report of your bachelor degree certificate from the following website: https://xwrz.chsi.com.cn/gateway . You are required to submit the verification report in your online application.

Interviews will be conducted by the Admissions Co-ordinator, the Programme Director, and the Associate Programme Director. Moreover, a one-week pre-entry workshop will be organised. Additional online materials will be provided for self-study prior to the programme commencement.

1 For IELTS and TOEFL qualifications for admission purpose, the University only accepts results of tests that are conducted at a test centre and within the validity period, viz. 2 years , from the time of submission of an admission application for the specific intake cohort.

2 For CET-6 results submitted for admission purposes, the University accepts only those obtained within the five-year validity period, counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort. Applicants must also demonstrate competency in spoken English through an oral interview conducted by the Program and/or obtain grade B or above in the CET-6 Speaking English Test.

Important Notes

Any false or misleading information and/or document(s) provided in the application, or any dishonest conduct observed during the application process, e.g. plagiarism, employing surrogate examination-taker, etc., will result in disqualification of application for admission, revocation of admission offer or termination of studies at the University if admitted, and any fees paid will be forfeited.

According to the laws of Hong Kong, forgery is a serious criminal offence that a convicted offender may be liable to imprisonment for a maximum of 14 years.

For more details, please visit:

Application Method

To apply to the MSoDA programme, the applicants are required to submit the following application materials through the online application system:

Identity documents (e.g. Identity card 1 , passport or other travel document)
Official Transcript (for academic qualifications of undergraduate and/or postgraduate studies): An official final transcript for a completed programme
The latest transcript if the programme is not yet completed
An official English-translated copy must be attached if the transcript is not in English. The interpretation of grades (in English) is also required.

Degree Certificate/Graduation Certificate/Proof of Study (as appropriate)
Verification proof by third-party authorities (for a completed programme): Online Verification Report of Higher Education Degree Certificate (中國高等教育學位在綫驗証報告), both Chinese and English versions, issued by the CHSI (中國高等教育學生信息網) for qualification(s) awarded by universities in Mainland China
Online Query of Overseas Credentials Evaluation Reports國(境)外學歷學位認証書 issued by the Chinese Service Center for Scholarly Exchange (CSCSE) (教育部留學服務中心) (applicable to Mainland applicants with qualifications awarded by overseas universities)

Proof of English language proficiency, e.g. IETLS, TOEFL Score Report (applicable to applicants whose degree or, exceptionally, an alternative qualification deemed equivalent, is not from a tertiary institution where English is used as the medium of instruction (MOI) for the degree programme)
Personal statement
Reference letters

1 Do not upload copy of HKID. Local students will be required to present HKID for verification upon registration.

The MSoDA Programme Office will conduct interviews with all applicants before making a formal offer for admission.

Apply Now

Programme Fees and Expenses

The MSoDA programme tuition fee from AY2026-27 is HK$181,500 .

An application fee of HK$400 will be charged upon completion of an online application. The application fee is non-refundable and non-referable.

You will also be expected to take care of additional expenses including accommodation, travel to and from Hong Kong, and Hong Kong Immigration visa charges (HK$400, if applicable) .

For more details for coming to Hong Kong for postgraduate study, you may visit: http://www.studyinhongkong.edu.hk/en/.

Admission Schedule

Application Deadline: 15 June 2026 (Non-local) / 31 July 2026 (Local)

Home

Department of Sociology & Social Policy

Master of Social Sciences in Sociology and Data Analytics (MSODA)

MSoDA Entrance Scholarship Scheme

MSoDA Entrance Scholarship Scheme

The MSoDA Entrance Scholarship Scheme aims to provide financial support to outstanding admittees who aspire to gain hands-on experience in working with real-world data and producing research outputs tailored to academic, policy, or professional audiences. Students who are interested in deepening their understanding of pressing social issues through sociological perspectives and advanced data analytical skills are strongly encouraged to apply.

Amount of Scholarship

The Scholarship Scheme selects outstanding students admitted to the MSoDA programme. Successful applicants will be awarded ranging from 25% to 100% tuition fee waiver , subject to their academic qualifications and interview performance.

An application fee of HK$400 is payable upon completion of the online application. The application fee is non-refundable and non-transferable .

The next application deadline is 30 May 2026 (Non-local) and 31 July 2026 (Local) for August/September 2026 intake.
