Programme Introduction

GHIT Programme
Unique Features

Shape the Future of Humanitarian Action

Are you ready to turn your passion for global change into real-world impact?
The MSc in Global Humanitarian Innovation and Technology (GHIT) (formerly known as Humanitarian Technology Inclusive Business, HTIB) at Lingnan University is a cutting-edge programme designed for aspiring changemakers who want to harness innovation, technology, and global collaboration to solve today’s most urgent humanitarian challenges.

Through a strategic partnership with the United Nations University Hub on Humanitarian Innovation and Technology at Lingnan University (UNU hub) , GHIT offers unparalleled access to global networks, research initiatives, and hands-on learning opportunities that prepare you to lead in the humanitarian sector.

What makes GHIT unique?

Unique partnership with the United Nations (UNU Hub) in humanitarian innovation and technology
Focus on inclusive innovation for non-techies without engineering backgrounds
Emphasis on financially sustainable humanitarian solutions and inclusive business models
Practical community engagement through real-life projects with local and international partners
⁠Interdisciplinary approach prioritizing "whole-person" education for diverse backgrounds
Targeted career prospects bridging management and engineering in local and international contexts
A combination of studio-based, maker culture, fieldwork, and traditional teaching methods
Importance of technology in driving efficiency, anticipation, and empowerment in public services; Without tech, humanitarian systems remain fragmented and slow
Teach in English with support in Cantonese/Mandarin
The class schedule includes weekend classes to accomodate working professionals

Background
Established in 2023 as the Master of Science in Humanitarian Technology and Inclusive Business (HTIB), this pioneering programme was designed to address pressing global challenges through innovation, HTIB became the first and only programme in Asia focused on leveraging technology for humanitarian impact.
In 2025, Lingnan University forged a landmark partnership with the United Nations University (UNU) to establish the UNU Hub on Humanitarian Innovation and Technology (HIT)—the first of its kind in Asia. This collaboration positioned HTIB as the cornerstone of the Hub’s educational mission, integrating cutting-edge research, global networks, and field-driven projects into its curriculum. To reflect this strategic alignment and enhance clarity for prospective students, the programme was renamed Master of Science in Global Humanitarian Innovation and Technology (GHIT) from AY2026-27 intake.

Learning Objectives
Understand humanitarian technology concepts and design processes
Master inclusive business fundamentals and innovation frameworks
Apply strategies and toolkits for product/service design
Explore best practices in disaster and crisis management
Create scalable humanitarian technologies and inclusive business solutions

Who should apply
GHIT is ideal for innovators from any discipline who want to sharpen problem-solving skills with technology insightsand inclusive business strategies.

Perfect for:
Decision-makers and entrepreneurs
NGO professionals, development practitioners, designers, project managers, product engineers
Those aiming for careers in tech startups, non-profits, academia, management, social entrepreneurship, healthcare, education, engineering, ESG, and global development

If you’re passionate about creating solutions that drive equity, sustainability, and impact, GHIT is your next step.

Progression
Students are required to achieve CGPA of 2.50 or above in order to proceed to the next term. The Board of Examiners shall, at the end of each term, recommend whether each student is:
Eligible for progression towards an award;
Eligible for an award; or
Required to withdraw from the programme.

A student will normally progress unless he/she falls within the following categories, any of which may be regarded as grounds for withdrawal from the programme:
The student cannot attain a CGPA of 2.50 at the end of each term;
The student has exceeded the maximum period of study, which is 3 years in full-time mode or 5 years in part-time mode; or
The student is unable to attain a CGPA of 2.67 at the end of the programme.

Why Choosing Lingnan?
Globally Recognized for Quality Education Lingnan University ranked No.1 worldwide in SDG 4: Quality Education in the THE Impact Rankings 2025 and 2026 - the only Liberal Arts University in Hong Kong offering a holistic education that blends arts, business and social sciences

Strong Performance in THE World University Rankings In 2025, Lingnan is placed in the 301-350 band globally among around 3000 institutions worldwide, indicating that we are the top 10% institutions globally, showcasing out excellence in global collaboration and diversity

Close Faculty-Student Relationship
Strong Global Exposure and Exchange Opportunities Lingan has partnerships with over 270 institutions in more than 50 countries, offering students extensive exchange programes, service learning programes, interships

Community Serving for Positive Changes Lingnan's motto ‘Education for Service’, we integrates community service and civic engagement into our curriculum. Our programme allow students to participate in Service-Learning Programs that empower them to make positive social impacts in Hong Kong communities

Sustainable Development Goals
Graduates from Lingnan University have the knowledge and tools for leading society in a more sustainable way, as sustainable development is an integral part of all programmes.

The programme helps to improve employment opportunities, particularly for young people who have ideas to solve questions that matter by providing quality teaching and coaching students in creativity, innovation, entrepreneurship, and sustainability. This programme provides knowledge in different fields of industries with an emphasis on innovation, entrepreneurship, and humanitarian technology. Moreover, students will get an opportunity to learn about the ideas of sustainable communities and responsible consumption of natural resources and production by the application of creative ideas, entrepreneurial approaches, and innovative management to create a better world.

Apply here

Career Prospect

Career Prospects

Your Gateway to a Career
Join GHIT

The GHIT Programme (formerly HTIB) empowers students with cutting-edge interdisciplinary skills at the crossroads of global health, technology, and innovation. Whether you're passionate about driving change in launching impactful tech solutions, GHIT prepares you to thrive in diverse career paths—from multinational enterprises and startups to NGOs and government agencies.



With strong industry connections and a hands-on learning approach, GHIT opens doors to exciting opportunities in Hong Kong, the Greater Bay Area, and global markets. Our graduates are not only job-ready—they’re future-ready.



What’s more, students consistently rate the programme’s learning outcomes highly, with an impressive average satisfaction score of 4.7 out of 5.

0 74 %

Across the past two graduating cohorts, 73.6% of HTIB graduates who found employment secured their first job within six months of graduation. Notably, 23.5% were hired in under one month, reflecting strong industry demand and graduate readiness.

Application Procedure

To apply for the GHIT programme, please complete the following steps by 30th June 2026:
Complete the online application form: https://www.ln.edu.hk/admission/apply

Upload scanned copies of the following documents to the system: ​
Copies of academic certificates and transcripts;

Proof of English language proficiency (IELTS, TOEFL), if needed;

ID card, passport, or any other identity document(s) of your home country;

A personal statement (written in English and not more than two pages);

Curriculum vitae;

Other relevant supporting documents.

Avoid applying at the last minute as according to the system, applications may be required to provide the letter of certificate/transcript, confidential recommendation letter, and China Qualification Verification report.

Settle the application fee (HKD$400) with a credit card. The application fee is non-refundable and non-transferable.

SAG Student YANG Meng's Taiwan Journey

From the faith-based Eco-Spiritual Village fostering mutual care, to the Senior Learning Center encouraging lifelong learning, Suyuan’s social prescription model, and Xikou Mutual-Help Home assisting dementia caregivers, these visits offered valuable insights and deepened my understanding of holistic elderly care and community support.

SAG Student QIN Chenyu's Taiwan Journey

During our trip to Taiwan, we visited four meaningful organizations. The Lianzhuyuan community demonstrated a faith-based mutual assistance spirit, where residents selflessly support one another. At the Gukeng Senior Learning Center, elderly individuals shared their passion for lifelong learning by teaching us how to roast coffee beans. Suyuan’s social prescription model highlighted the importance of community-based healthcare. Lastly, the mutual-help family visit showed the strength of community support and volunteerism. These experiences deeply inspired me and expanded my understanding of holistic elderly care.

CRS508 Social Development and Policy Change in Asia (3 credits)
MIH501 Globalisation, Policy and Society (3 credits)
MIA607 NGOs, Civil Society and the State (3 credits)

The course equips students with leading theories and approaches for studying the interrelationship between non-governmental organisations (NGOs), civil society and the state. It examines the rise of NGOs in international, national and local governance and advocacy. Specific topics include the definition and classification of NGOs and civil society, the roles played by NGOs in development, the major approaches to studying NGOs, and the challenges and limitations facing NGOs. It concludes with a preliminary examination of the latest development of civil society and NGOs in the Greater China Region.
CPA601 Comparative Public Administration Capstone Project and Symposium Presentation (6 credits)

The course is meant to provide students with an opportunity to reflectively and practically integrate the lessons from other policy related courses. It focuses on the public policy issues that a society faces by identifying and thoroughly analysing the causes and consequences of various social problems and the opportunities and limitations for policy responses to them. This course has been divided into two parts. The first part comprises an intensive comparative methodological lesson on researching various policy issues. Students will be taken through different methodologies and analytical techniques. In the second part, students will be expected to work individually or in groups to carry out short empirical research based on primary or secondary data.

These activities not only enriched our knowledge system but also allowed us to immerse ourselves in Hong Kong's society, experience daily life, identify more public policy issues, and acquire additional professional knowledge through enjoyable experiences.

They are conscientious and dedicated, always doing their best to support us in our studies and career development.

Master of Social Sciences in Comparative Public Administration

Course Description

Explore More

Programme Overview

Unique Features
Programme Information
Programme Structure
Course Description
Digital and Smart Governance, DSG concentration

Course Description

Core Courses

CPA501 Policy Processes and Analysis (3 credits)

This course aims to enable students to understand the policy process and identify, analyse, and evaluate major policy issues. The course consists of two parts: policy process and studies on major policy issues. For the first part, the policy process is considered in a very broad context – from the evolution of an idea and the enactment of legislation, to program implementation and evaluation. Students will study major steps in the policy processes, from issue identification, policy decision-making, policy implementation, to policy evaluation. In the second part of the course, key policy issues will be discussed further, including education, healthcare, welfare etc. This course will further show how quantitative and qualitative analyses could be performed in policy studies. After taking the course, students will be expected to be able to analyse major policy issues from an analytical perspective.

CPA502 Research Methods in Public Management (3 credits)

This course is designed to introduce students to a wide range of empirical research methods in public management. Topics will include concept formation, theory development and testing, the design of data collection activities, data visualisation, qualitative interviews, fieldwork, basic qualitative and quantitative data analysis techniques and causal inference. Throughout the semester, students will develop a research paper based on self-collected survey data.

CPA503 Public Policy Lab (3 credits)

This course is designed to help students understand the basics of public policy development, initiation, implementation, and assessment across different countries. This course provides students with an overview of the numerous theories, models, and methodologies used by academics and practitioners in the field of policy studies. Comparative case analyses will help students understand how the characteristics of public policy differ in achieving goals, delivering services, and evaluating their effectiveness across countries (or across localities) and how they influence policymakers’ attitudes and behaviour, organisational performance, and other outcomes. This course also exposes students to analysing policy issues related to the introduction of information technology in public management. Special attention will be given to reflect on increasingly smart governance issues, especially citizens’ concerns about privacy and data management during policy implementation. By engaging in in-class discussions and extracurricular activities, each student will interact and work closely with the lab instructor through desk crits, pin-ups, and formal reviews. Students will be encouraged to conduct small-scale research on a variety of public policy topics and to attend field trips and seminars with policymakers and practitioners from various professions. By completing this course, students will be able to recognise and define public policy issues across different countries, examine public policy issues in which they are interested in, and provide good suggestions on how such issues could be addressed or solved.

CPA509 Theories and Approaches of Comparative Politics (3 credits)

This course attempts a general survey in the field of comparative politics. Some important concepts and methods of the discipline are introduced. Specific topics include methods and approaches of comparative politics; government, regime and state; unitary, federal and confederal systems; typologies of state-society relations, specifically on bureaucratic- authoritarianism, totalitarianism, corporatism, civil society and populism; political culture and nationalism; interest group and political party; election and political participation; parliamentary, presidential and semi-presidential systems; comparative democracies between majoritarian and consociational model; and lastly on the topic of democratisation and regime change.

SOC601 Governance, Management and Delivery (3 credits)

This course is designed to explore how the nature of governance, management, and delivery across the globe differs and how such differences shape the policymaking process. Much of the teaching in this course will be based around the analysis of case studies and the assessment builds directly on these.

Elective Courses

CPA504 Chinese Politics, Policy and Governance (3 credits)

This course provides a comprehensive overview of China's political development, with an emphasis on China’s role in various aspects of global governance. It offers an in-depth analysis of how Chinses governance affects public action in practice by examining different policy areas, such as health reform, public finance, higher education, environmental protection, technology innovation, and other critical issues. This course also highlights China’s significant economic and political activities through policies, such as the Belt and Road Initiative, and the BRICS block (Brazil, Russia, India, China and South Africa). The basic objective of this course is to provide students with an opportunity to learn about and critically reflect on China’s role in global and regional governance frameworks. From that foundation, we will move on to current governing challenges in China as well as the future shape of China's socio-political change.

CRS507 China’s Regional Development: Comparative Perspectives (3 credits)

This course aims to provide a comprehensive understanding of China’s regional development from a comparative perspective. It will provide students with a set of commonly used concepts in understanding regional development in China and teach students basic analytical skills with examples and data. The course focus on not only the system of cities but also vast rural areas in China. The topics include social inclusion, migration, economic development, urbanisation, globalisation, education, governance, and financialisation. Lecture materials are based on not only different metropolises in China, e.g. Shanghai, Beijing, Shenzhen, Hong Kong, etc. ranging from emerging to highly developed international metropolises but also rural areas. Based on explicit development models in these topical areas, the course will examine the various forces and factors that influence regional development and explores interdisciplinary approaches and tools for interventions in response to critical pressures and challenges.

CRS508 Social Development and Policy Change in Asia (3 credits)

This course introduces students to recent social changes with particular attention given to compare and contrast social, economic, demographic changes taking place in Asia. More specifically, this course examines critically major social policy challenges confronting rapid social and political economy changes in Greater China region and East and Southeast Asia. Particular attention will be given to examining how governments, markets and civil societies in the region respond to such challenges in adopting social policy policies appropriate for managing rapid changes. Students will be engaged in case analysis, critically reflecting upon how social and political economy changes in selected Asian societies would have affected policy changes. Adopting a comparative approach in analysing policy formation and implementation, together with the collaborative learning activities, this course will enable students to understand the most recent developments related to poverty alleviation, education, health, social security, and housing policies, as well as urban and cultural changes in Asia.

MIH501 Globalisation, Policy and Society (3 credits)

This course focuses on several key topics in education and social policy in East Asia and the rest of the world, with particular reference to analyse how education and social policy have been affected by the growing trends of globalisation. The course takes an explicitly comparative approach with each of the issues examined through case studies of societies located in East Asia like Hong Kong, Taiwan and Mainland China, Japan, Korea and Singapore, critically examining how the globalising economy and the growing tide of neo-liberalism have affected social change and policy reform. Largely student-centered, the course is structured around student-guided discussions of assigned readings, with the goal of encouraging the drawing of conclusions about important policy issues from the comparison of different cases. Through preparation for discussions, organising group presentations and completing written assignments, students will develop independent inquiry skills to explore the interrelationships between policy and social phenomena.

MIA607 NGOs, Civil Society and the State (3 credits)

The course equips students with leading theories and approaches for studying the interrelationship between non-governmental organisations (NGOs), civil society and the state. It examines the rise of NGOs in international, national and local governance and advocacy. Specific topics include the definition and classification of NGOs and civil society, the roles played by NGOs in development, the major approaches to studying NGOs, and the challenges and limitations facing NGOs. It concludes with a preliminary examination of the latest development of civil society and NGOs in the Greater China Region.

Capstone Project

CPA601 Comparative Public Administration Capstone Project and Symposium Presentation (6 credits)

The course is meant to provide students with an opportunity to reflectively and practically integrate the lessons from other policy related courses. It focuses on the public policy issues that a society faces by identifying and thoroughly analysing the causes and consequences of various social problems and the opportunities and limitations for policy responses to them. This course has been divided into two parts. The first part comprises an intensive comparative methodological lesson on researching various policy issues. Students will be taken through different methodologies and analytical techniques. In the second part, students will be expected to work individually or in groups to carry out short empirical research based on primary or secondary data.

Graduates' Sharing

Graduates from AY2024-25

GAO Shuo (Graduated from AY2024-25)

My CPA storybook was filled with more than just lectures. Its most exciting chapters were written during our field trips—from observing governance in action at the Hong Kong Legislative Council to attending police anti-scam talks. These experiences made concepts like public policy and citizen engagement tangible.

On campus, I was constantly motivated by the genuine passion and selfless support of our professors, who were always ready to guide us. This inspiring environment fueled my growth through challenging group projects and presentations, which honed my teamwork and public speaking skills. The lively debates with professors and peers on policy analysis and stakeholder management didn’t just broaden my perspective—they built my confidence.

What made CPA truly special was its warm, family-like atmosphere. Spontaneous class trips taught me that growth isn’t just about knowledge, but also friendship, cultural exchange, and balancing work with joy.This holistic journey revealed a version of myself I hadn't known—capable of grappling with complex ideas and thriving independently. CPA was more than a master's programme; it was a year that unlocked a person I was excited to become.

If you're looking for an education that blends rigorous theory with a warmly human experience, your adventure starts here. Come and write your own unique CPA story!

CUI Yiheng (Graduated from AY2024-25)

The CPA program covers a wide range of content. By comparing the political systems of different countries and regions, it helps us understand the operation of the world machine, cultivates our international perspective, and enhances our ability to identify, analyze and solve problems.
Our course will teach some statistical methods, so as to better analyze and study policies by using data. In addition, representatives from various fields will also be invited to share. For instance, the staff of WWF vividly demonstrated the policy knowledge and daily work in the field of animal protection with specific examples.

Of course, our study was not limited to theory. We also visited places such as the ICAC and the Ngau Tau Kok Police Station, gaining a deep understanding of the daily work of these institutions. At the ICAC, through watching videos and attending Q&A sessions, we gained a better understanding of the real work of the ICAC. At the police station, we attended a lecture on cyber security to learn how to navigate the cyber security landscape.

The one year of study in the CPA program at Lingnan University is a very precious memory for my entire life.

LAI Bo Tak (Graduated from AY2024-25)

I value my time in the Master of Comparative Public Administration (CPA) program as an integral part of my life. This program has helped me appreciate the convergence of theory and practice, equipping me to engage with the system of global public administration critically. Working with different case studies, conferences and visits helped me grasp the geopolitical, economic, and social district factors that affect public governance. Each case study contributed to my ability to appreciate the varying public policies' administrative frameworks and gain value of the policy's effectiveness. Studying with classmates from different backgrounds has been the forerunner of my academic career. This has been the basis of my rich academic conversations on the social cultural administrative practices of governance.

In addition to the last point the program provides for the guidance of the faculty to initiate research in the field of public administration. Faculty support has encouraged and nurtured my academic focus and public service. In the realm of public administration, the CPA program has inspired me to make a positive change. I am excited to carry these lessons into my future career.

ZHAO Yifeng (Graduated from AY2024-25)

Joining the CPA Program was like becoming part of a big family filled with a strong learning atmosphere, unity and friendship support. During this learning journey, I gained a deeper understanding of the research areas, significance and social value of public administration as a discipline. Throughout the program, professors often guided us to adopt a comparative analysis perspective, enabling us to examine public policies across different countries, regions and social contexts and to explore management approaches and solutions to various social issues. Through professors' teachings and academic discussions with classmates, I enhanced my ability to practically apply professional knowledge.

Meanwhile, the professors were extremely patient and responsible, providing us with diverse learning and exchange experiences. They frequently invited scholars to give lectures and seminars, led us on study visits, and organized class outings. These activities not only enriched our knowledge system but also allowed us to immerse ourselves in Hong Kong's society, experience daily life, identify more public policy issues, and acquire additional professional knowledge through enjoyable experiences.

Studying at Lingnan University has made my academic career more colorful and fulfilling. I will always cherish the memories of my time in the CPA Program and wish everyone a successful future!

Graduates from AY2023-24

MAI Zijian

(Graduated from AY2023-24)



Enrolled in the CPA programme at Lingnan University, I studied relevant public policy theories, analyzed development trends, and examined public policies and government management issues from a comparative and international perspective. Through field trips, I explored the history of local governments, regional governance structures, and the evolution of public policies.



The specialized courses are grounded in diverse disciplinary foundations, including public administration, political science, and data analysis, equipping us with a comprehensive understanding of regional governance and comparative public policy. CPA programme boasts a professional teaching team with abundant resources, featuring instructors not only from Lingnan University but also from leading universities across Asia, fostering collaboration in social change and policy research.

Read More

NIAN Mingjie

(Graduated from AY2023-24)



Studying CPA at Lingnan University has been a unique and rewarding experience. The curriculum is highly specialized and interdisciplinary.



Through the lectures, I was able to analyze issues such as public policy and government management from a comparative and international perspective. The programme also includes public policy lab courses and experiential learning activities, such as a visit to the Hong Kong Legislative Council, which allows us to apply theory to practical administration.



The professors have rich teaching and research experience, bringing practical cases and research findings in public management from different countries and regions. They are conscientious and dedicated, always doing their best to support us in our studies and career development.



This year at Lingnan has been an incredibly valuable experience for me. I highly recommend the CPA programme, especially to those who wish to pursue a career in administrative management.

Read More

QU Pei

(Graduated from AY2023-24)



The one-year study in the CPA programme at Lingnan University left a deep impression on me. Learning from experienced professors and participating in various events allowed us to build strong bonds with classmates and faculty members.



CPA503 Public Policy Lab and CPA601 Comparative Public Administration Capstone Project and Symposium Presentation were the most rewarding parts of my experience. The public policy lab invited accomplished professionals from various fields to deliver lectures, which were highly beneficial. Additionally, we had numerous opportunities for field visits, such as to the Court of Final Appeal and Po Leung Kuk, providing practical insights. For the capstone project, we collaborated in groups, which helped us develop teamwork, communication, and coordination skills. Conducting street interviews together not only enriched our lives and experiences but also allowed us to understand diverse perspectives on important issues.



This year in the CPA programme at Lingnan University has been both valuable and unforgettable.

Read More

Admission

Application Period for 2026 - 2027 Intake:
1 Oct 2025 - 10 July 2026*
*The Programme Office reserves the right to change the application closing date without prior notice based on the actual admission situation.

Admission Requirements (AY2026/27)
To be eligible for entry into this programme:
an applicant shall normally hold a relevant bachelor’s degree awarded by a tertiary education institution recognised for this purpose by the University; or
have obtained an equivalent qualification; or
have provided satisfactory evidence of academic and professional attainment.

English Proficiency

In addition to the above, an applicant whose degree or, exceptionally, an alternative qualification deemed equivalent from a tertiary institution, is not using English as the medium of instruction (MOI) for the programme should obtain:
a minimum score of 79 (internet-based test) in TOEFL 1 ; or
a band score of 6.0 or above in IELTS 1 ; or
a minimum score of 450 or above in the College English Test Band 6 (CET-6), with proof of competency in spoken English 2 ; or
an equivalent score in a recognised test; or
an equivalent qualification

1) For IELTS and TOEFL qualifications for admission purposes, the University only accepts results from a single test sitting. The test must be conducted at a recognised test centre and fall within the two-year validity period, counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort.
2) For CET-6 results submitted for admission purposes, the University accepts only those obtained within the five-year validity period, counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort. Applicants must also demonstrate competency in spoken English through an oral interview conducted by the Programme and/or obtain grade B or above in the CET-6 Speaking English Test.
Interviews may be conducted if deemed necessary by the Admissions Coordinator and the Programme Director.

Supporting Documents
Please refer to the Admission Information | School of Graduate Studies for information.

Programme Introduction

CPA aims to meet the rising demand for highly skilled talents in public management and public administration.

With dual emphasis on public policy/administration and governance, this programme is designed to meet the labour market needs to promote fast development and good governance in the Greater Bay Area (GBA), particularly when Hong Kong Special Administrative Region will actively work with Macau Special Administrative Region and other nine cities in the Guangdong province. Anticipating more people to people interactions across borders would result in development issues, which requires public policy and governance measures appropriate for better economic, social and cultural integration across the GBA, the proposed programme will offer new insights and comparative/international perspectives for student learning.

Message
from the Programme Director

Welcome to the Master of Social Sciences in Comparative Public Administration (CPA) Programme at Lingnan University!

As Programme Director, I am thrilled to introduce you to this unique program me , designed to equip you with the knowledge and skills to thrive in the rapidly evolving field of public administration and governance. In recent years, rapid socio-economic changes, particularly the rise of digital transformation, have presented new challenges and opportunities for public management. The CPA program me embraces these advancements, preparing you to leverage technological progress and navigate the complexities of modern governance.
Our distinctive approach is rooted in comparative analysis. We believe that understanding diverse governance models and policy approaches across different contexts is crucial for effective public leadership. The curriculum integrates insights from political science, sociology, economics, and management, providing a holistic understanding of the public sector. It is carefully crafted to provide a strong foundation in core concepts, while also allowing for specialization through a range of elective courses.
What truly sets our program me apart is its emphasis on experiential learning. Through p olicy simulations, interactions with leading practitioners, and a substantial capstone project, you will apply your knowledge to real-world scenarios and develop practical skills highly valued by employers.
We are committed to providing a supportive and stimulating learning environment, guided by a team of experienced academics and practitioners passionate about public service. Whether you are a recent graduate or an experienced professional seeking to advance your career, the CPA P rogram me will provide the tools and networks you need to succeed in this dynamic era.
I invite you to explore our website to learn more about the program me’ s curriculum, faculty, and admission requirements. We look forward to welcoming you to the Lingnan University community!

CPA aims to meet the rising demand for highly skilled talents in public management and public administration.

With dual emphasis on public policy/administration and governance, this programme is designed to meet the labour market needs to promote fast development and good governance in the Greater Bay Area (GBA), particularly when Hong Kong Special Administrative Region will actively work with Macau Special Administrative Region and other nine cities in the Guangdong province. Anticipating more people to people interactions across borders would result in development issues, which requires public policy and governance measures appropriate for better economic, social and cultural integration across the GBA, the proposed programme will offer new insights and comparative/international perspectives for student learning.

suitableBackgrounds: Source: https://www.ln.edu.hk/sgs/programmes-on-offer/master-of-social-sciences-in-health-and-social-services-management Home School of Graduate Studies Programmes on Offer Master of Social Sciences in Health and Social Services Management Application Period: 1 Oct 2025 - 30 Jun 2026 --- Source: https://www.ln.edu.hk/sgs/mhsm SGS MHSM - Master of Social Sciences in Health and Social Services Management (MHSM) Welcome to MHSM Programme Why choose the MHSM Programme?
To address this pressing need, the School of Graduate Studies at Lingnan University has launched the Master of Social Sciences in Health and Social Services Management (MHSM).

It is dedicated to developing students' strategic thinking, resource integration capabilities, and cross-sectoral leadership, preparing graduates for roles in service design, operational management, and policy advocacy across multiple domains.
Theorising Health and Social Services I This course will explore major theoretical debates in health and social services.

Managing Service Users and Stakeholders In this course, theoretical, philosophical and practical discussions will be explored about ways of incorporating experiences, ideas, and expectations of potential service users in the design and delivery of health and social services.
Students will learn about approaches to establish and maintain a strong and valuable working relationship with service users and other stakeholders.

They will also be led to examine the ethical boundaries in the relationship with different stakeholders and identify factors that enhance or inhibit the involvement of service users in service delivery.

--- Source: https://www.ln.edu.hk/sgs/mhsm/programme/programme-structure Home School of Graduate Studies Master of Social Sciences in Health and Social Services Management Programme Structure Explore More Programme Overview Unique Feature Programme Structure Course Description Programme Information Programme Structure Students will be expected to complete 30 Credits comprising six courses (18 Credits), two elective courses (6 Credits) and one project (6 Credits) .
learningObjectives: The Programme is a non-credit bearing programme. Students are welcome to apply in the specified periods, but admission is subject to a competitive selection process due to limited spaces.
Mr. QU Fengyang (MHSM) served at Jockey Club CADENZA e-Tools for Elder Care (cadenza.hk/e-tools), CUHK Jockey Club Institute of Ageing:

“This program really opened my eyes to the real-life situations and specific needs of the elderly in Hong Kong. It also gave me a clearer picture of the current social service system here. Because of this, I feel even more determined to work in social services in Hong Kong and contribute to addressing the challenges of an aging population.”
Mr. GYAMFI CHARLES KWAME RAHMAN (SAG) served at Jockey Club Gerontechnology and Smart Ageing in Place Project:

“In this service practicum, I supported educating people about smart aging, introducing young learners to ways they can manage issues that negatively affect aging. I believe this aligns well with our coursework, as it allows us to practice what we learn in class. It has made me realize that there is much to be done to assist both the elderly and the young, which can help shape the lives of young people.”
Mr. HUANG Baoqing (HAM) served at Jockey Club CADENZA e-Tools for Elder Care (cadenza.hk/e-tools), CUHK Jockey Club Institute of Ageing:

"The Programme has significantly contributed to my personal growth and development in several ways. It enhanced my empathy and communication skills by allowing me to connect deeply with the elderly community, understanding their challenges and needs on a personal level. This taught me the importance of active listening and adapting my approach to effectively engage with diverse groups of people.”

skillsDeveloped: Sector development requires not only greater resource investment, but more critically, a cohort of professional leaders capable of holistic planning, innovative service design, and efficient management.

Through a scientific and systematic approach to service management, it aims to strengthen the development of health and social services, and contribute to a more integrated ecosystem for population health and social care.

It is dedicated to developing students' strategic thinking, resource integration capabilities, and cross-sectoral leadership, preparing graduates for roles in service design, operational management, and policy advocacy across multiple domains.
Health and Social Services Management Skills in managing people and organisation have become invaluable for health care and social service providers.
This course aims to enhance students’ leadership and managerial skills in health and social service delivery.

It entails advanced discussions on theoretical frameworks, concepts, and approaches concerning health and social services management in operational level, including utilisation of human resources, materials, equipment and technology etc.
Also, students will be introduced to skills in the academic research proposal and academic writing.
The first part will provide students with knowledge and insights for addressing the gaps in society and technology interface, particularly for older persons.

Students will be introduced to existing and emerging assistive technologies and equipment for delivering care to older persons and other vulnerable populations groups such as telemonitoring, telehealth, assistive, ICT technologies and other gerontechnological devices.

This part of the course will further expose students to relevant topics such as user experience design, home automation and machine ethics to equip them with the skills and knowledge necessary for developing effective healthcare services and products.
careerDirections: Sector development requires not only greater resource investment, but more critically, a cohort of professional leaders capable of holistic planning, innovative service design, and efficient management.
The programme is designed to cultivate professional managerial talent for the social welfare and healthcare sectors.
This course aims to enhance students’ leadership and managerial skills in health and social service delivery.

Students will also be exposed to cutting-edge technologies, such as artificial intelligence (AI) and robotics, and examine potential benefits and risks associated with the health and social services profession.
The seminars will be delivered by professionals in relevant fields to share practical experiences.
The course integrates theory, policy, and practical casework to prepare students for roles as effective practitioners and managers in health and social service agencies.
Classes will feature on-hands exercises and workshops, so that students learn things that they can quickly implement into their (future) career.

Participants will collaborate with NGOs and relevant healthcare organizations in Hong Kong, enhancing their understanding of the local health and social services sector and aiding in their career planning.

Leveraging the practical resources of LU Jockey Club Gerontech-X Lab, the course emphasises developing students ’ ability to assess technological feasibility, plan implementation pathways, and manage ethical risks ， preparing them to become modern managers capable of using technology to enhance service efficiency and quality.

Industry Connection through Real-World Cases and Expert Insights The programme emphasises staying closely aligned with industry developments by incorporating real institutional operation cases and policy practices from the Greater Bay Area and international contexts.

SGS MHSM - Master of Social Sciences in Health and Social Services Management (MHSM)

Welcome to MHSM Programme

Why choose the MHSM Programme?

Global population ageing continues to intensify alongside a widespread rise in public health awareness, driving a sharp increase in society's demand for diversified health and social services. Sector development requires not only greater resource investment, but more critically, a cohort of professional leaders capable of holistic planning, innovative service design, and efficient management.



To address this pressing need, the School of Graduate Studies at Lingnan University has launched the Master of Social Sciences in Health and Social Services Management (MHSM). The programme is designed to cultivate professional managerial talent for the social welfare and healthcare sectors. Through a scientific and systematic approach to service management, it aims to strengthen the development of health and social services, and contribute to a more integrated ecosystem for population health and social care.



Built upon a core framework of modern management science, the programme deeply integrating the cutting-edge technological applications and humanistic insight essential for service innovation. It is dedicated to developing students' strategic thinking, resource integration capabilities, and cross-sectoral leadership, preparing graduates for roles in service design, operational management, and policy advocacy across multiple domains. These include social services for older adults, child and youth wellbeing, and community health promotion.

Course Description

Course Description
Programme Information

Course Description

Core Courses (6 courses, 3 Credits each)
1. Theorising Health and Social Services I
This course will explore major theoretical debates in health and social services. It will also enhance understanding of social aspects and ethical dilemmas in health service provision and their role in the process. The course will be delivered through lectures.

2. Health and Social Services Management
Skills in managing people and organisation have become invaluable for health care and social service providers. This course aims to enhance students’ leadership and managerial skills in health and social service delivery. It entails advanced discussions on theoretical frameworks, concepts, and approaches concerning health and social services management in operational level, including utilisation of human resources, materials, equipment and technology etc. The theoretical discussions will be delved into organisational culture and dynamics, fundraising, risk management and ethics etc. It will be supplemented with relevant case studies inspired by real situations of managing health-related social service delivery and the health sector in general, which is for the kinds of experiences they are likely to encounter at their workplaces. The course will be delivered through lectures, guest talks and fieldtrips.

3. Research in Health and Social Services
The module will focus on qualitative and quantitative approaches to researching health and social care management. It will involve practical approaches to gathering and analyzing data for health and social services. Students will be taken through research design, data collection, data management and data analysis of qualitative and quantitative research methods. They will be introduced to essential software for analyzing data including NVivo and SPSS. Also, students will be introduced to skills in the academic research proposal and academic writing. In collaboration with the Fong Sum Wood Library (Lingnan University), students will also be taken through methods in the literature review and managing literature in health and social care.

4. Modern Technology in Health and Social Services
This technologically-driven course is designed for senior health and social service practitioners, who have responsibilities and resources for innovating existing service and products to achieve higher organisational efficiency and user satisfaction.
The course is in two parts. The first part will provide students with knowledge and insights for addressing the gaps in society and technology interface, particularly for older persons. Students will be introduced to existing and emerging assistive technologies and equipment for delivering care to older persons and other vulnerable populations groups such as telemonitoring, telehealth, assistive, ICT technologies and other gerontechnological devices. Students will also be exposed to cutting-edge technologies, such as artificial intelligence (AI) and robotics, and examine potential benefits and risks associated with the health and social services profession.
In the second part of the course, students will learn to lead an effective innovation process using human-centred design method and gain hands-on experience in assistive technology and automation design. This part of the course will further expose students to relevant topics such as user experience design, home automation and machine ethics to equip them with the skills and knowledge necessary for developing effective healthcare services and products. They will have real-time experience in harvesting and applying big data in decisions about care delivery relating to the maintenance or improvement of physical and mental health through diagnosis, treatment and disease prevention. Thus, this course utilises problem-based learning approach. Students will gain ample hands-on design experience with technology.

5. Theorising Health and Social Services II
This course will be a continuation of the debates on health issues in social service delivery. However, this module focusses on practical and empirical aspect. Instead of lectures, this one will involve a series of practitioner seminars. The seminars will be delivered by professionals in relevant fields to share practical experiences. In particular, there will be a component of Chinese medicine practice and the role of social service agents and agencies. Each of the topics will relate to some of the theoretical issues raised during the first part of the course.

6. Chinese Medicine in Health and Social Services
Chinese Medicine (CM) has been found to be a cost-effective method in disease prevention and treatment. Hence, more and more health and social care workers are introducing CM in their practice and public engagement activities. Thus, some fundamental knowledge of CM is useful and important in the proper planning and management of health-related social services. The course is divided into two parts. The first part provides students general understanding of the basic theories, working principles, methods for health promotion and disease healing in Chinese medicine. This comprises an introduction to the five key areas in Chinese medicine, including the basic theories of Chinese medicine, methods of diagnosis and treatment, acupuncture & moxibustion, Chinese medicinal materials, and Chinese herbal formulary.

In the second part of the course, students will learn and experience how CM is being delivered in a real-life context. They will be arranged 8 hours of attachment at a Chinese Medicine Clinic or a CM service provider. The course written assignment and group presentation will be linked with this work experience and theories and debates from lectures and seminars.

Elective Courses (3 credits each, select any two courses)
1. Managing Service Users and Stakeholders
In this course, theoretical, philosophical and practical discussions will be explored about ways of incorporating experiences, ideas, and expectations of potential service users in the design and delivery of health and social services. Students will learn about approaches to establish and maintain a strong and valuable working relationship with service users and other stakeholders. They will also be led to examine the ethical boundaries in the relationship with different stakeholders and identify factors that enhance or inhibit the involvement of service users in service delivery.

2. Social Policy in Modern Societies
This course introduces students to social policy concepts and theories, with attention to the comparison of the social, economic, demographic changes taking place in the Greater China and East Asian regions. More specifically, it examines significant social policy challenges confronting societies in Greater China and Asia, examining how governments, markets and civil societies in the region respond to such challenges in adopting social policy policies appropriate for managing rapid changes.

3. Health Policy: Theoretical and Practical Approaches
This course delves into concepts, frameworks, and practice of health policy, with a focus on policy development, analysis, implementation, and evaluation. Emphasis is placed on understanding the interplay between health systems, governance, economics, ethics, and the social determinants of health. The course draws on case studies from Hong Kong’s unique context, regional developments in Asia, and global health policy challenges such as pandemics, health equity, and universal health coverage to situate the discourse on health policy in a social service context. Students will develop analytical and practical skills to critically assess and contribute to health policy in diverse settings.

4. Health and Well-being of Children and Youth
This course provides an in-depth overview of the health and well-being of children and youth, particularly those facing difficult life circumstances such as trauma, neglect, abuse, disability, and systemic disadvantages. Students will study evidence-based frameworks, interventions and strategies aimed at improving outcomes by addressing physical, mental, emotional, and social dimensions of child welfare. The course integrates theory, policy, and practical casework to prepare students for roles as effective practitioners and managers in health and social service agencies.

5. NGO and Social Services: Policy and Management Perspective
This course provides the essential function of non-governmental organizations (NGOs) and social service agencies in the realms of healthcare delivery, policy advocacy, and community health management. Emphasizing both global and regional perspectives, particularly in Asia, the course will offer how NGOs enhance public health systems, tackle health emergencies, and address inequalities in healthcare access. Students will learn role of policy framework and regulatory, funding mechanisms, and management strategies that influence NGO activities in healthcare from practitioners, and case studies related to NGOs response in pandemic such as COVID-19 and natural disaster.

6. Resources Management and Fundraising in Public and Private Sectors

This course provides students with resource management and development, basic fundraising theories, and techniques, as well as building the branding of the service with special emphasis on the social service sector. It will also cover the arguments between the Control on Fundraising and self-regulation by the sector. It will touch on various concerns regarding Fundraising, namely the rational fundraising cost, risk management, and proposal writing for funding support to NGOs.

This course covers topics such as the concepts of philanthropy, the understanding of giving behavior (including annual giving and capital giving), the cost-effectiveness of various fund-raising activities, the use of volunteers, building prospect relationships, stewardship (accountability and reporting), ethical issues of fundraising, making a business case for new initiatives, obtaining grant and project support, etc.

7. Innovation Management
This course teaches students to review innovative methods and processes and apply them to address challenges related to innovation management within the business, public, and private sector. These innovative methods and processes include organisational structure, meeting procedures, idea generation practices, etc., with students gaining knowledge, skills and attitudes related to these innovative methods and processes and applying them in the context of case studies and practical exercises. The course also teaches students to promote and implement innovation methods within organisations through effective communication.

Students will learn how to apply what they learn in the context of concrete, real-world challenges by designing and writing a grant proposal in groups for a project related to innovation management, as well as critically review specific innovation management strategies in relation to an individual research assignment. Lectures on innovation management will include an introduction to various innovation theories, innovation project management, and methodologies and tools for innovation. Classes will feature on-hands exercises and workshops, so that students learn things that they can quickly implement into their (future) career.

Capstone Project (6 credits)

1. Health and Social Services Project

This course aims to facilitate students to integrate and apply the knowledge and skills acquired through different courses of the program. They are expected to complete a dissertation in the form of either a ‘critical literature review’, an ‘empirical research study’, or a ‘work-based project’. After several introductory sessions on the requirements of the project and methods for studying a chosen issue, students need to work out a project proposal and present it in class. This is an independent study, and students are expected to seek advice from their supervisor to produce a final project.

Entry Requirements

For a Master’s Degree, an applicant shall normally:
hold a relevant bachelor’s degree awarded by a tertiary education institution recognised for this purpose by the University; or
have obtained an equivalent qualification; or
have provided satisfactory evidence of academic and professional attainment.

English Language Requirements
In addition to the above, an applicant whose degree, or exceptionally, an alternative qualification deemed equivalent, was not awarded by a tertiary institution where English was used as the medium of instruction (MOI) for the programme should obtain
• a minimum score of 79 in TOEFL (Internet-Based Test) 1 ; or
• a band score of 6.0 or above in IELTS 1 ; or
• a minimum score of 450 or above in the College English Test Band 6 (CET-6), with proof of competency in spoken English 2 ; or
• an equivalent score in a recognised test; or
• an equivalent qualification to prove his/her English language proficiency, which will be considered on a case-by-case basis.

1 For IELTS and TOEFL qualifications for admission purposes, the University only accepts results from a single test sitting. The test must be conducted at a recognised test centre and fall within the two-year validity period, counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort.
2 For CET-6 results submitted for admission purposes, the University accepts only those obtained within the five-year validity period, counted from the date of submission of the admission application for the specific intake cohort. Applicants must also demonstrate competency in spoken English through an oral interview conducted by the Programme and/or obtain grade B or above in the CET-6 Speaking English Test.
*Interviews may be conducted if deemed necessary by the Admissions Coordinator and the Programme Director.

Career Prospects

Career Prospects

Service Practicum Programme 2025-2026

The Service Practicum Programme (the Programme) invites students from the following Master’s Programmes:



o Master of Social Sciences in Health and Social Services Management

o Master of Science in Health Analytics and Management

o Master of Science in Smart Ageing and Gerontology



The objective of the program is to offer hands-on experience to LU master’s students interested in social service careers. Participants will collaborate with NGOs and relevant healthcare organizations in Hong Kong, enhancing their understanding of the local health and social services sector and aiding in their career planning.

The Programme is a non-credit bearing programme. Students are welcome to apply in the specified periods, but admission is subject to a competitive selection process due to limited spaces.

Mr. QU Fengyang (MHSM) served at Jockey Club CADENZA e-Tools for Elder Care (cadenza.hk/e-tools), CUHK Jockey Club Institute of Ageing:

“This program really opened my eyes to the real-life situations and specific needs of the elderly in Hong Kong. It also gave me a clearer picture of the current social service system here. Because of this, I feel even more determined to work in social services in Hong Kong and contribute to addressing the challenges of an aging population.”

Mr. GYAMFI CHARLES KWAME RAHMAN (SAG) served at Jockey Club Gerontechnology and Smart Ageing in Place Project:

“In this service practicum, I supported educating people about smart aging, introducing young learners to ways they can manage issues that negatively affect aging. I believe this aligns well with our coursework, as it allows us to practice what we learn in class. It has made me realize that there is much to be done to assist both the elderly and the young, which can help shape the lives of young people.”

Mr. HUANG Baoqing (HAM) served at Jockey Club CADENZA e-Tools for Elder Care (cadenza.hk/e-tools), CUHK Jockey Club Institute of Ageing:

"The Programme has significantly contributed to my personal growth and development in several ways. It enhanced my empathy and communication skills by allowing me to connect deeply with the elderly community, understanding their challenges and needs on a personal level. This taught me the importance of active listening and adapting my approach to effectively engage with diverse groups of people.”

Application Procedure

Submission of Application
Applicants may submit applications for admission through the University's Online Admission System at https://apply.ln.edu.hk/ . Upon successful submission of the application, applicants will receive an application acknowledgement by email.
Please refer to the Programme List for information about the application period of individual taught postgraduate programmes.

Supporting Documents
Applicants should scan and submit the following documents to the Online Application System for evaluation of their applications:
Identity documents (e.g. Identity card*, passport or other travel document)

Official Transcript (for academic qualifications of undergraduate and/or postgraduate studies):

An official final transcript for a completed programme

The latest transcript if the programme is not yet completed

An official English-translated copy must be attached if the transcript is not in English. The interpretation of grades (in English) is also required.

Degree Certificate/Graduation Certificate/Proof of Study (as appropriate)

Verification proof by third-party authorities (for a completed programme):

Online Verification Report of Higher Education Degree Certificate (中國高等教育學位在綫驗証報告), both Chinese and English versions, issued by the CHSI (中國高等教育學生信息網) for qualification(s) awarded by universities in Mainland China

Online Query of Overseas Credentials Evaluation Reports國(境)外學歷學位認証書 issued by the Chinese Service Center for Scholarly Exchange (CSCSE) (教育部留學服務中心) (applicable to Mainland applicants with qualifications awarded by overseas universities)

Proof of English language proficiency, e.g. IETLS, TOEFL Score Report (applicable to applicants whose degree or, exceptionally, an alternative qualification deemed equivalent, is not from a tertiary institution where English is used as the medium of instruction (MOI) for the degree programme)

Personal statement

Reference letters (if any)

*Do not upload copy of HKID. Local students will be required to present HKID for verification upon registration.

Graduation Requirement

To qualify for the award of the Master of Social Sciences in Health and Social Services Management offered by Lingnan University (LU), students must, where applicable:
Complete 9 courses (6 courses, 2 elective courses and 1 capstone project)# and obtain a minimum of 30 credits;
Achieve a minimum CGPA of 2.67; and
Fulfil the University exit requirement on English language competency, if applicable; and fulfil all University/programme requirements.
A student who fulfils all the requirements for graduation shall be awarded a Master of Social Sciences in Health and Social Services Management degree with one of the following classifications:

Degree Classification

Minimum Cumulative GPA

Distinction

3.50

Pass

2.67

Students will be awarded a Postgraduate Diploma in Health and Social Services Management if they complete not less than five courses with a minimum GPA of 2.33, and do not continue in the programme. Acceptance of an intermediate award implies an intention not to complete the required assessments or courses for the final award.

# Subject to Senates's Approval

Programme Information

Unique Features

Unique Features

Multidisciplinary and Application-Oriented Approach

The programme transcends traditional disciplinary boundaries through a problem-driven approach, integrating theories and methodologies from public administration, social policy, information technology, and health sciences. Students will develop a systematic analytical framework, to address complex challenges in health and social services from multiple perspectives.

Frontier Technology Application and Management

A dedicated core course, “Modern Technology in Health and Social Services”, focuses on gerontechnology, artificial intelligence and related innovations. Leveraging the practical resources of LU Jockey Club Gerontech-X Lab, the course emphasises developing students ’ ability to assess technological feasibility, plan implementation pathways, and manage ethical risks ， preparing them to become modern managers capable of using technology to enhance service efficiency and quality.

Systematic Integration and Practice of Traditional Chinese Medicine Wisdom

This programme innovatively incorporates Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) into health and social services management. Students explore how TCM wisdom can be integrated into service systems such as health promotion, rehabilitation care, and chronic disease management. Short-term placement opportunities at Lingnan University’s Chinese Medicine Clinic further strengthen the connection between theory and practice.

Whole-Lifecycle and Multi-Group Service Management Perspective

The curriculum is designed to cover a wide range of beneficiary groups. Beyond the core issues of ageing society, elective courses such as "Health and Well-being of Children and Youth" enable students to understand service needs and management characteristics across different age groups and diverse communities.

Industry Connection through Real-World Cases and Expert Insights

The programme emphasises staying closely aligned with industry developments by incorporating real institutional operation cases and policy practices from the Greater Bay Area and international contexts. Through industry forums and executive workshops, seasoned practitioners and decision-makers share cutting-edge insights, helping students expand their professional networks.

Study Tours to Broaden Global Perspectives

Short-term overseas study tours to leading regions in health and social service innovation, such as the Greater Bay Area, Taiwan, and Singapore, allow students to experience service models and management practices through institutional visits, expert seminars, and cultural immersion, broadening their comparative international outlook.

Deep Integration of Theory and Practice

The programme has established an internship partnership network with high-quality social service organisations, healthcare institutions, and elderly care providers across Hong Kong and the Greater Bay Area. Students may undertake short-term internships in areas such as elderly care, child welfare, or community health according to their own interests, gaining valuable professional experience.

programmeName: Doctor of Smart Manufacturing
school: Wu Jieh Yee School of Interdisciplinary Studies
summary: To directly address the urgent talent needs and strategic priorities outlined, namely, the convergence of technologies in Industry 4.0 and the specific developmental goals of the GBA and Hong Kong, the Wu Jieh Yee School of Interdisciplinary Studies (WJYSIS) launched a new taught doctoral programme, the Doctor of Smart Manufacturing (DSM).
suitableBackgrounds: Nowadays, most taught postgraduate programmes related to smart manufacturing in Hong Kong primarily exist at the master’s level, such as the Master of Science in Advanced Manufacturing or Master of Science in Robotics.
Furthermore, the DSM programme adopts a blended teaching method that accommodates both fresh graduates and industry professionals, while priority will be given to those with experience.
The course prepares graduates to lead in the development and implementation of digitally enhanced sustainable industrial systems.

It requires students to conceive, design, and execute a substantial body of original research that makes a significant and original contribution to the forefront of smart manufacturing and its interrelated disciplines.

The thesis (40,000-60,000 words) must demonstrate scholarly rigour, interdisciplinary integration, methodological innovation, and a high degree of autonomy, ultimately advancing both academic knowledge and industrial or societal practice.
Applicants must also demonstrate competency in spoken English through an oral interview conducted by the Programme and/or obtain grade B or above in the CET-6 Speaking English Test.

For Part-time Study Applicants applying for the part-time mode must: Hold a valid visa or entry permit that authories study in Hong Kong; and Ensure their visa status permits part-time study for the entire duration of the programme.

Confidential Recommendation An applicant should invite his/her referee(s) to complete the Confidential Recommendation form and return the form in sealed envelopes directly to the DSM Programme Office stated below by post as soon as possible after you submit your online application (no later than the official application deadline of the programme).
learningObjectives: Admission: [email protected]
UG06, Lau Chung Him Building,
Lingnan University, Tun Mun,
New Territories, Hong Kong
Source: https://www.ln.edu.hk/wjysis/dsm/about-us/programme-and-curriculum-committee
Wu Jieh Yee School of Interdisciplinary Studies
Doctor of Smart Manufacturing (DSM)
Programme and Curriculum Committee

By equipping students with both technical mastery and translational skills, the programme prepares them to lead in the rapidly evolving landscape of advanced manufacturing and contribute meaningfully to their organisations’ adaptive and competitive goals.

Through its focus on real-world applicability, ethical leadership, and cross-sectoral relevance, the DSM programme aims to support the technological upgrading and green transition of industries within Hong Kong, the Greater Bay Area, and beyond.

Source: https://www.ln.edu.hk/wjysis/dsm
Doctor of Smart Manufacturing

Doctor of Smart Manufacturing

Programme Highlights

To directly address the urgent talent needs and strategic priorities outlined, namely, the convergence of technologies in Industry 4.0 and the specific developmental goals of the GBA and Hong Kong, the Wu Jieh Yee School of Interdisciplinary Studies (WJYSIS) launched a new taught doctoral programme, the Doctor of Smart Manufacturing (DSM).

Nowadays, most taught postgraduate programmes related to smart manufacturing in Hong Kong primarily exist at the master’s level, such as the Master of Science in Advanced Manufacturing or Master of Science in Robotics. As the first specialized taught doctoral programme in Hong Kong dedicated to the integration of smart technologies, materials, manufacturing engineering and sustainable production, the DSM stands out for its interdisciplinary depth and industry-aligned vision. This unique programme is designed to cultivate strategic technology leaders who possess a global outlook, systems thinking, and the capacity to drive innovation-led industrial transformation.

The curriculum is meticulously structured to explore forefront areas such as additive manufacturing, robotics and automation, and advanced materials, while strengthening participants’ abilities to formulate and execute solutions for strategically complex challenges. In addition, DSM emphasizes sustainable industry and development, offering relevant elective courses such as carbon neutrality and environmental governance, sustainable industry and green energy. By equipping students with both technical mastery and translational skills, the programme prepares them to lead in the rapidly evolving landscape of advanced manufacturing and contribute meaningfully to their organisations’ adaptive and competitive goals.

Furthermore, the DSM programme adopts a blended teaching method that accommodates both fresh graduates and industry professionals, while priority will be given to those with experience. This approach not only widens access to high-level education but also fosters a collaborative and dynamic learning environment. Emerging talents gain foundational and advanced expertise in smart manufacturing, while experienced professionals enhance their capability to implement cutting-edge solutions and lead sustainable innovation within their sectors. Through its focus on real-world applicability, ethical leadership, and cross-sectoral relevance, the DSM programme aims to support the technological upgrading and green transition of industries within Hong Kong, the Greater Bay Area, and beyond.

Programme Structure

The DSM programme is designed as a research-oriented taught doctoral degree that integrates structured coursework with substantial original research. Students are required to complete 8 core courses and 2 elective courses along with an independent doctoral thesis to develop interdisciplinary expertise in smart manufacturing. The overall structure of the DSM programme is summarised in the table below. A total of 13 courses are available, comprising eight core courses, four elective options, and one thesis.

In total, students are required to complete 10 courses, amounting to 30 credits. Each student is also required to complete a doctoral thesis (40,000 - 60,000 words) and successfully pass an oral defence to obtain 30 credits.

Each taught course will consist of 42 contact hours to be delivered either three hours per week for 14 weeks or in an intensive mode to fit with the students’ busy schedules and the course instructors’ availability.
